###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BJk75.32$fg3.16368@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <395D14B6.29D7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com> <395D1593.E9803C68@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:395D1593.E9803C68@cusys.edu...
>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > I have a mainframe cobol program writing an output file that is over
8Gb
> > > in size.
> > > I want to split the output and produce say 4 files.  Can anyone think
of
> > > a way to write say 10000 records to one file then the next 10000 to
the
> > > second file and so on until finished?
> > > Or should I sort the output in a second step to create the four files?
> > > Any help would be appreciated
> >
> > If you do it in a second step, at least you'll know how much data you
are dealing with
> > because you can count it on the first step.
>
> So what do you do, write out a record with the record count and read it
> in with a second cobol program?

No need to use a second program. Just write all the output to a file, CLOSE
the file, then OPEN it again, this time for INPUT. You have your record
count, so you know how to to "fairly" divide up the output across the
multiple output files.

You can do the same thing if the "huge" file is the original input: Just
OPEN INPUT the file, read until end, counting; then CLOSE, OPEN INPUT again
and you are back at record one.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** hobbsb@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-07-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jl57e$85e$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <395D14B6.29D7@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```

> > I have a mainframe cobol program writing an output file that
> > is over 8Gb in size.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Maybe one can use a little finesse on this problem ...

Can the program that generates the 8 GB file determine how many records
*will* be generated?  If so, let it calculate how many records to write
to each file.  One can avoid creating a huge intermediate file or a last
output file with only a few records.  One could make this program work
such that it doesn't matter if the output files total 8 GB or 8 TB,
it'll create the appropriate number of properly sized files.

But ... why does the file have to broken up in the first place?  If the
next processing step aborts on files over a certain size or number of
records, I would suggest "fixing" that step.  One may not always
remember that that step will only process up to X GB files or Y number
of records - with the corresponding loss of time and scrambling around
trying to split the file.



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
I think you are all making this way too complicated.

Assign one file for input.
Assign a second for output, in the JCL assign it to a TAPE drive.

Read and write to the desired record count, then close the tape file
and open it again for output.  Make it non cataloged, non labeled if
necessary.  I've never had to do this, but I don't see on the face of
it why it won't work.  Never tried it though....
```

- **From:** William Lynch <wblynch@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
(snip)

> I think you are all making this way too complicated.
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> it why it won't work.  Never tried it though....

If you're using tape, why not just write the file out as one entity
(tape, of course, assumes a flat file)? At the end of that step you know
all there is to know about the file you just created and you can split
it off into as many sub files as you want. 

What is it you're trying to accomplish, anyway?

Bill Lynch
```

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
I'd have to do a little digging but I know that I have some code from this 
newgroup that allows switching DDnames attached to an FD.  It at least 
gets around the multiple SELECT/ASSIGN situation for creating a sequential
set of files from a program.

If there is no rule for record sequence or file content in the smaller files,
I would prefer to pass the entire file thru a sort with the SPLIT parm and a
list of 4-10 ddnames to separate the file out into smaller pieces.

If the first file MUST contain the first n records while the second file MUST 
contain the second n records, then I suppose the DDname switching DLL
is the route that I would take.

If anyone is interested, I can check on where I have that DDname switching
source.
```

```
Fred Young wrote:

> 
> If I remember my 1401 days, you could put half the file on tape, and the other
> half on cards.  That should overcome the 8g limit.  And boy, that 1401 card
> reader was sure fast.

It was a thing of joy to watch.  When doing a binary load (i.e. not slowed down 
by processing) it was amazing to see those cards flying through the air and landing 
exactly where they were supposed to.  I moved to a Honeywell installation, and WHAT a 
contrast.  Frequently the thing threw the cards all over the floor.  Anybody who did not 
have a sequence key in their deck was in BIG trouble.
```

#### ↳ Re: Splitting output file

- **From:** petert@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-07-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jp0lc$o22$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Thanks everybody for your input.....
Phew.....where to start....
The order IS important.... its the output of a fastscan of an IMS DB.
The order of course is parent, child....child, parent, parent, child,
child etc.
File must be split before new parent.
The reason for splitting is that the target is Oracle/Unix but the only
way to move it is via a PC (different networks, security, firewalls, you
dont wanna know....).  If I FTP to the PC in one go, I get hard disk
errors >2Gb...
We can split it via a mainframe sort, rejoin it back via Unix, then
logically split again for loading...
So, back to the problem....
the responder who suggested multiple select/assigns has pointed me the
way I think, also performing a paragraph for all the writes and deciding
the file in that.
That was the sort of detail/idea I was looking for.
Thanks
Peter

In article <8jhl55$q24$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  petert@my-deja.com wrote:
> Hi all
> I have a mainframe cobol program writing an output file that is over
8Gb
> in size.
> I want to split the output and produce say 4 files.  Can anyone think
of
> a way to write say 10000 records to one file then the next 10000 to
the
> second file and so on until finished?
> Or should I sort the output in a second step to create the four files?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Are there job opportunities in the Northwest for COBOL programmers?

- **From:** "mongoose" <srahi@together.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00

```
From actual experience, It is very hard to get entry level cobol jobs,
especially if you learn it thru a trade school. Out my class of 20 people
learning cobol in a trade school, only 5-6 got jobs in cobol programming.
Some still have'nt found jobs after a year. I got a job thru a friend who
knew the manager.
This is only valid in the New York,New Jersey,Philidelphia area.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Where can I get a good Manual/Book on MS COBOL 2.1 please ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k3f0m$vqa$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8jia4m$iab$1@news.vsnl.net.in> <Iq995.7324$c5.19978@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Just "FYI" - as far as their Language Reference manual goes, look toward the
front for a section called "Acknowledgement" - which talks about the COBOL
Standard and credits some of the original contributors to that design.

You will also see that it give EXPLICIT prior permission for you to copy that
manual in part of whole.  This may not apply to the other manuals, but almost
ALL COBOL LRM's may legally be copied - even though they are copyrighted.

If that manual doesn't include the "Acknowledgement," then it has its own
problems (probably).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ 200x Implementations (was: What is Fujitsu?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jtjrk$70$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<395AC8D2.56A34FEB@mtsac.edu> <8jfun7$g9j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <U5T65.74$gB5.63880@nnrp2.sbc.net> <8jhnrh$ng8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <395ccda3.53651730@207.126.101.100> <8jir00$7hh$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <395d1322.71445799@207.126.101.100>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
>
> Tell me about the Conditional compilation in current NetExpress.

Thane,
  I have kept this in my "to do" list and am finally getting around to it.
What you want to look at (in Micro Focus' implementation - that has been
there for SEVERAL years) is

 - $IF "directive"
 - $IFDEF "directive"
- $ELSE and $ENDIF (no hyphen as I recall) constructs
- $CONSTANT directive

I have been told by one of my "usually reliable sources" that >>IF and
>>EVALUATE may be accepted when the ISO2000 directive is on, but they don't
think they are actually processed (yet).

Two *major* diferences between the MF (and this does date back to Micro
Focus, not just MERANT) implemenations are:

 - You can "interchangeably" use 78-levels and constants defined by $CONSTANT
 - $DISPLAY to display (during compilation) what "path" or value of constants
are in effect for that specific compilation.

In my opinion (but not that of J4) BOTH of those are quite useful
"extensions" to what will be in the Standard.

   ***

Does this give you enough to "get you started"?
```

```
Tim Hillock wrote:

Just recently having got back from the tail-end of J4 at Newbury, I have
very serious doubts anybody is in the driving seat - it's a bit like a
runaway train.

Bill will correct me if I've got it wrong - but I think Vendor Compiler
input is currently limited to IBM (Ann Bennet), Merant(Don Schricker)
and Compaq(Don Nelson), and the three Japanese companies. The
non-players, not exhaustive, Unisys, Acu, Liant(RM/COBOL) etc.... - what
does the 'non-players' tell you about the 'Standards" ?

Plus there's all sorts of nifty features that can be vendor implemented
- translation - they don't include it in their version of COBOL 200X if
they don't like it!!!  QUESTION - who are the bloody standards being
designed for ?

Hows zat grab you ?

Jimmy
```

```
Ooops ! Now I've got to revise my dates.

	Standard 2002 - compiler implementation 2005 - OO maybe 2008
	Standard 2003 - compiler implementation 2006 - OO maybe 2009

Note : the 'OO bit' is introduction of PORTABLE classes from one
compiler to another, i.e. OO libraries as in other OO languages.

Geez - it's getting awfully close to me entering a funny farm before the
new version is operable !

Jimmy
```

```
the additions to the new standard are already supported in NetExpress.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ 200x Standard implementation (was: What is Fujitsu?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jq9l7$dv9$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<395AC8D2.56A34FEB@mtsac.edu> <8jfun7$g9j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <U5T65.74$gB5.63880@nnrp2.sbc.net> <8jhnrh$ng8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <395D2605.B0C3E758@earthlink.net> <8jj9po$e9c$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <395D7128.7EDE2D4B@home.com> <8jpp50$kcp$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Paul,
  The problem is that MERANT (not Merant <G>) took a "snap shot" of OO in the
Standard and implemented it.  So did Fujitsu and so did IBM.  UNFORTUNATELY,
each of them used DIFFERENT snap shots, so they have different (incompatible)
implementations.  MORE IMPORTANTLY, none of them match the current definition
(which may/probably will still change again - before it is approved).

Several MAJOR areas (not OO) that MERANT has not addressed (AFAIK) yet are:
 - Common  Exception Handling
 - Standard Arithmetic
 - Validate
 - Bit/Boolean USAGEs (and operations)
 - New Floating Point USAGEs
 - New Binary USAGEs
 - User Defined functions

      ***

I do believe that MERANT has implemented MORE of the draft Standard than any
other implementor - and that its OO implementation is RELATIVELY close to the
draft (However, I think that Siemen's is closer).  But I don't want ANYONE to
think that any vendor is doing even half of what will be required - once the
draft is approved.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is Fujitsu?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-07-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<395AC8D2.56A34FEB@mtsac.edu> <8jfun7$g9j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <U5T65.74$gB5.63880@nnrp2.sbc.net> <8jhnrh$ng8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <395D2605.B0C3E758@earthlink.net> <8jj9po$e9c$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <395D7128.7EDE2D4B@home.com> <8jpp50$kcp$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
On Mon, 3 Jul 2000 10:01:05 +0100, "Paul Barnett"
<paul.barnett@merant.com> wrote:

>As far as I know Dec 2002 is still the target date. As for OO, it's already
>supported and it was first introduced aometime around 1993 (as far as I
>know). The date that J4 hit is up to them - not Merant. Most, if not all, of
>the additions to the new standard are already supported in NetExpress.
>

Paul - you are just plain wrong.  I don't know if it's because of some
misunderstanding or what.  I'd sit here and bite my tounge, but you
are just being a bit TOO optimistic.  While Merant (and others)
support large parts of the proposed standard, none support "MOST IF
NOT ALL".  I would put Merant on the leading edge -- I'll grant that.
Second place would go to Fujitsu and third to IBM.  

Let me give you just THREE Examples:

Repositiry - It's been in the OO portion of the standard almost from
the beginning.

Interface -- NO ONE Supports it.  Also early in the OO portion.

User Defined Functions - Again NO ONE supports them.

All I'm asking Paul, is that you tone down the vendor rhetoric a bit.
---
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```


Paul Barnett wrote:
> 
> As far as I know Dec 2002 is still the target date. As for OO, it's already
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Paul,

In case it was misconstrued I wasn't implying any onus on MERANT,
(got to watch that guy WMK, he says it should be caps ??). So you're
telling me OO started back in '93 - what excellent progress - '93 to
2002, then follow that up with changes the compiler vendors will have to
make. And as you, Thane and I know, apart from a 'quickie' OO program
that does "Hello World" we can't swap a damn line of code -  total lack
of portability between Fujitsu and NetExpress, or any other, as soon as
you hit vendor provided classes. (And that doesn't include things like
Level 78s and comp-5 which are No-Nos in the current draft of the
Standard).

As it stands Thane and I can swap code, with minor alteration (using N/E
2000 directive), but we would have to write VERY GENERALIZED classes -
no neat features like calling arrays, collections/dictionaries - and
most certainly not GUIs.

Again no reflection on the compiler vendors, but until there is
commonality giving us the ability to swap generic COBOL class libraries
- OO COBOL is just so much hot air. Agreed you can write OO applications
in F/J and N/E - but solely for those compilers.

There's a not very subtle message in the above, and the ground swell
will increase, via the 'Net, as developers latch on and the 'light goes
on' for them individually  :-

(a) COBOL as a COMMON language should provide the features that WE
DEVELOPERS want, plus the Standards must react quickly to new
challenging innovations from other languages.

(b) Not your baby - and the sales people wont like it - a particular
compiler, complying with the very LATEST standards, should stand or fall
on the IDE and other productivity tools it provides for developers.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is COMB SORT ?

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<395D63C4.8505B29E@mediaone.net>`
- **References:** `<3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu> <rAO45.30393$9W1.517190@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8j0drg$ave$1@news.igs.net> <3957577F.B13E7455@cusys.edu> <395776E1.C03FD597@istar.ca> <39587B6A.500D7EDE@zip.com.au> <3958DE4C.1DA5DE36@dced.state.ak.us> <3959F9FA.3003815@cusys.edu> <1ho65.68923$cH1.592344@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <395A843F.4CB0A0FC@cusys.edu> <Pine.GSO.3.96.1000629081457.6496B-100000@eclipse> <395C1C3E.96E0AE01@mediaone.net> <395CA096.BEADF133@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> "Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:
> >
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> I would be interested in learning about this LSD radix sort.

Its workings are kind of counter-intuitive. Intuitively, we understand sorting
things starting with the most significant digit and working things down to the
least significant. LSD-sort, by its own name, is a least-significant-digit sort,
that is, is starts from the least significant digit and works its way from right
to left until the file is in order. It is of paramount importance here that the
method of sorting the keys based on their n-th digit from the right is _stable_,
that is, the relative order of the records remains intact (quicksort, for one,
does _not_ possess this property). Usually, key-indexed sort is used, but there
is also a way of using a linked structure to do this, thereby avoiding moving
records at all. As an illustration, consider this example, where the numbers are
sorted in the stable manner based first on the rightmost (LS) digit, then the
second, and finally, the first (the leftmost column represents an unsorted
original file):

975 142 203 028
142 203 028 142
028 384 142 197
384 975 975 203
197 976 976 384
589 197 384 589
203 028 589 975
976 589 197 976

Note that the sorting time is proportional to the number of digits and keys, that
is, O(N). The only other sort  capable of achieving this is the proxmap (address
computation) sort. Furthermore, it is not necessary to sort based on just _one_
LS digit. Having enough memory, it is easy to use 5-6 rightmost digits at a time
(i.e. 100000 ot 1000000 radix) to do LSD sorting. The file above can be actually
sorted in a single pass by keying its keys into a 1000-element table and
maintaining the counts of each key in the bucket whose index equals the key.


>  But I have
> to say that the number # criterion for coding a sort in a COBOL program
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> at all.

This is very true, all the more that the 'command' is most probably going to work
faster than the self-coded sort, even though i have seen proofs to the contrary.
And it will certainly be more error-prone, or at least guaranteed as such by the
vendor. On the other hand, once a sorting (or any other high-performance routine,
for that matter) has been coded, tested, and tuned in COBOL, there is not reason
to trust it less than one 'guaranteed' by any vendor. COBOL is a general
programming language, and with the latest developments in the compiler
technology, it is pretty quick (about 10% of non-I/O speed of Fortran), so there
is no real reason _not_ to program in it.

> Certainly complex sorting mechanisms have no place being coded
> in a COBOL program.

Why not? See above. I do not believe in the sacred faith in the vendor-guaranteed
'commands' as opposed to ones we code. After all, are we programmers or
proverbial 'users'? An encapsulated routine that is properly coded, tested,
debugged, documented, and black-boxed has no less right to existence than any
other black box, whether it is IBM-certified or not. Actually, IBM-certified is a
blessing. Think of the 'bullet-proof', 'guaranteed-forever' black boxes coming
from the Microsucks factory.

Kind regards,
====================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl
====================
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Complex vs black box solutions

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-07-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39609950.6F426744@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<3951919F.5CC91528@home.com> <fjm45.1789$9W1.111773@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8it6qp$e0c$1@news.igs.net> <8itpo7$j1h$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <39527E43.E92E5618@cusys.edu> <rAO45.30393$9W1.517190@news-west.usenetserver.com> <8j0drg$ave$1@news.igs.net> <3957577F.B13E7455@cusys.edu> <395776E1.C03FD597@istar.ca> <39587B6A.500D7EDE@zip.com.au> <3958DE4C.1DA5DE36@dced.state.ak.us> <3959F9FA.3003815@cusys.edu> <1ho65.68923$cH1.592344@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <395A843F.4CB0A0FC@cusys.edu> <Pine.GSO.3.96.1000629081457.6496B-100000@eclipse> <395C1C3E.96E0AE01@mediaone.net> <395CA096.BEADF133@cusys.edu> <395D63C4.8505B29E@mediaone.net>`

```
"Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:
...

Thanks for the description of the LSD radix sort.

> >  But I have
> > to say that the number # criterion for coding a sort in a COBOL program
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> from the Microsucks factory.

The cost benefits are just not there when we can plug in black box sorts
or code simple, easy to maintain sorts.  Even with cutting and pasting
from a well tested program, once we make the changes to fit it in, we
have to test well.  And when a maintenance programmer adjusts a table
size, it has to be obvious that he is not changing the sort mechanisms.

In general, once a complex algorithm is encoded, it should be hidden (OO
anybody?) as much as possible.  Cobol's big strength is that it is a
maintainable language.  Complex algorithms are vulnerable to maintenance
mistakes no matter what language they are coded in, so use black box
solutions whenever possible if maintenance is your major concern.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol encryption algorithms?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-07-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<95o75.2863$09.27426@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<8j7524$udi$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <osK55.48482$Gh.481120@typhoon.austin.rr.com> <8jhrbq$u70$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <395cd469.55386189@207.126.101.100> <8jjumt$fpu$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <Prl75.44$fg3.22857@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:Prl75.44$fg3.22857@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net...
> Russell Styles <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM> wrote in message
> news:8jjumt$fpu$1@sshuraaa-i-1.production.compuserve.com...
> >     although I am sure that almost anything could be done in
> > cobol, isn't this [cryptographic hash algorithms] the sort of thing that
C
> does better?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

various cryptographic routines requires bit handling. This is
supported in the most modern COBOL compilers and the
next standard (whenever it comes).

But even in absolutely portable COBOL-74 code, bit handling
is easy.

Below is (from http://www.etk.com/download/etkpak/etkpak.htm#BITPK)
code that shows how to do that:


Summary: Provides bit manipulations of up to 32 bits. Can get, set, clear,
test, and pack bits.

How to use: Instead of non-portable redefinitions to access bits packed in
numeric variables, use BITPK's get operation to convert a number into a
string of 32 characters each with values "0" or "1". You can then easily
test or otherwise manipulate these character values. The pack operation
packs the character values back into a number.

Limitations: At most 32 bits can be handled at a time.

To call BITPK, define BITPK-CONTROL as follows:

              01  BITPK-CONTROL.
                  02  BITPK-TASK-ID           PIC X(4).
                  02  BITPK-OPERATION         PIC X(1).
                  02  BITPK-FEEDBACK          PIC X(1).
                  02  BITPK-NUMBER            PIC 9(10) COMP.
                  02  BITPK-BITS.
                      03  BITPK-BIT           PIC X(1)  OCCURS 32 TIMES.

Define CALL-BITPK as follows:
              CALL-BITPK.
                  CALL "BITPK"
                        USING BITPK-CONTROL
              .

BITPK supports the following operations:

G (get)
Get the bits of BITPK-NUMBER interpreted as an unsigned 32-bit number. If
the number is larger than 32 bits a (R)ANGE feedback is returned and the bit
string is all zeroes.

P (pack)
The reverse operation of get. Builds the 32-bit number having the bit string
given in BITPK-BITS.

S (set)
For each bit of the bit string given that is a one, set the corresponding
bit in BITPK-NUMBER. This is the binary OR operation.

A (and)
For each bit of the bit string given that is a one, set the corresponding
bit in BITPK-NUMBER if that bit is also a one, otherwise clear the bit. This
is the binary AND operation.

X (xor)
For each bit of the bit string given that is different from the
corresponding bit in BITPK-NUMBER, set that bit in BITPK-NUMBER, otherwise
clear the bit. This is the binary XOR operation.

C (clear)
For each bit of the bit string given that is a one, clear the corresponding
bit in BITPK-NUMBER.

1 (test for 1)
For each bit of the bit string given that is a one, test if the
corresponding bit in BITPK-NUMBER is a one. Return an (A)LL feedback if all
bits tested were ones, a (N)ONE feedback if none of the bits were ones, or a
(S)OME feedback if only some of them were.

0 (test for 0)
For each bit of the bit string given that is a one, test if the
corresponding bit in BITPK-NUMBER is a zero. Return an (A)LL feedback if all
bits tested were zeroes, a (N)ONE feedback if none of the bits were zeroes,
or a (S)OME feedback if only some of them were.

BITPK returns these feedbacks: a space means there were no errors. An "O"
means that the specified operation was invalid. An "R" means that the number
given was too large. An "A", "N", or "S" means that either All, None, or
Some bits met the condition.


 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID.    BITPK.

 AUTHOR.        LEIF SVALGAARD.
 DATE-WRITTEN.  96/12/06
     -REVISED:  97/01/22.

 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
 SOURCE-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.
 OBJECT-COMPUTER. PORTABLE.

 DATA DIVISION.

 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

 01  BIT-HANDLING.
     02  BIT-VALUE               PIC 9(10)  COMP.
     02  BIT-TOP                 PIC 9(10)  COMP  VALUE ZEROES.
     02  THE-WEIGHT              PIC 9(10)  COMP.
     02  BIT-WEIGHTS.
         03  BIT-WEIGHT          PIC 9(10)  COMP  OCCURS 32 TIMES.

     02  BIT-COUNT               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
     02  BIT-MATCH               PIC S9(3)  COMP.
     02  BIT-NBR                 PIC S9(3)  COMP.

     02  BIT-WANTED              PIC X.
     02  BIT-STRING.
         03  THE-BIT             PIC X      OCCURS 32 TIMES.

 LINKAGE SECTION.

 01  BITPK-CONTROL.
     02  SESSION-TASK-ID         PIC X(4).
     02  BITPK-OPERATION         PIC X(1).
     02  BITPK-FEEDBACK          PIC X(1).

     02  BITPK-PATTERN           PIC 9(10)  COMP.
     02  BITPK-BITS.
         03  BITPK-BIT           PIC X      OCCURS 32 TIMES.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION
     USING BITPK-CONTROL.
 BEGIN-PROGRAM.
     IF BIT-TOP = ZEROES
         PERFORM INITIALIZE-BIT-WEIGHTS
     .
     PERFORM GET-AND-CHECK-THE-BITS
     MOVE SPACE TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "G"
         MOVE BIT-STRING TO BITPK-BITS
     ELSE
     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "S" OR "O"
         PERFORM SET-THE-BITS
     ELSE
     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "C"
         PERFORM CLEAR-THE-BITS
     ELSE
     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "A"
         PERFORM AND-THE-BITS
     ELSE
     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "X"
         PERFORM XOR-THE-BITS
     ELSE
     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "1"
         PERFORM TEST-IF-ONES
     ELSE
     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "0"
         PERFORM TEST-IF-ZEROES
     ELSE
     IF BITPK-OPERATION = "P"
         PERFORM PACK-THE-BITS
     ELSE
         MOVE "OPERR" TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
     .
 RETURN-TO-CALLER.
     EXIT PROGRAM
     .

 INITIALIZE-BIT-WEIGHTS.
     MOVE 1 TO THE-WEIGHT
     PERFORM SET-BIT-WEIGHT
       VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 32 BY -1
         UNTIL BIT-NBR < 1

     MOVE THE-WEIGHT TO BIT-TOP
     .

 SET-BIT-WEIGHT.
     MOVE THE-WEIGHT TO BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR)
     ADD  THE-WEIGHT TO THE-WEIGHT
     .

 GET-AND-CHECK-THE-BITS.
     MOVE BITPK-PATTERN TO BIT-VALUE
     IF BIT-VALUE < BIT-TOP
         MOVE ZEROES TO BIT-STRING
         PERFORM GET-THE-BIT
           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32

         PERFORM CHECK-THE-BIT
           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
     ELSE
         MOVE "RANGE" TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
         MOVE  ZEROES TO BIT-STRING
     .

 GET-THE-BIT.
     IF BIT-VALUE NOT < BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR)
         SUBTRACT BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) FROM BIT-VALUE
         MOVE "1" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
     .

 CHECK-THE-BIT.
     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = ZERO
         NEXT SENTENCE
     ELSE
     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = SPACE
         MOVE ZERO TO BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR)
     ELSE
     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) NOT = "1"
         MOVE "1"  TO BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR)
     .

 SET-THE-BITS.
     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
         PERFORM SET-THE-BIT
           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
     .

 SET-THE-BIT.
     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
         IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "0"
             MOVE "1" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
             ADD BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) TO BITPK-PATTERN
     .

 CLEAR-THE-BITS.
     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
         PERFORM CLEAR-THE-BIT
           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
     .

 CLEAR-THE-BIT.
     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
         IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
             MOVE "0" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
             SUBTRACT BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) FROM BITPK-PATTERN
     .

 AND-THE-BITS.
     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
         PERFORM AND-THE-BIT
           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
     .

 AND-THE-BIT.
     IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
         IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) NOT = "1"
             MOVE "0" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
             SUBTRACT BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) FROM BITPK-PATTERN
     .

 XOR-THE-BITS.
     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
         PERFORM XOR-THE-BIT
           VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
     .

 XOR-THE-BIT.
     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
         IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
             MOVE "0" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
             SUBTRACT BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) FROM BITPK-PATTERN
         ELSE
             NEXT SENTENCE
     ELSE
         IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "0"
             MOVE "1" TO THE-BIT (BIT-NBR)
             ADD      BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR)  TO  BITPK-PATTERN
         ELSE
             NEXT SENTENCE
     .

 TEST-IF-ONES.
     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
         MOVE "1" TO BIT-WANTED
         PERFORM TEST-THE-BITS
     .

 TEST-THE-BITS.
     MOVE ZEROES TO BIT-COUNT, BIT-MATCH
     PERFORM TEST-IF-BIT-IS-WANTED
       VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
         UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32

     IF BIT-COUNT = BIT-MATCH
         MOVE "ALL"  TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
     ELSE
     IF BIT-MATCH = ZEROES
         MOVE "NONE" TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
     ELSE
         MOVE "SOME" TO BITPK-FEEDBACK
     .

 TEST-IF-BIT-IS-WANTED.
     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
         ADD 1 TO BIT-COUNT
         IF THE-BIT (BIT-NBR) = BIT-WANTED
             ADD 1 TO BIT-MATCH
     .

 TEST-IF-ZEROES.
     IF BITPK-FEEDBACK = SPACE
         MOVE "0" TO BIT-WANTED
         PERFORM TEST-THE-BITS
     .

 PACK-THE-BITS.
     MOVE ZEROES TO BITPK-PATTERN
     PERFORM PACK-THE-BIT
       VARYING BIT-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
         UNTIL BIT-NBR > 32
     .

 PACK-THE-BIT.
     IF BITPK-BIT (BIT-NBR) = "1"
         ADD BIT-WEIGHT (BIT-NBR) TO BITPK-PATTERN
     .

```

---

<a id="t-798ea1427a"></a>
## Thread: Vendor's presence in newsgroup — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### Vendor's presence in newsgroup

- **From:** "Costas Giannoulis" <diavasi@otenet.gr>
- **Date:** 2000-07-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jkfcm$3lt$1@newssrv.otenet.gr>`

```
Hi all,

The last few weeks i have noticed an intensive presence of MERANT in this
newgroup. I don't know if this caused by the recent 'runtime fees war' but i
have to say bravo  to MERANT and mr Coleman. It is important for a user -
who has no access to other sources - to take an answear directly from the
Vendor or to hear that his question will be forward to a technical group. I
invite all vendors to do the same.

Regards, Costas
```

```
> >> Yes, I do. I can think of probably half a dozen. Just for
starters:
> >>
> >> 1. The source code is almost assuredly an ASCII text file
(in your
> >> words, "a regular document" it just has a funny extension).
Use Word,
> >> Notepad, Wordpad, etc., click Open, click on the file. You
may have to
> >> change the browse mask to "*.*"
> >> 2. Direct the compiler output listing to a file. That FOR
SURE is an
> >> ASCII text file.
> >> 3. In the unhappy event that the original is not a uniform
text file
> >> (has tabs or other strange characters), the editor has a
provision -
> >> almost always - to produce source that is straight ASCII
(Realia
> >> editor, for example).
> >> 4. Open the editor in a DOS window. Tap [Ctrl]-[PrtScreen].
That puts
> >> the whole thing on the Windows clipboard. From there it may
be pasted
> >> virtually anywhere.
> >> 5. Write a COBOL program to convert the (assumed bizzare)
CBL file to
> >> ASCII text and use that.
> >>
> >> I suspect you did not know you can move something from a DOS
window
> >> (not full screen) to the Windows clipboard. In which case,
your
```

- **From:** "The Holy Kipper" <theholykipper@madasafish.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-06T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<8k2gu9$9h8$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>`

```

Paul Barnett <paul.barnett@merant.com> wrote in message
news:8jqe2s$mmk$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk...
> Visit www.jobserve.com - it's where most jobs get advetised, use COBOL as
a
> search key work and see what happens. You might find vacancies for Junior
> programmers.
>
> I first leaned COBOL on a City & Guilds course but then went on to to a
HND.
>

Another tack is to start sending out spec' letters & CV's to everyone you
know that uses COBOL, (the Software Yearbook is a good place to start as
well as all the major Banks, Insurance Companies etc.).  I did the
Computeach course, sent out about 200 letters got replies from about 10 of
them, and managed to get work with Barclays.

Something to remember (I'm sure Computeach will have stressed this) is to
highlight reasons why they should give you a trainee position rather than
wait for someone with experience e.g. lower costs, enthusiasm, commitment to
learning etc.

Also check out www.stepstone.co.uk and search for Programmers.  Try to avoid
the agencies, look for ones where the companies are advertising themselves.

Good Luck

Kippers
```

<a id="t-a2f192e93c"></a>
## Thread: COBOL/JCL — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL/JCL

- **From:** ddgtaylor@aol.com (DDgtaylor)
- **Date:** 2000-07-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000702152249.01226.00001086@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```
Does anyone know how start JCL from a cobol program.
I am writing a program that creates JCL script, i then want the program to run
the JCL statements.

If poss please reply to derrick_tay@hotmail.com

Many Thanks
Derrick
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL/JCL

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)
- **Date:** 2000-07-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000702173358.06332.00000063@ng-da1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<20000702152249.01226.00001086@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```
>Subject: COBOL/JCL
>From: ddgtaylor@aol.com  (DDgtaylor)
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Very easy...

Just write your JCL statements to a DDNAME that's allocated to an internal
reader and they'll be submitted automatically, e.g.,

//ddname DD SYSOUT=(*,INTRDR)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/JCL

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2000-07-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jq13c$dtk$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<20000702152249.01226.00001086@ng-cd1.aol.com> <20000702173358.06332.00000063@ng-da1.aol.com>`

```
In article <20000702173358.06332.00000063@ng-da1.aol.com>,
  theodp@aol.com (Theo DP) wrote:
> >Subject: COBOL/JCL
> >From: ddgtaylor@aol.com  (DDgtaylor)
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Correct, just a little remark: You might need to set the SYSOUT class
explicitely to A: SYSOUT=(A,INTRDR).
And you must CLOSE the file. And you must add the delimiter // at the
end of the job stream (ISPF does this, too).
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL/JCL

- **From:** joarmc@SWBELL.NET (John McKown)
- **Date:** 2000-07-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn8lve1t.utc.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net>`
- **References:** `<20000702152249.01226.00001086@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```
On 02 Jul 2000 19:22:49 GMT, DDgtaylor <ddgtaylor@aol.com> wrote:
>Does anyone know how start JCL from a cobol program.
>I am writing a program that creates JCL script, i then want the program to run
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Assuming that by JCL you mean IBM's "Job Control Language" and that you're 
speaking on OS/390 (MVS), then the answer is that you cannot really do this
directly. What you can do is create a "normal" file defination in your program
which has 80 byte records defined. You use normal WRITE commands to create the
JCL images to this file. This JCL which runs the step which runs this program
would have a DD statement which is a special form of SYSOUT called an "Internal
Reader". Basically the DD statement would look something like:

//DDNAME DD SYSOUT=(*,INTRDR)

You would need to create a complete JOB with the JOB statement and everything.
After you have CLOSEd the file in your COBOL program, the JOB you just created
would be put on the input queue and run later (maybe immediately, maybe not).
There would not really be any connection between your program and this other
job. They would be independant. Well, the submitted job could be written to
require a file that is created by your program. But if it did, then the second
job would probably get a "dataset in use" message and "hang" until this job
finished.

If what you wanted to do was to create the JCL for another job and have this
program wait for it to finish executing and then continue (possibly reading
file created by the new job), then this is really not possible. There are some
very strange things that you could try, but to be honest, I'm not really up
to explaining how to do it. You program could create a TSO CLIST (or REXX
exec) and then invoke TSO to run it, but I'm really hazy on exactly how to
do this.

I would sincerely suggest that you try to figure out another way to do what
you want (unless the independant JOB idea is OK, that's simple.)

John
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/JCL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jop39$2mo$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20000702152249.01226.00001086@ng-cd1.aol.com> <slrn8lve1t.utc.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net>`

```

   <snip>
> Well, the submitted job could be written to
> require a file that is created by your program. But if it did, then the
second
> job would probably get a "dataset in use" message and "hang" until this job
> finished.
>

I have never used them, but wouldn't "batch pipes" solve this issue?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL/JCL

```
Bill, you're correct, 'Batch Pipes' COULD be the solution here if
it fits.

Sometimes tho', what really is needed is to have a program / job when
it reaches some decision point, Start of another Job to do something
unrelated to the current data.

Here, for the Edification of those who haven't downloaded the IBM MVS/OS-390
manuals (or copies the disks from work!) is the intro from the manual:
========================================================
1.0 Introduction to BatchPipes
BatchPipes/MVS offers a way to connect jobs so that data from one job can
move through processor
storage to another job without going to DASD or tape.  It addresses a
growing problem that faces
installations with batch workloads: insufficient time to complete that work.
Given a batch jobstream with a
data flow of certain characteristics, BatchPipes can dramatically shorten
the elapsed time of the jobstream.
The jobs can run faster and process larger volumes of data in the available
batch window; your processors
are freed to run more work -- perhaps extending the period of time that
interactive applications are available
or supporting your company's work in another time zone.  In short, you might
get more work from your
processors.

BatchPipes, running on MVS/ESA (1):

   Allows two or more jobs that formerly ran serially to run concurrently.

   Reduces the number of physical I/O operations by transferring data
through processor storage rather
    than transferring data to and from DASD or tape

   May reduce tape mounts and use of DASD.


What difference does parallel processing make to my batch workload?  Large
tightly-coupled
processing systems running with MVS can have many jobs in various stages of
processing at one time.
Batch applications have not traditionally taken advantage of this multiple
processing capability. Rather, they
often consist of multiple jobs that run one after another. With BatchPipes,
the processor can run two or
more jobs in a jobstream at one time.  Jobs that once ran sequentially can
now run in parallel because data
records or blocks are available to the next job immediately after they are
written.  That is, the whole
sequential file does not have to be written and then closed before the next
job can access the file.

What difference does keeping data in the processor make to my batch
workload?  When data
moves from one job to another through processor storage, the transfers take
place in microseconds as
compared to the milliseconds required to transfer data to and from tape or
DASD.  Keeping data in processor
storage reduces the number of physical I/O operations, causes less I/O
contention, and frees the device
that holds the intermediate data set.  An additional benefit of transferring
data through processor storage is
a reduction in the mounting and managing of tapes for applications using
BatchPipes.

 (1) MVS/ESA is a trademark of the International Business Machines
Corporation.

Subtopics
1.1 An Example of How BatchPipes Changes Traditional Batch Processing
1.2 Resources Required by Jobs Using BatchPipes
1.3 Choosing Candidates for BatchPipes
1.4 Comparing BatchPipes with Other IBM Solutions for Batch Applications
1.5 Preparing to Use BatchPipes
1.6 Running BatchPipes
1.7 Managing the Workload in the BatchPipes Environment
1.8 Running BatchPipes with Multiple Copies of a Job
1.9 Using a Filter to Make Two Copies of a Data Set
1.10 Using BatchPipeWorks with BatchPipes Jobs
1.11 Customer Application Using BatchPipes
1.12 Appendix: Overview of BatchPipes Functions

********************************************************************
1.1 An Example of How BatchPipes Changes Traditional Batch Processing

Most installations have batch jobstreams that use intermediate data sets
such that output from one process
(a job or step) is written to DASD or tape.  This data is then available to
be read by a second process
immediately after the data set closes or the first process ends.  Figure 1
shows the traditional job-to-job data
flow:


Figure 1. Two Jobs in a Traditional Batch Jobstream

In the figure, Job1 writes data to an I/O device.  When all the records have
been written and the data set is closed, Job2 starts.  Job2 reads the data
from the device.  After Job2 finishes processing all the records, the data
set is no longer required and can be deleted.  In effect, the transfer of
data from one job to the other is at a data set level.

Compare the processing of the two jobs in Figure 1 with Figure 2, which
shows those same two jobs using BatchPipes.  Notice that external storage
devices are absent, replaced by a processor storage buffer holding a small
portion of the intermediate data set.

In Figure 2, Job1, a writer to the processor storage buffer, and Job2 , a
reader from that buffer, run
concurrently.  Job2 can obtain data from the processor storage buffer as
soon as Job1 writes the first
block.  Output from Job1 becomes immediately available as input to Job2.
You can think of the data as
"flowing" from Job1 to Job2.  The processor storage buffer is called, in
BatchPipes terms, a pipe, through
which data flows, always in the same direction, from a writer job to a
reader job. Writer-->pipe-->reader are
known as a pipeline.

To understand the elapsed time savings, compare timelines of traditional
job-to-job processing and
BatchPipes job-to-job processing.  The timeline for the traditional
processing of Job1 and Job2 might look like
this:


     <------Job1----><--------------Job2------->
     ___________________________________________
     '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '
     0     2     4     6     8    10     12    14


Figure 3. Timeline Showing a Traditional Two-Job Jobstream

With BatchPipes, the two jobs can run concurrently in less time than Job2
(the longer-running job) formerly
needed.  Elapsed time savings come from concurrent processing of the two
jobs, from elimination of I/O
wait times (time intervals during which a job waits for I/O transfers to and
from DASD or tape), and from
reduction of I/O transfer times between jobs.

The timeline for the two jobs using BatchPipes might look like this:


     <-------Job1----------->
      <-----------Job2------->
     ___________________________________________
     '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '  '
     0     2     4     6     8    10     12    14


Figure 4. Timeline Showing Two Jobs Using BatchPipes

Running the two jobs concurrently demonstrates a form of parallelism, the
simultaneous processing of
many tasks (or jobs) within one application. Figure 4 shows two jobs running
concurrently in a
multiprocessing environment.  Without BatchPipes, the jobs would not run
concurrently.

 %%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

BatchPipes is one of several software and hardware functions that IBM offers
to improve the elapsed time
of batch jobs that run on MVS.  You might be familiar with:

   VIO to expanded storage
   Hiperbatch
   Batch Local Shared Resources (BLSR)
   Sequential Data Striping.

To answer questions you might have about how BatchPipes compares with these
functions, this section
looks at whether these functions benefit the same type of batch jobstream
that BatchPipes benefits, a
jobstream where:

   The jobs use sequential QSAM, BSAM, or GSAM.
   Output from one job or jobstep is input to a second job or jobstep
   The data set is intermediate. (Note that BatchPipes provides several
ways to customize the data flow in  case you need a permanent copy of data
for backup or recovery.)

Both VIO to expanded storage and BatchPipes target similar batch
environments.  They eliminate the I/O wait times that occur when a job waits
for I/O transfers to and from storage devices; they handle data that is
intermediate.  VIO keeps all the data in expanded or auxiliary storage from
the time the data is created until  the entire data set is deleted;
BatchPipes keeps only a small portion of the data set in virtual storage at
one time.  Therefore, there is no limit to the amount of data that
BatchPipes can transfer.

VIO supports both random and sequential I/O; BatchPipes supports only
sequential I/O.

VIO improves performance for jobs that use data sets that are relatively
small.  If the job requires large
quantities of data (for example millions of records), VIO is not suitable
because expanded or auxiliary
storage will be exhausted, or because the storage will be paged (eventually
to DASD) and performance
could actually decrease.

Because BatchPipes allows writer and reader jobs to run concurrently,
elapsed times generally improve
more with BatchPipes than with VIO.

VIO requires the use of expanded storage; BatchPipes does not.

Hiperbatch targets a different type of batch application from BatchPipes.
With hiperbatch, the target
application has many readers simultaneously accessing data that one writer
job created.  The shared data all resides in processor storage and on DASD;
the data persists after being read, subject only to reuse of hiperbatch
buffers by other hiperbatch activity. In contrast, BatchPipes holds very
little of the data in processor storage at one time, and the output from the
writer job is temporary.

With hiperbatch, readers run simultaneously with each other, but not with
the writer; with BatchPipes, writer and reader jobs run simultaneously.

With hiperbatch, there's always the chance the reader might read from DASD,
rather than processor
storage; with BatchPipes, the reader always finds the data in processor
storage.

BLSR also targets a different type of batch application than BatchPipes.
With BLSR, the target application uses direct or keyed VSAM in a
non-sequential access pattern.  In contrast, BatchPipes supports QSAM and
BSAM in a completely sequential access pattern. Further, for data integrity
reasons, BLSR writers and readers must run serially.  BatchPipes writers and
readers must run concurrently.

BLSR is not generally suitable for sequential access patterns (because the
performance could actually
decrease) and BatchPipes is not suitable for random access patterns.

Sequential Data Striping benefits batch jobs that spend much of their
elapsed time sequentially reading or writing large DASD files.  When a data
set is created, Sequential Data Striping allocates the data set on as many
as 16 volumes.  Jobs that later read the data have their read requests
processed in parallel from those devices;  Sequential Data Striping
automatically "reassembles" the records as the data is transferred from
DASD.  Once all the records are reassembled, Sequential Data Striping
returns control to the job.

With Sequential Data Striping, the data is kept on DASD; with BatchPipes,
the data temporarily resides in processor storage.  Sequential Data Striping
reduces I/O operations and I/O wait times; BatchPipes eliminates them.

^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
1.5 Preparing to Use BatchPipes

BatchPipes is not part of MVS itself; it is a subsystem, running as a
started task under MVS.  Installing,
initializing, and starting BatchPipes is similar to getting any
SMP-installed program product up and running.  For your initial release of
BatchPipes, a system programmer installs the BatchPipes code and initializes
one or more instances of BatchPipes subsystems (using parmlib members to
define the subsystem to MVS), then IPLs MVS.  With proper planning,
subsequent installations of BatchPipes do not require an IPL.

Once BatchPipes is installed and candidate jobs examined and chosen, using
BatchPipes with your jobs requires JCL and scheduling changes.

Data Set DD Statement Changes:  The DD statements that describe the output
data from the writer and
the input data to the reader must tell the system to use BatchPipes, rather
than the I/O subsystem, to
transfer the data. The specific parameter on the DD statement is:

    SUBSYS=subsysname


where subsysname identifies the BatchPipes subsystem that is to control
the data flow.

For example, to tell the system that the writer of the data set named
ACCTRD.TEMP is using the BatchPipes subsystem named BP01 to control its I/O
requests, the DD statement looks like the following:

  file://SYSUT2 DD DSN=ACCTRD.TEMP,SUBSYS=BP01,
  //          LRECL=80,RECFM=FB

The reader job would have a similar JCL specification, including the
SUBSYS=BP01 parameter.

Scheduling Changes:  You might need to change the scheduling of the jobs so
that they can run
concurrently.  This might mean:

   Changing input to job scheduling packages that you are using, such as
Operations, Planning &
    Control/ESA (OPC/ESA)
   Evaluating the use of initiators to reflect the added job or jobs that
are competing for the processor's use.

Application JCL Changes:  BatchPipes transfers data from one job to another.
To use BatchPipes to
transfer data between two or more jobsteps in the same job, JCL statements
must be changed to turn the jobsteps into separate jobs.

Another JCL change that might increase the benefit of BatchPipes is
described in "Running BatchPipes with Multiple Copies of a Job" in topic
1.8.

Application Code Changes:  BatchPipes requires no changes to applications'
normal I/O declares,
macros, or routines; it intercepts application requests for I/O and passes
the data through processor
storage.
========================================================


PLEASE ALSO, note, that many sites have an 'EXIT' that reads all the CONSOLE
messages
looking for a specific STRING at the beginning.  Once that string is found,
the exit executes
a predefined job or takes a specific action in the message.  That is how
many places a CICS
transaction or program can 'execute' a batch process.  There is another
method under CICS 4 and
later, but it doesn't apply here anyways.
```

```
"Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net> wrotf:

>As we now have an increased vendor presence in the Newsgroup, let me ask
>a question if I may:

>Is there going to be a 2001 User's conference?  And why was there no
>2000 conference?


***  Way back in late October/early November of last year, I was    
      working up the training budget for my organization.  Since we
      hadn't heard anything at the 1999 Users Conference about one
      in 2000, I gave the friendly folks at Merant a call.  I can't
      remember who I talked to, but the gentleman told me no
      Users Conference for 2000.  Too much money, and that
      they'd rather 'customize' training for individual customers' 
      needs..  I for one greatly miss the conference!!!

      Mike Madara
      Prince Frederick, MD
```

#### ↳ Re: 2001 Merant Users Conference?

- **From:** Steve Burns <stephen.d.burns2@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3963766D.5B6D594F@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<8jqr8f$np4$1@news.chatlink.com>`

```
There is a national organization of micro focus user groups (consiting
mostly of presidents of the various regional groups).  We meet every
month by telephone, and have been doing so for 4 or 5 years.  When
Merant first announced that there would be no 2000 annual users
conference, that organization responded (almost immediately) with a
position paper.

The postion of the user group presidents was that the annual conference
was extremely valuable for learning about merant products and for
networking with other users.   Merant had intended to replace the annual
conference with increased internet seminars and meetings.  we pointed
out that the networking would not happen via the internet.

In any case Merant announced that the annual conference was extremely
costly and they felt they could use that budget in a more cost effective
manner.  So far I have not seen much in the way of regional seminars or
internet offerings.  There have been a few that have been offered, but
nothing that comes close to the content available in just a half day of
the old annual conference.  There has been no word at all about a
conference for 2001.

Since the lead time for a conference that size is a year or more, I am
fairly sure there will NOT be a Merant users conference in 2001, unless
it is rather late in the year.....  there has not been as much as a hint
thta Merant is considering bringing back the old annual conference, or
even some scaled down, cheaper version.

"Lucas, Todd" wrote:
> 
> As we now have an increased vendor presence in the Newsgroup, let me ask
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 2000 conference?
```

```
(It's dumb, there should be one in Calgary with the greatest

While the old style M/F conference would partially have had its uses I

to shake out from the trees - but to date no success. Maybe you User
Group presidents can make some suggestions or co-operate with Ed ?

restricts financially for folks not domiciled in the US.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Paddy Coleman wrote:
> 
> Folks,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> MERANT International.

Too bad.  I think MERANT is being very short sighted on this.

Even small software houses with niche products hold user conferences. 
They may not be at some fancy hotel in Las Vegas, or they may not rent
out Disney World, but they still manage to hold something somewhere. 
Conferences build customer loyalty, encourage customer networks, and
create feedback between customers and the developers--all of which are
highly valuable.  

Can it be that MERANT is so in tune with their customers that they know
exactly what they want, what it should cost, and how it should be
delivered (with or without 'amnesty')?  Do MERANT customers buy whatever
product is "the next new thing you must have" without seeing it or
talking to people using it?  Do all MERANT customers enjoy such a close
and warm relationship with their sales rep that they don't need to speak
with anyone else in the company, or ask anyone else a question?  If the
answer is "yes" to all of the above, then I guess a user conference is
not needed.  :)

Besides, now I have to tell my kids they aren't going to Disney World
next year either!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 2001 Merant Users Conference?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k4gsm$t84$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
David,

I personally understand your comments and I too will miss Disney World <g>.
Mind you, my children are not as forgiving so I have had to fund a family
holiday to Orlando this summer!

World (as MERANT or co-sponsering as with the recent Microsoft event
in Orlando).  It is intended that these events will be better focussed on
MERANT's Egility solution areas.

All the best.

Paddy Coleman
```

```
Steve,
  In "ye goode olde dayse," when I worked for Micro Focus (not MERANT),  I
spoke at several user groups.  HOWEVER, these trips were:

 A) *always* requested by the user-groups
 B) usually associated (if out of town) with some other "customer visits"
(either current or prospective customers).

I don't know what has happened in recent years (i.e. "let's stress Egility
and not COBOL") - but have the user groups REQUESTED "technical"
representatives to speak at their meetings?  I won't swear to it, but my
GUESS is that if you want "improved" participation/representatives of
specific technologies, that ASKING for it is the first step. The second step
would probably be helping MERANT to see the "cost-justification" of getting
such people to your groups (especially if they are from Newbury).

This is PERSONAL opinion only - and does NOT represent any input (official or
unofficial) from current MERANT representatives.
```

- **References:** `<8jqr8f$np4$1@news.chatlink.com> <3963766D.5B6D594F@boeing.com> <396397E3.FF2D53A8@home.com> <39648668.13A10389@erols.com> <8k2b7j$ko1$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <3964FEB4.6BA8B538@larich.com> <8k4gsm$t84$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <396617FE.8C223491@boeing.com> <8k5c1t$3d5$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <072bdb19.f560f6e3@usw-ex0101-006.remarq.com> <OYpYzjH6$GA.357@cpmsnbbsa07> <39673691.126853918@207.126.101.100>`

```
I understand what you are saying that it can't be done without corporate
involvement however, one would think that those with vested interest would
want to raise the level of understanding of todays capabilities of COBOL.
By this I mean all of the companies that provide compilers and related tools
for mainframes, mid-tiers and PC platforms i.e. IBM, UNISYS, Merant,
Fujitsu, FLEXUS, CA, Compuware etc.  I realize that some support a broad
range of languages and may be more interested in other than COBOL.  It would
appear to me that an organization that supports COBOL might be a plausible
solution like other industries have i.e. Apple Growers Association,
Association of Cotton Growers, Association of Builders, HMO Association etc
where the association supports an idea/concept/industry rather that one
particular vendor.  The vendors would be dues paying members and the
association would be non profit run by paid professionals with marketing,
sales(Success Stories) and advertising skills.  The associations that I used
are not necessarily real just examples.

Just an idea by an "Old Fart" who thinks that COBOL is the best solution in
certain situations just like C is the best solution in others and VB in
others etc.  If you have a toothache you go to a dentist not an MD and you
don't take a 18 wheeler to the grocery store for a loaf of bread & milk.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 2001 Merant Users Conference?

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mcv95.533$B92.257@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<8jqr8f$np4$1@news.chatlink.com> <3963766D.5B6D594F@boeing.com>`

```
Steve,

I finally found a newsgroup that seems to be listing all of the messages
(knock on wood!).

I would agree with you in that Merant does not need to be overly
extravagant at the conferences that they hold.  I mean c'mon - the
Bellagio in Vegas after it first opened?  A room there is $200+ a
night - I can imagine what it would cost to hold a conference there.  I
don't even want to think about what Disney cost's.  Like everyone else I
like to combine the vacation with the conference attendance, but they
don't have to be in the exact same location.

As for me, I don't need anything fancy - I just need something to
interact with other users and to get a chance to get their opinions on
what is best for our (C-Cubed) users.  The only chance I get to hear
about Merant related topics are from Merant sales people - and we all
know that they always have THE BEST solution for a customers needs <-
being sarcastic in case you couldn't tell.

The conferences equate to 80% of our (C-Cubed) marketing resources, and
now that they are gone we are scrambling for ways to promote our
products and services.  Merant sales people are not going to talk about
most of our products, as we offer alternatives to Merant products -
their theory is "why promote the competition when we can make $$$ with
our own products - even IF it is not the BEST solution".  That is why
I'm concerned with the "no big user conference" solution.  Sometimes a
potential user needs to have ALL options and views presented to them,
allowing them to make a more informed decision about what to purchase
and try.

Just my thoughts on the subject...

Todd Lucas
E-Mail:  Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net


Steve Burns <stephen.d.burns2@boeing.com> wrote in message
news:3963766D.5B6D594F@boeing.com...

or

of
```

```
I have never tried it, but it seems that a CALL "SYSTEM" USING COMM-LINE
where COMM-LINE has something like the input for a mail message (eg. "mail
a.rusbach...") should work. If the report is not plain text, then it should
be sent as an attachment.
regards,
Paulo Vieira, Emporsoft

"Anton" <a.rusbach@coss.nl> wrote in message
news:8jhp8i$rom$1@news1.xs4all.nl...
> Dear all,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: generating an email

- **From:** "Albert Gielis" <L.Gielis@vici.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-07-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k2uk5$n3i$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`
- **References:** `<8jhp8i$rom$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`

```
Anton,

It's not as hard as it seems!

In your config file (the file in which you link external Cobol filenames to
the lp spooler (DEFINE-DEVICE etc.), add a device called MAILBOX. The syntax
will be pretty close to what is used for the lp spooler. I haven't got an
example handy (I'm at home) but you could try something like
    mail $LOGNAME
or something like that.
We use it at quite a lot of client sites.

We do not allow the user to mailk reports directly to other users; only to
his or her own mailbox. That should be fine as the user can easily forward
the output to whomever he/she wants. It also ensures that the user sees (and
god willing) check what goes out.

Good luck,

Albert Gielis
Anton <a.rusbach@coss.nl> schreef in berichtnieuws
8jhp8i$rom$1@news1.xs4all.nl...
> Dear all,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: generating an email

- **From:** "Anton" <a.rusbach@coss.nl>
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k4ba1$oem$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`
- **References:** `<8jhp8i$rom$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`

```
Thanks everyone for your input!

We made sure 'sendmail' was on the unix system and we built a small
testprogram that CALLs the SYSTEM using 'echo message | mail
a.rusbach@coss.nl'
which works great.

thanks again,
Anton Rusbach

8jhp8i$rom$1@news1.xs4all.nl...
```

```
"Dogan Dridi" <Dogan.Dridi@secu.de> wrote:

>Hi,
>i need a free Cobol-Compiler, but i can't find anyone in the Internet.
>Can anyone help me?
>
>

Dogan:

The best place to get a free compiler or to learn how to write COBOL
code is to buy Thane Hubbell's book, "Teach Yourself COBOL In 24
Hours!"

It is not only a great book, but also incudes 16 and 32 bit versions
of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  The ISBN number of Thane's book is:

0-672-31453-3

You can buy the book on-line at:

http://www.hasting.com
http://www.bookpool.com
http://www.barnesandnoble.com




Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
What's free or what's not free in a package is up for debate (buy a copy
of the free compiler on an expensive CD and get a free instruction
manual (text book).  But since we don't need to buy the book to get the
compiler (it can be downloaded for free off the net), the original "if"
clause is not true, making the "then" clause irrelevant.
```

```
ago. I still program in it at work, on the PC now, but we use Merant which is
*way* too expensive for my use at home. I figured that since the compiler is
free there's no problem with me copying it, but as far as I'm concerned, I paid
for that 'free' compiler. The fact that I consider it money well spent is
irrelevant.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol-Compiler?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39637b08.251826987@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<39605d63$0$12930@businessnews.de.uu.net> <3960cc94.16763525@news.epix.net> <3960E609.139DA3A4@dced.state.ak.us> <68D0C78FAF99AE49.088DCA905487F0FB.5D1F435A5E39634E@lp.airnews.net> <39633072.C8D3D5B0@cusys.edu> <396358A4.E093679B@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
On Wed, 05 Jul 2000 07:47:49 -0800, Calvin Crumrine
<Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us> wrote:



>BTW, I bought Thane's book strictly for the compiler, then I gave it to my
>brother so he could learn COBOL. I learned it on the mainframe about 12 years
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>irrelevant.

Thanks first for buying the book.  The whole debate seems a bit silly,
but here's the deal.

I *INSISTED* that the book be packaged with a compiler.  Originally
the book was to come sans CD.  I felt and do feel strongly that if you
want someone to really learn the language they had better be provided
with a compiler.   I personally secured the Fujitsu version because
they were giving away the CD.  At the TIME it was not available for
download.  In addition to the full CD (the download has no manuals)
you get some bonus material in the evaluation version of sp2 (So I
could do GUI in a single chapter, not to mention the fact that I loke
the tool), and also some Lotus Screen Cam movies I creates showing how
to do different things with the compiler in case you had trouble.
Also, on the CD is content that would not fit in the book without
making it too large.  

The RETAIL price of the book is $24.99.  If the CD had NOT been
included the price would have been 19.99.  So you ARE paying for the
CD.  The price would have been the same if there were a CD with NO
compiler on it, so you are TECHNICALLY not paying for the compiler.
However, it IS true that the CD increased the final price of the book.
---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol-Compiler?

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39635288.11134868@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<39605d63$0$12930@businessnews.de.uu.net> <3960cc94.16763525@news.epix.net> <3960E609.139DA3A4@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us> wrote:

>Bob Wolfe wrote:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>the book to get the compiler then the compiler isn't free, is it?

Calvin:

Technically, you are 100% correct.  The book costs about $20.00 US$.
I guess if you are buying it and the compiler comes with it, one could
legitimately make the claim that the book costs $19.00 and the
compiler costs $1.00...or perhaps the book costs $1.00 and the
compiler costs $19.00 (or choose the combination of your choice).

I guess I just made the assumption that the book was a $20.00 book and
the compiler was thrown in for free.

You are indeed 100% accurate and I will change the note that I
frequently post to help students find a low-cost compiler.

Here is my revised text:

The best place to get an inexpensive compiler or to learn how to write
COBOL code is to buy Thane Hubbell's book, "Teach Yourself COBOL In 24
Hours!"

The book only costs about $20.00 US$ and includes the compiler at no
additional charge.

It is not only a great book, but also incudes 16 and 32 bit versions
of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  The ISBN number of Thane's book is:

0-672-31453-3

You can buy the book on-line at:

http://www.hasting.com
http://www.bookpool.com
http://www.barnesandnoble.com



Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-27e79d67fc"></a>
## Thread: Display and accept not working — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Display and accept not working

- **From:** "������������sl���xz������" <camslixz@fuse.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sm1lc6h7op3147@corp.supernews.com>`

```
quick question :

I'm just beginning an intro to COBOL class this week.  I'm using REALIA
compiler.  It's a simple label program that initially displays: "how many
labels would you like to print?" then accepts the amount.  My problem is
this...

On the computers at school after initializing the program the display
appears...accepts the amount an produces a file with the appropriate amount
of labels...      (running windows 95)

When I run the exe from my home PC I get a blank line with no cursor....if I
hit anything other than return, I lock up....when I hit return...the next
line returns a prompt.  The labels file is still being made but now there's
nothing in it....It's really confusing 'cause I know it works but it's not
working on my home machine....          (running windows 98)

I would really appreciate any ideas here....I would like to do my work from
home and not have to just work on the machines at school.
and yes I have the same compiler on my machine. I can compile and link to
create exe's but they won't run properly.
```

#### ↳ Re: Display and accept not working

- **From:** Rich Rohde <richNOriSPAM@richware.net.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1d5151d6.3537cc80@usw-ex0103-018.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<sm1lc6h7op3147@corp.supernews.com>`

```
When I first started writing COBOL program on the PC, I ran into
similar problems. The solution in all cases had to do with the
run-time files. Have you loaded the compiler and all files on
your home computer? If yes, are there some settings you have
overlooked? I know with MICRO FOCUS (MERANT) COBOL (in DOS), we
had a file ADISCTRL, with screen and keyboard settings, which
needs editing.

Best of luck..

Rich


-----------------------------------------------------------

Got questions?  Get answers over the phone at Keen.com.
Up to 100 minutes free!
http://www.keen.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Display and accept not working

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k06q7$mf0$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<sm1lc6h7op3147@corp.supernews.com>`

```
From one of my "usually reliable sources,"

"Hi Bill,

I checked with our support folks who said:

"Not aware of any problems but perhaps if the client could call us at
Support (630) 505-6926 with all of his pertinent info (Compiler Version,
Sample Pgm, etc), we would be able to assist him further."

I don't know if he is under maintenance nor what version of the compiler he
is using.  But if he is using a supported compiler, he should call that
number above and maybe our folks can figure out what the problem is.  But we
are not aware of any problems running under Win98."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Display and accept not working

- **From:** jamon <tpsllc1NOtpSPAM@aol.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-07-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<05da597e.29d1c5bf@usw-ex0107-055.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<sm1lc6h7op3147@corp.supernews.com> <8k06q7$mf0$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Try changing the font when the screen appears. The fonts are
different in 95 - 98. I had a problem with the cursor in the
wrong place or not showing.
```

```
   Did anybody of this group already use Relativity for RM-COBOL
85 indexed files with Fujitsu Power Cobol for Windows indexed
files?

have the same format and internal structure of the RM-COBOL085
indexed files.

Cobol indexed files and for the that I know, it doesn't exist a
officially liberated ODBC driver for Fujitsu.

   Rovani Marcelo Rech
   rovani@rech.com.br
   Novo Hamburgo/RS - Brazil

Up to 100 minutes free!
```

```
> have the same format and internal structure of the RM-COBOL085

It's possible if you limit your files to:

USAGE IS DISPLAY SIGN IS SEPARATE

Some troubles may come if you use duplicate record keys.

WBR, Spase.
```

## Thread: ODBC to MS-ACCESS — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### ODBC to MS-ACCESS

- **From:** bithh@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-07-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jr4u0$8ri$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I wonder if someone could give me some tips on how to formulate the
following SQL statements for the ODBC interface for MS-ACCESS:

1) EXEC SQL
     SELECT DEPTNAME, MGRNO
     INTO  :DEPTNAME, :MGRNO
     FROM DEPT
     WHERE DEPTNO = :DEPTNO
   END-EXEC

2)  EXEC SQL
      DECLARE C1 CURSOR FOR
        SELECT DEPTNO, DEPTNAME, MGRNO
        FROM DEPT
        WHERE ADMRDEPT LIKE 'AB%'
    END-EXEC

    EXEC SQL
      OPEN C1
    END-EXEC

    EXEC SQL
      FETCH C1
      INTO :DEPTNO, :DEPTNAME, :MGRNO
    END-EXEC

I am looking forward to an answer
thanks in advance


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```
