<a id="t-68105f1077"></a>
## Thread: Vendor "input" to the COBOL newsgroups — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### Vendor "input" to the COBOL newsgroups

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jr98o$dgv$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As your friendly "non-moderator," I wanted to make several statements about
vendor input into the COBOL newsgroups (the preferred, comp.lang.cobol as
well as the less used alt.cobol).

First and foremost, both of these newsgroups are NOT moderated.  Therefore,
anyone can post whatever they want with whatever "hiding" of their
affiliations and return email addresses that they want.   Having said that,
any and ALL input that "clearly" identifies the background knowledge and
motivation of the poster - as well as ways to contact the poster "off-line"
is much, MUCH preferred.

I think that everyone in the newsgroups really appreciates the "technical"
support that various vendor participants provide about THEIR OWN products.
When a question comes in from a Fujitsu user and gets a Fujitsu employee's
response (even if it is made clear that this is not an "official" reply), I
think that all the readers find this useful, beneficial, and desirable.
Similarly for all the other COBOL and tool vendors *and* distributors (e.g.
Micro Focus/MERANT, IBM, Flexus, Norcom, AcuCORP, etc ...)

However, (and I have commented on this in the past - via private and public
statements), when a question comes in from a user saying "How do I do this
with product XYZ" or "I am having the following specific problem with product
XYZ" - if we get a reply (either admitting or not that the poster is a vendor
or distributor),  "I can't help you with product XYZ, but with OUR product it
is easier/better/simpler/whatever - and doesn't have that problem".   Then we
usually find it less useful.  When the reply turns into a "basing" of product
XYZ by a vendor or distributor of product ABC, then many of us REALLY resent
it. (Occasionally, there are times when an employee of vendor ABC may be able
to explain some OTHER interface such as Windows and USEFULLY say - "I am not
certain that this is how it works with XYZ, but it does work with ABC and you
might want to try it with XYZ."

It is also HIGHLY appreciated when those "associated" with a product,
distributor, etc "identify" themselves when commenting on another product
(and usually even when they comment on their own).  Now most of us who are
regulars in comp.lang.cobol KNOW that Bob Wolfe is from Flexus and Paddy
Coleman is from MERANT.   Therefore, if either  of them forgot on one or two
messages to include their usual "signature line," I don't think anyone would
complain.  I don't know if all the regulars know that "gazaloo" is associated
with MERANT (and his return email doesn't make this clear - although he has
said this in some past posts), on the other hand,  Paul Barnett's return
email DOES make it clear that he is with MERANT.

Certainly the "recent" apparent "flames wars" seem to include
vendors/distributors who don't seem to be making their company
affiliations/backgrounds clear.  I won't say that this is "wrong" - but it is
certainly not what I like to see and when I find out about such "hidden
agendas," it tends to make me discount ALL posts from such posters.

General Hints to vendors and distributors:

1) Any and all comments about your OWN products are greatly appreciated.

2) Whenever  possible, please identify your company "affiliation" (or
background) - especially when you are commenting on ANOTHER company's
product.

3) The default (AFAIK) is to assume that any post from company employees is
"not the official statement" of the company, however, it never hurts to make
this clear when posting "personal input/opinions".  On the other hand,
(compare the recent statements from Tony Hill of MERANT), when you ARE giving
us "official policy," PLEASE do make this clear (and preferably, let us know
where/how we can get this "confirmed" via one of your official channels - not
just a newsgroup).

4) "Bashing" of other vendors' products reflects poorly on you and your
company.  You are free to do it (because this is a non-moderated NG), but
please consider the consequences before doing so.  I know that I have some
new names to add to my "killfile" and I suspect other NG readers may be doing
the same.
```

#### ↳ Re: Vendor "input" to the COBOL newsgroups

- **Date:** 2000-07-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jrfh1$4he6$1@newssvr04-int.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<8jr98o$dgv$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

Well put; I agree wholeheartedly.
```

```
Hi,

I successfully completed my 424/425 COBOL City & Guilds Distance Learning
Course with CIL Ltd UK (www.computeach.co.uk) and am offering my complete
Course work to anyone who will make an offer for it. I am now working
succesfully in the Industry as a result of this course so I don't need it
anymore.

Work includes Complete CIL Documentation and Trg Folders for all of the
Coursework and my marked (and passed) projects for:

Data Processing Priciples
Job Control Languages and Program Design
Programming in COBOL
COBOL Programming Projects (City & Guilds 424 and 425 advanced COBOL)


The complete course cost me �3,500. If someone wants all my course work for
�200 its theirs, providing they pay the postage and packaging.

Hope this helps someone out there,

Rgds,

John Ridgers UK
email John@Ridgers.com
Tel 01244 531718
```

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lBkC6qAldmY5IwB6@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8jt5hu$1a3o$1@shadow.skypoint.net>`

```
In article <8jt5hu$1a3o$1@shadow.skypoint.net>, Jonathan Laub 
<laubj@lakesoft.net> writes
>Help!
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Jonathan,

If you're getting this message from AcuBench version 4.x then you can 
try running 'initocxs.bat'. This can be found in (by default) 
C:\ACUCBL43\ACUSP.

I've seen this problem once, but I can't remember at present what the 
cause was.

If this doesn't solve your problem, or if you need more information then 
I'd recommend contacting your local support office. Alternatively please 
feel free to email me at <neaton@acucorp.com> and I'll do my best to 
help.

Best regards

Nigel
```

```
Now I really feel like a "duh"!

Acucobol is "user profile" aware.  Being on a notebook I work with several
profiles.  Each profile has its own OCX settings... need I say more.  For
each profile I use, had to re-install to get those profile settings created.

Thanks Anyway

Jonathan
```

## Thread: Finding COBOL success stories. — 3 msgs

### Finding COBOL success stories.

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39634290.237368534@207.126.101.100>`

```
I'm hoping to find someone here "who knows" the answser to this.

In the last few days PeopleSoft has announces a new suite of internet
delivered products.  I know that a large portion of PeopleSoft is in
COBOL, specifically MicroFocus (MERANT) COBOL.  Does anyone know if
PeopleSoft leveraged their COBOL using MERANT's Internet enabling
technology?

Thanks.
---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: Finding COBOL success stories.

- **From:** jim_shenton@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-07-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kur4u$hgn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<39634290.237368534@207.126.101.100>`

```
In article <39634290.237368534@207.126.101.100>,
  thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:
> I'm hoping to find someone here "who knows" the answser to this.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/

Hi Thane,

I don't know how PeopleSoft have acheived this, however if you want to
make use of an existing COBOL asset in a fully integrated way with new
development in Windows, ASP, Java, and XML then check out Transoft
Component Framework (TCF) http://www.transoft.com
This gives the ability to wrap COBOL code as a component called via
COM, CORBA, and EJB. In addition calls to components are handled by an
application server to give scalability.

Hope this is useful.

Jim
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-56117f4d64"></a>
## Thread: How to get "Gold Mind" a bad reputation — 24 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to get "Gold Mind" a bad reputation

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k0dkk$o36$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
(posted and mailed to ils-international)

Someone in the current GUI discussion thought that
   "Nowitt Aol"
        was intended to be read as "Know it all".

It seems to me that given how totally UNRESPONSIVE this person is and how
his/her tone is turning off so many of the other comp.lang.cobol readers, it
may be that we were reading this wrong.  Possibly, this is intended to be
read as,

   "No Wit, A**Hole"

Certainly the tone and approach of the posts would be more typical of a
disgruntled former employer or customer trying to DISCOURAGE anyone from
looking at Gold Mind than from someone who actually wanted those not familiar
with the product/services to look at it seriously.

Obviously, a "disgruntled" person would explain the "anonymous" postings as
well as the silly, conflicting, and counter-productive responses that have
appeared so far.

If someone at Gold Mind wants to track this person down and verify that it is
actually someone trying to sabotage their product, I know that most of the
newsgroup would be happy.  If, on the other hand, this actually is a person
TRYING to interest people in the capabilities of Gold Mind, I strongly,
STRONGLY, suggest that they review the posts so far and take this person to a
"netiquette 101" class.
```

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>

- **References:** `<8k0dkk$o36$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
> STRONGLY, suggest that they review the posts so far and take this person
to a
> "netiquette 101" class.

personality flaws such as those exhibited are rarely fixed by a different
medium.

(oh my, how righteous 2 glasses of red wine makes me -- the sequential
evenings of 15 pints, ducking high-heel wielding "ladies" with the evening
tempered by Chinese John's curry & chips, seems so long ago...)
```

```
Rick Price wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rick

Rick, I left in '75. But you 'Northerners' always did it differently to
us 'Southerners' - Jeez sounds like the US Civil War <G>.

If I recall, pizza was new vogue, and although Chinese restaurants were
coming in, no take-aways or Indian food. (Yes there would have been
Indian at Southall/Hayes around Heathrow - but basically serving and
Indian community). Although I very much like baked spuds with cheese
grilled(broiled) on top, your 'jacket potatoes' with various fillings is
DEFINITELY new since I left. So is the Americanization of menus.

Jimmy
```

```
brucepbarrett wrote:
> 
> When I was there it was "Fish & Chips" and vinegar.  Sounds new to me,  like
> corn as a topping for pizza.

I don't care for the American habit of having catsup on french fries
(chips), but I don't mind the European habit of putting mayonnaise on
it.

My take is that vinegar was to counter extremely greasy fries.  I don't
know if it is just a leftover these days, or whether the Brits still
have that amount of grease in their fries.

I don't find a desire to put lemon on my fish or sauce on my steak.
```

```
                                        or with (clear) vinegar (not the

                                        or with catsup,
```

```
Three Tuns - can't say I know it. Still wasn't an inveterate boozer. Did
you do sqaure-bashing at RAF Uxbridge ? Me, I was in the snotty part,
other side of the stream in Hillingdon House, Hq No 11 (Fighter) Group.

Little historical anecdote about Uxbridge. T.E Lawrence(of Arabia) under
the name Shaw served at Uxbridge in '20s. On parade one day SWO(Station
Warrant Office) noticed Shaw was medal-less. "Shaw, back to the barracks
! And come back wearin' your bleedin' medals !".

Aircraftsman Shaw did that - loaded everything he had from WWI, Star of
Iraq(Mesopotamia), Star of Islam, etc., etc.... The poor sod came back
on the parade ground weighed down one side with all the junk he had to
wear.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Lost Newsgroup Postings?

- **From:** Danial Clarke <danial.clarke@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3965E640.9C2E3D2E@merant.com>`
- **References:** `<8k08js$n2o$1@news.chatlink.com> <8k25ej$f4v$1@news.cerf.net> <zx795.124372$7o1.2892341@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
There are two different questions here:

1) Why do I only see some of the answers to a post?

The simple answer is that usenet/email/the internet isn't a perfect system. To
quote 'The Moderated Newsgroups FAQ':

    Also, e-mail, net news, and robo-mod software are imperfect, and
    human moderators are, after all, human.  Your message may have
    failed to reach the moderator, or been lost in a system or disk
    crash, or been misplaced by the moderator, or been approved and
    then failed to propagate correctly via net news back to your site.

In other words, somewhere between the moderator and your news server the
articles will have gone missing. For the same reasons you sometimes get articles
in the wrong order.

If you need to read the whole thread, the best advice is to try another news
server or a site like www.deja.com.

2) Too much old news

Whichever news server you're connecting to will have expiry times set up for
each newsgroup, which defines the minimum and maximum length of time the
articles will hang around for. Independant of that your newsreader (Outlook
Express in this case) keeps a record of which articles you've read. When you
open the newsgroup it should display headers for all the articles available,
with the ones you haven't read in bold.

Tim, if the problem is that it's not remembering which artices you've read then
it's a problem with your OE setup. However if it knows what you've read but just
has a long list of previously read articles then that's because your news server
is not expiring the articles very quickly. I wouldn't knock it - most people
complain that articles dissappear too quickly!


This is all a bit off-topic; for more info check out news.newusers.questions.

Dan.

Tim Hillock wrote:

> You guys are lucky, I've got postings dating back to March in this news
> group alone.  Its seems that everytime I update outlook express (1st version
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

```

news servers, or that the relative ordering will be retained.

Dan.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Lost Newsgroup Postings?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2000-07-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3966A3B6.98089C98@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<8k08js$n2o$1@news.chatlink.com> <8k25ej$f4v$1@news.cerf.net> <zx795.124372$7o1.2892341@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <3965E640.9C2E3D2E@merant.com>`

```


Danial Clarke wrote:
> 
> There are two different questions here:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The simple answer is that usenet/email/the internet isn't a perfect system. To
...
As I understand it, there is no "master" newsserver to insure everyone
is synchronized.  All newsservers are logically interconnected, but
(unlike email servers) each only speaks directly to a few immediate
neighbor(s).  Smaller ISP's may have only a single feed (one neighboring
server), while larger ISP's tend to have multiple feeds.  When someone
enters a news item, it propagates from his newsserver to its neighbors
in the network, to the neighbor's neighbors, etc. until it eventually
reaches all newsservers -- provided there isn't a hardware glitch, a
software glitch, or a shortage of resources or self-imposed message
count limit on one of the receiving nodes to break the chain.  You can
also expect that some messages will arrive at your newsserver in
different a order from that seen on other newsservers.   The fewer the
feeds into your ISP's newsserver, the higher the odds of lost messages. 
Several years ago when I went from a smaller ISP with one feed to one
with multiple feeds, I saw a five-fold increase in the number of
messages seen in one of my newsgroups.  It's also possible that your own
ISP may have insufficient hardware resources dedicated to the newsserver
and is at times rejecting some inbound news traffic that exceeds its
capacity.

You might try comparing message counts with someone who has a different
newsserver or perhaps with dejanews.  If your ISP has significant
message-loss problems on news groups that are important to you and is
unable or unwilling to improve, try another ISP.  
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
by checking the feeds from multiple places.  I am NOT recommending that as
the "normal" way of processing NGs - but it might be worthwhile to check it
occasionally for "missing" messages.
```

## Thread: Advanced COBOL topics? — 16 msgs

### Advanced COBOL topics?

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pb46ms86b7eebd5h9smj7ia4achboabuth@4ax.com>`

```
Hello fellow COBOL listers,

I am currently in the process of creating a new COBOL course with the (temporary) title
"COBOL for OS/390 - Advanced Programming Workshop"

While I have compiled a few topics that might be of interest to the participants, I wonder
if there are topics that I overlooked or which you would like to be included in such a
course.

This course is not intended to teach programming per se, but the COBOL features that are
around (or will be around soon (?)).

The planned environment is for OS/390 - but non-os/390 suggestions are very welcome

My current list of topics include (no particular order yet)

        Compile time options *)
	Run time options *)
	Compiler directing Statements
		COPY .... REPLACING
	Advanced Table processing
		Searching
		Occurs Depending On
		Hashing
	File processing
		Processing varying length records
	Intrinsic functions
		currently implemented
		functions proposed in the coming standard
	LE/370 callable services
	Sort and Merge
		QSAM
		VSAM
		tables
	Report Writer
	Performance Considerations
		PIC STRINGS
		USAGE clauses
		Statement usage
        Problem Determination
		Source level
			Declaratves
			WITH DEBUGGING MODE
		Post Mortem		
			Understanding the compiler listing
			Working with the Linkage Editor listing
		Post Moretem via Dump
			Basic Dump navigation 
				Instruction executed
				Variables involved
				Finding working-Storage and Linkage Section in the dump
		Post Mortem via LE/370
	
*) These will not be separate Sessions, but will be woven into the other session as needed


As I said, these are the sessions that are (nearly) finished.  If you have any comments
(good) or addtions/proposals (better) ... have a go at it




     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)



      When we are planning for posterity, we ought to remember that virtue is not hereditary. -- Thomas Paine
```

#### ↳ Re: Advanced COBOL topics?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000705094852.17839.00000285@ng-fj1.aol.com>`

```
Volker Bandke writes ...

>Hello fellow COBOL listers,
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>


-Some thoughts as another vendor interspersed, for comparison


>        Compile time options *)
>	Run time options *)

- I include these in my debugging courses



>	Compiler directing Statements
>		COPY .... REPLACING

- this is simple; it's in my intro class



>	Advanced Table processing
>		Searching
>		Occurs Depending On
>		Hashing

- yes, first two in my advanced topics course, also; hashing? hmm. not very
COBOL per se



>	File processing
>		Processing varying length records

- yes, in my advanced class also


>	Intrinsic functions
>		currently implemented

- some in my intro, all in my advanced course

>		functions proposed in the coming standard

- hmm; interesting; I stay away from topics that are not available, except in
occasional passing references




>	LE/370 callable services

- different course; all services covered for COBOL, PL/I, C, and Assembler in
"Application Design Using LE Services"



>	Sort and Merge
>		QSAM
>		VSAM
>		tables

- from a COBOL perspective, QSAM and VSAM look the same to the sort verb; sort
verb does not apply to IBM COBOL for tables in memory [yet]




>	Report Writer

- really? I get zero requests for this; understand that it is not a required
module for the current version but will once again be required for the new
standard; I'll wait 'til then.




>	Performance Considerations
>		PIC STRINGS
>		USAGE clauses
>		Statement usage

- hmm; I sprinkle this through out various courses; maybe a section on this
would be good; remember to discuss performance implications of compiler and
run-time options also




>        Problem Determination
>		Source level
>			Declaratves
>			WITH DEBUGGING MODE

- in my debugging classes; amazing how few people are aware of these features



>		Post Mortem		
>			Understanding the compiler listing
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>				Finding working-Storage and Linkage Section in
the dump

- in my debugging classes



>		Post Mortem via LE/370

- I assume you mean debugging using LE dumps; I put this in my debugging
classes.


What? no STRING, UNSTRING, INSPECT, subroutines, parameter passing and
receiving, nested programs, multiple entry points, local-storage, recursive
programs, null-terminated strings, pointers or procedure-pointers? Or do you
include these in your intro class?

	

[snip]



Regards,


Steve Comstock

www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com

Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

## Thread: ACU-COBOL and report writing — 4 msgs

### ACU-COBOL and report writing

- **From:** "Russ" <r_koning@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iJI85.2152$8Q5.21046@news22.bellglobal.com>`

```
I have a program written in ACU-COBOL that I want to produce reports from.
I don't have any ODBC drivers for the program.  Is there any report writer I
can use that could interpret these files (even if I have to tell it what
each field is) without having the source code for the program?

I guess my question is - is there any report writer program that natively
reads acu-cobol?

Thanks,
```

#### ↳ Re: ACU-COBOL and report writing

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39637aa5.251727742@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<iJI85.2152$8Q5.21046@news22.bellglobal.com>`

```
On Wed, 05 Jul 2000 15:44:14 GMT, "Russ" <r_koning@yahoo.com> wrote:

>I have a program written in ACU-COBOL that I want to produce reports from.
>I don't have any ODBC drivers for the program.  Is there any report writer I
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>reads acu-cobol?

I'm 80% sure that AcuCOBOL supports the COBOL report writer syntax.
Additionally, if you want to use something like Crystal reports,
AcuCorp does have ODBC drivers for their Vision file system.

---
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ACU-COBOL and report writing

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 2000-07-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k25a7$f4s$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<iJI85.2152$8Q5.21046@news22.bellglobal.com> <39637aa5.251727742@207.126.101.100>`

```
"Thane Hubbell" <thaneH@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:39637aa5.251727742@207.126.101.100...
> I'm 80% sure that AcuCOBOL supports the COBOL report writer syntax.

To my knowledge, as of 5.0.0 Acucobol does not support report section. I
must also admit that I have never really missed it. I belong in the camp
that does not see the big benefit with the report section. It automates a
few things yes, but in general, it is as easy as to do the report manually.

> Additionally, if you want to use something like Crystal reports,

DI Systemer AS in Norway has a Windows report tool which is unique in the
sense that they provide you with a DLL that functions as a printer engine,
leaving to you as a developer to determine the level of control you want,
ranging from complete (the user won't see the engine at all) to virtually
non existing (all conditions are handled by the engine). The scoop with this
utility is that it totally removes the tedious task of designing the report
from the Cobol developer, so he/she can focus on what counts, namely the
program logic. The report may be designed using a wysiwyg designer, putting
graphics and data where you want. The report may then be designed and
modified by anyone, be it the support department, a value adding reseller
and/or the customer himself.

A generic demonstration:

    load DLL
    load preferred form
    open print
    pump data
    request status
    close print
    unload DLL

The data are transferred like: "FIELD_ID;DATA"

You may have multiple reports and whatever redesign that is done to the
reports there are no need to modify the Cobol source. The idea is that the
Cobol application transmits whatever data it has to the printer engine, the
printer engine then picks what the report says it needs. The beauty of this
is obvious compared to report utilities like Crystal Report and other ODBC
dependent report utilities, you keep control and use of the original logic
in your Cobol application.

There are support graphical basics and you may even do a preview of the
report to the screen.

The engine was originally developed for DI Systemer AS own customers in
Norwegian, but is now translated to English and they are considering to let
other software companies use it.

The software may of course be used with other languages than Acucobol. The
demand is that the hosting application must be 32 bit Windows capable of
using a DLL.

Cobol rules.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ACU-COBOL and report writing

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<+yS0WrA6GlZ5IwYs@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<iJI85.2152$8Q5.21046@news22.bellglobal.com> <39637aa5.251727742@207.126.101.100> <8k25a7$f4s$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
In article <8k25a7$f4s$1@news.cerf.net>, Cheesle 
<cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> writes
>To my knowledge, as of 5.0.0 Acucobol does not support report section. I
>must also admit that I have never really missed it. I belong in the camp
>that does not see the big benefit with the report section. It automates a
>few things yes, but in general, it is as easy as to do the report manually.
>

Correct, we do not currently support report section. As and when the new 
standard demands it we intend (AFAIK) to implement it.

I agree with Cheesle that this will have limited appeal though. There 
are several reporting packages which understand Vision files natively, 
and (of course) AcuODBC gives ODBC access to Vision files. Feel free to 
email me for a list of vendors offering native Vision access, or (as 
time allows) I'll post a representative list here.

Best regards

Nigel
```

---

<a id="t-97c1e10a83"></a>
## Thread: Open Implementation — 3 msgs

### Open Implementation

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jv370$vgq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Open Implementation

I recently ran across a new (to me) concept that might interest the
group.

The standard concept of modular programming says that the calling
program does not know or care about how the called module does its
job.  Open implementation says that there are cases where the strategy
used by a called module is important.  Where the strategy used by the
called module is inappropriate, the calling program may create its own
version of the module code using a more appropriate strategy or
otherwise fudge the process to achieve better results.  OI calls for a
mechanism to let the calling program set the strategy used by a module
or provide information to the called module so it can select an optimum
strategy.


A COBOL Example

Suppose you have a list of codes and related data in a sequential
file.  Various programs need to retrieve the data associated with a
specific code.  This is a natural situation for creating a module to
load the data from the file into a working storage table and retrieve
the data for a code.

There are two strategies that the module can use.

1. Use the file data in an unsorted state and use a sequential lookup
(SEARCH).
2. Sort the file data after it is loaded and use a binary search
(SEARCH ALL).

Sorting the file data adds an overhead cost to the module, but the
binary search is more efficient, especially if there are many values in
the table.  The optimum strategy depends upon two factors - the number
of values in the table and the number of times the module is called.
If there are only a small number of values in the table, the cost of
the two types of searches may be equivalent.  If the module is only
called a small number of times, there may be insufficient gains from
the more efficient search to offset the cost of sorting the data.

The OI solution is to have both strategies in the module and let it
choose the best one for the circumstances.


Objections

I can think of a few objections to the OI concept.

1. It is unnecessary.  Given the power of modern computers, it makes no
difference whether the called modules are efficient or not.

However, COBOL systems frequently deal with very large files.  When you
start processing millions of records, small improvements in performance
can still count.

2. It is too costly.  The extra code will cost more than the benefits
gained.  It would be more cost efficient to spend the money on hardware
than on programming.

However, the cases where OI is appropriate can occur frequently within
an application or company.  If you write off the cost over many
instances, then the programming can be justified.

3. Good application specifications should allow a software designer to
choose the best strategy from the beginning.  There should be no need
for elaborate programming.

Unfortunately, specs are not always trustworthy.  (Let�s face it. They
are never trustworthy.)  Even in legacy systems where the use of a
module is well known and understood, business rules can change
overnight.  Its all too frequent that we get a message from the gods
like �Now that we have completed the web site that allows our clients
to enter orders directly, management has decided to process orders
every half hour rather than in one overnight batch.  We expect that
each transaction batch will have no more than 10% of the previous daily
batch and most will have less than 1%.  Of course, the rapid and
efficient processing of orders is now more important than ever to meet
the expectations of our web customers.�


Code

I have written an example to test out the OI solution.  The code is in
a separate message.  The code consists of a program (openimp) to test
the called module and a called module (tblookup) to look up values in
the data.


Documentation

The tblookup module accepts three values:
- a one-character code that tells the module which function to
perform.  The codes are - "C" to configure itself for an optimum
strategy and "F" to find and return the data associated with a code
from the table.
- parameter1 - for the configure function, this is the expected number
of calls to the module as a COMP-3 S9(7) value.  For the find function,
this is the code to find in the table.
- parameter2 - for the configure function, this value is not used.  For
the find function, this area is filled with the data found in the
table.  If tblookup cannot find an entry for a code, it fills this area
with hex 00 (LOW-VALUES).

Note that tblookup is written to make it as easy as possible to clone
the module for tables with different code and data sizes.

To choose the optimum strategy, tblookup estimates the cost of using
each strategy and chooses the one with the lowest cost.  The cost of a
sequential search is number-of-calls * file-size * factor1, where
factor1 is the cost of doing a sequential search per table item.  The
cost of the binary search is the cost of sorting the data (file-size *
factor2) plus the cost of doing the search (number-of-calls * file-size
* factor3).

I picked the values that for the factors in the code from thin air and
they no relationship to reality.  The factors themselves will vary with
the environment - computer, operating system, compiler used, etc.
However, it should be possible to determine them experimentally.

The openimp program
- creates a test file of values
- calls tblookup to configure the module
- calls tblookup to find a value that is in the table
- calls tblookup to find a value that is not in the table.

The test file is written in a reasonably random order to test out the
sorting procedure.


References

http://www.parc.xerox.com/spl/projects/oi/
```

#### ↳ Re: Open Implementation

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39634312.FCC9F69F@iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<8jv370$vgq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Ed Milne wrote:
> 
> Open Implementation
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
<snip>
Interesting, however, for the example you cite I would almost always use
an Indexed File or a Database.
No point solving a problem that has been solved already.

I note that this would require an awful lot more code, just how many
strategies were you going to provide for each caller?

With OO, this technique becomes redundant, because you can overload the
method when a new caller requires something special, or if you wish a
cleaner more understandable implementation, code another method.
```

#### ↳ Re: Open Implementation

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8jv3dg$vq2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8jv370$vgq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Here is the source code described in the previous message.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. tblookup.
      * Generic template for a module to lookup and return data from
      * a sequential file.

      * To change the maximum size of the table
      * - change the value in table-max
      * - change the OCCURS value for table-item
      * To change the length of the key
      * - change the table-key definition
      * - change the value in table-key-length
      * To change the length of the data
      * - change the table-data definition
      * - change the value in table-data-length

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT tabrc-file ASSIGN TO tabfile
             ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL
             ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  tabrc-file.
       01  tabrc-record                                    PIC X(1000).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  work-fields.
           05 ws-when-compiled         VALUE SPACE        PIC X(8)BX(8).
           05 item-nbr                 VALUE ZERO   COMP   PIC S9(5).
           05 swap-nbr                 VALUE ZERO   COMP   PIC S9(5).
           05 jump-size                VALUE ZERO   COMP   PIC S9(5).
           05 upper-limit              VALUE ZERO   COMP   PIC S9(5).
           05 no-of-calls-x.
             10 no-of-calls            VALUE ZERO   COMP-3 PIC S9(5).
           05 sequential-search-time   VALUE ZERO   COMP-2.
           05 binary-search-time       VALUE ZERO   COMP-2.
       01  switches.
           05 record-swapped-sw        VALUE 'Y'           PIC X.
             88 record-swapped         VALUE 'Y'.
           05 first-time-sw            VALUE 'Y'           PIC X.
             88 first-time             VALUE 'Y'.
           05 end-of-file-sw           VALUE SPACE         PIC X.
             88 end-of-file            VALUE 'Y'.
           05 strategy-sw              VALUE 'S'           PIC X.
             88 strategy-sequential    VALUE 'S'.
             88 strategy-binary        VALUE 'B'.
       01  table-area.
           05 table-max                VALUE 1000   COMP   PIC S9(4).
           05 table-size               VALUE ZERO   COMP   PIC S9(4).
           05 table-key-length         VALUE 2      COMP   PIC S9(4).
           05 table-data-length        VALUE 10     COMP   PIC S9(4).
           05 table-item OCCURS 0 TO 1000
                         DEPENDING ON table-size
                         ASCENDING KEY table-key
                         INDEXED BY tabidx.
             10 table-key                                  PIC XX.
             10 table-data                                 PIC X(10).

       LINKAGE SECTION.
       01  link-method                                     PIC X.
           88 link-method-configure    VALUE 'C'.
           88 link-method-find         VALUE 'F'.
       01  link-parm1                                      PIC X(1000).
       01  link-parm2                                      PIC X(1000).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING link-method
                                link-parm1
                                link-parm2.
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Main program logic
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
           EVALUATE TRUE
           WHEN link-method-configure
             MOVE link-parm1(1:LENGTH OF no-of-calls)
               TO no-of-calls-x
           WHEN NOT link-method-find
             DISPLAY 'tblookup-001: Invalid method code ' link-method
             STOP RUN
           WHEN first-time
             INITIALIZE first-time-sw
             PERFORM 0100-initialization
             PERFORM 2000-find-item
           WHEN OTHER
             PERFORM 2000-find-item
           END-EVALUATE

           GOBACK
           .
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Prepare the program for processing
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
       0100-initialization.
      * Display the WHEN-COMPILED date as the program version.
           MOVE WHEN-COMPILED              TO ws-when-compiled
           DISPLAY 'tblookup-002: Version ' ws-when-compiled
      * Load the table with data from the file
           PERFORM 7000-load-table
      * Determine the best strategy
           PERFORM 1000-determine-strategy
      * Sort the table if using a binary search
           IF strategy-binary
             PERFORM 8000-sort-table
           END-IF
           .
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Determine the optimum strategy from the configuration parameters
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
       1000-determine-strategy.
           COMPUTE sequential-search-time = no-of-calls
                                          * table-size
                                          * .001
           COMPUTE binary-search-time = (table-size * .002)
                                      + (no-of-calls
                                       * table-size
                                       * .0005)
           IF sequential-search-time > binary-search-time
             SET strategy-binary           TO TRUE
           ELSE
             SET strategy-sequential       TO TRUE
           END-IF
           .
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Find a matching item in the table and return its data
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
       2000-find-item.
           SET tabidx                      TO 1
           IF strategy-binary
             PERFORM 2100-binary-search
           ELSE
             PERFORM 2200-sequential-search
           END-IF
           .
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Find a matching item in the table using a binary search
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
       2100-binary-search.
           SEARCH ALL table-item
           AT END
             MOVE LOW-VALUE
               TO link-parm2(1:table-data-length)
           WHEN link-parm1(1:table-key-length) = table-key(tabidx)
             MOVE table-data(tabidx)
               TO link-parm2(1:table-data-length)
           END-SEARCH
           .
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Find a matching item in the table using a sequential search
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
       2200-sequential-search.

           SEARCH table-item
             VARYING tabidx
           AT END
             MOVE LOW-VALUE
               TO link-parm2(1:table-data-length)
           WHEN link-parm1(1:table-key-length) = table-key(tabidx)
             MOVE table-data(tabidx)
               TO link-parm2(1:table-data-length)
           END-SEARCH
           .
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Load data from the file into the working storage table
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
       7000-load-table.
           OPEN INPUT tabrc-file

           PERFORM
             UNTIL end-of-file
             READ tabrc-file
             AT END
               SET end-of-file             TO TRUE
             NOT AT END
               ADD  1                      TO table-size
               IF table-size >= table-max
                 DISPLAY 'tblookup-003: table overflow'
                 STOP RUN
               END-IF
               SET  tabidx                 TO table-size
               MOVE tabrc-record           TO table-item(tabidx)
             END-READ
           END-PERFORM

           CLOSE tabrc-file
           .
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Sort the table
      * Logic for a COMB Sort taken from
      * www.etk.com/papers/sorting/sorting.htm
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
       8000-sort-table.
           MOVE table-size                 TO jump-size
      * use the next free item in the table as a swap area
           ADD  1                          TO table-size
           PERFORM 8100-sort-cycle
             UNTIL NOT record-swapped
               AND jump-size <= 1
      * reset the table to its correct size
           SUBTRACT 1                    FROM table-size
           .
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Process one cycle of the sorting procedure
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
       8100-sort-cycle.
           COMPUTE jump-size = (10 * jump-size + 3) / 13
           COMPUTE upper-limit = table-size - jump-size - 1
           INITIALIZE record-swapped-sw
           PERFORM 8200-swap-items
             VARYING item-nbr FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL item-nbr > upper-limit
           .
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Compare and, if necessary, swap two table items
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
       8200-swap-items.
           COMPUTE swap-nbr = item-nbr + jump-size
           IF table-key(item-nbr) > table-key(swap-nbr)
             MOVE table-item(item-nbr)     TO table-item(table-size)
             MOVE table-item(swap-nbr)     TO table-item(item-nbr)
             MOVE table-item(table-size)   TO table-item(swap-nbr)
             SET  record-swapped           TO TRUE
           END-IF
           .
       END PROGRAM tblookup.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  openimp.

      * Test the tblookup module

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT tabrc-file ASSIGN TO tabfile
             ACCESS MODE IS SEQUENTIAL
             ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  tabrc-file.
       01  tabrc-record                                    PIC X(1000).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  work-fields.
           05 sub1  OCCURS 2           VALUE 1      COMP   PIC S9(4).
           05 sub2                     VALUE ZERO   COMP   PIC S9(4).
       01  switches.
           05 count-overflow-sw        VALUE 'Y'           PIC X.
             88 count-overflow         VALUE 'Y'.
       01  fixed-values.
           05 codes
           VALUE '1QAZ2WSX3EDC4RFV5TGB6YHN7UJM8IK9OL0P'    PIC X(36).
           05 file-size                VALUE 0100   COMP-3 PIC S9(5).
           05 no-of-calls              VALUE 1000   COMP-3 PIC S9(5).
       01  table-record.
           05 table-key                VALUE SPACE         PIC XX.
           05 table-data.
             10                        VALUE 'REC# '       PIC X(5).
             10 table-rec-no           VALUE ZERO          PIC 9(5).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Main program logic
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Create the table file
           PERFORM 1000-create-file
      * CALL tblookup to set strategy
           CALL 'tblookup' USING BY CONTENT 'C'
                                            no-of-calls
                                            ' '
      * CALL tblookup to find last value in table
           CALL 'tblookup' USING BY CONTENT   'F'
                                              table-key
                                 BY REFERENCE table-data
      * CALL tblookup to find a value not in the table
           MOVE ALL ':'                    TO table-key
           CALL 'tblookup' USING BY CONTENT   'F'
                                              table-key
                                 BY REFERENCE table-data

           GOBACK
           .
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Create a test table file
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
       1000-create-file.
           OPEN OUTPUT tabrc-file

           PERFORM
             file-size TIMES
             PERFORM 1100-create-key
             ADD  1                        TO table-rec-no
             WRITE tabrc-record          FROM table-record
           END-PERFORM

           CLOSE tabrc-file
           .
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
      * Create a record key with a random but unique value
      *-----------------------------------------------------------------
       1100-create-key.
           SET  count-overflow             TO TRUE
           PERFORM
             VARYING sub2 FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL sub2 > LENGTH OF table-key
             IF count-overflow
               ADD 1                       TO sub1(sub2)
               IF sub1(sub2) <= LENGTH OF codes
                 INITIALIZE count-overflow-sw
               ELSE
                 MOVE 1                    TO sub1(sub2)
               END-IF
             END-IF
             MOVE codes(sub1(sub2):1)      TO table-key(sub2:1)
           END-PERFORM
           .
       END PROGRAM openimp.
```

## Thread: OT: "Humor" — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: "Humor"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k3f95$t9k$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
(In case you haven't seen this before)


Not so long ago...
Not so long ago...
 -------------------

An application was for employment
A program was a TV show
A cursor used profanity
A keyboard was a piano!
Memory was something that you lost with age
A CD was a bank account
Compress was something you did to garbage not something you did to a file.
And if you unzipped anything in public, you'd be in jail for awhile!
Log on was adding wood to a fire
Hard drive was a long trip on the road
A mouse pad was where a mouse lived
And a backup happened to your commode!
Cut - you did with a pocket knife
Paste you did with glue
A web was a spider's home
And a virus was the flu!

I guess I'll stick to my pad and paper and the memory in my head
I hear nobody's been killed in a computer crash but when it happens they wish
they were dead!
```

## Thread: retail & 9999 — 3 msgs

### retail & 9999

- **From:** Joshua Gibson <gibsonjb@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3963F4AD.6684775A@earthlink.net>`

```
 I heard of a store manager telling cashiers not to type 9999 into the
cash register because it could cause havoc for the system. Is this Y2K
related?
```

#### ↳ Re: retail & 9999

- **From:** "The Riddler" <riddler_cubed@nospam.yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k1qde$4f7$1@news.cis.ohio-state.edu>`
- **References:** `<3963F4AD.6684775A@earthlink.net>`

```
Maybe, depending on if part of that entry is seen as the year '99'. I've
seen some systems that rely on a numeric value of all 9's to signify
something special, like end of input, which could cause some havoc that is
not Y2K related.

It's been a while for me, but with MVS if you cataloged a tape and didn't
want it to expire you gave it a special expiration date which contained some
9's, if not all (I don't quite remember). Does anyone know if this caused
any kind of conflicts as last year wound down?

"Joshua Gibson" <gibsonjb@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:3963F4AD.6684775A@earthlink.net...
> I heard of a store manager telling cashiers not to type 9999 into the
> cash register because it could cause havoc for the system. Is this Y2K
> related?
>
```

<a id="t-35f66b8898"></a>
## Thread: ASCII Translation Tool — 14 msgs

### ASCII Translation Tool

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000707175634.19907.00000595@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

When we sell our system to a new customer they will often provide us with an
ASCII file containing their student information.  However, usually every school
gives us a different format so I an allways writing translation programs.  What
I would like to do is have one generic ASCII translation program that I could
use.

What I am looking for is an ASCII translation tool that our support people (non
programmers) could use that would take all these different ASCII file formats
and translate them into the one generic ASCII format that is supported by my
COBOL program.  They could use this tool to specify what the incomming ASCII
file looks like and then they would output to my ASCII format.  This tool would
have to be able to run from a command line (i.e. TRANSLATE UNIQUE.ASC TO
GENERIC.ASC).

Is anyone aware of a tool like this ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@leif.org>
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Sit95.11358$09.150853@typhoon.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<20000707175634.19907.00000595@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```

What

could

>

(non
> programmers) could use that would take all these different ASCII file
formats

since you have not specified the format (and cannot because there
```

#### ↳ Re: ASCII Translation Tool

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6nE95.73$HD.16073@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<20000707175634.19907.00000595@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Bob7536 <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000707175634.19907.00000595@ng-bg1.aol.com...
> Good Morning,
>
> When we sell our system to a new customer they will often provide us with
an
> ASCII file containing their student information.  However, usually every
school
> gives us a different format so I an allways writing translation programs.
What
> I would like to do is have one generic ASCII translation program that I
could
> use.
>
> What I am looking for is an ASCII translation tool that our support people
(non
> programmers) could use that would take all these different ASCII file
formats
> and translate them into the one generic ASCII format that is supported by
my
> COBOL program.


What you are looking for is a generic mapping tool. Mercator
(www.mercator.com) is a hell of a good product, which would certainly handle
your needs. However, it's not inexpensive (3K-5K single user depending on
options) , and regardless of what the sales folks tell you, it is NOT an
'end-user' tool. (Well, end-users might be able to handle the mapping, but
the data definition - the 'type tree'  development -  is not for amateurs).

(SHAMELESS PITCH ALERT) However, there are any number of very qualified
Mercator consultants available, especially if you are in the Racine, WI
area.
```

#### ↳ Re: ASCII Translation Tool

- **From:** "Floyd Johnson" <floydj@netins.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k7hrd$j5b$1@ins21.netins.net>`
- **References:** `<20000707175634.19907.00000595@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Bob:

One way I have handled this problem in the past is to use MS Excel and MS
Access.  MS Excel has an import feature that lets you manually define the
input file.  The imported file is then stored as a spreadsheet.  The spread
sheet then can be imported into MS Access - from which you can create a
query/report that meets your new specs and saved as a report that can be
read by your COBOL program.

Other possible variations - not yet tried, but in theory possible:

1.  OCDB interface to connect directly to the excel spreadsheet
2.  You may be able to import the ascii file directly into MS Access -
bypassing the first step
3.  It seems to me that Fujitsu included a file manipulation tool in their
free compiler that allowed this kind of job to be accomplished.  I don't
know if it is still available in their latest versions (free or commercial).
4.  It may be possible to use VB Script to automate anyone of these methods

These last four are just brainstorming - so try at your own risk.

Floyd Johnson

"Bob7536" <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20000707175634.19907.00000595@ng-bg1.aol.com...
> Good Morning,
>
> When we sell our system to a new customer they will often provide us with
an
> ASCII file containing their student information.  However, usually every
school
> gives us a different format so I an allways writing translation programs.
What

could
> use.
>
> What I am looking for is an ASCII translation tool that our support people
(non
> programmers) could use that would take all these different ASCII file
formats
> and translate them into the one generic ASCII format that is supported by
my
> COBOL program.  They could use this tool to specify what the incomming
ASCII
> file looks like and then they would output to my ASCII format.  This tool
would
> have to be able to run from a command line (i.e. TRANSLATE UNIQUE.ASC TO
> GENERIC.ASC).
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: ASCII Translation Tool

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@netins.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<396752FB.8FE5C445@netins.net>`
- **References:** `<20000707175634.19907.00000595@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Bob,

One solution that I have used for a similar problem is to import the data into one
or more database tables.  I use MS Access because it (at the time) was the only
database product available to me.  Access has a rather straight forward "import"
wizard that simplifies this process.  Once the data is imported, there are at least
two methods to get access -

    - use ODBC (from the language of your choice) to access your data
    - create a query or report that can be read by your COBOL program

It might be possible to automate this process using VB Script - involving a minimal
amount of programming in order to define the input and output fields.

You might also look at the Fujitsu COBOL product.  Though it has been a while since
I looked at the full product, it seems to me that they use to include a file tool
that could do what you are asking.

Neither of these are, as provided, simple user processes.  But with some scripting
and assuming the files are clean (they always are, aren't they), it may be possible
to make it so.

Good luck,
Floyd

Bob7536 wrote:

> Good Morning,
>

> Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: ASCII Translation Tool

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<396893DF.1269@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<20000707175634.19907.00000595@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Bob7536 wrote:
> 
> Good Morning,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is anyone aware of a tool like this ?

Our ParseRat (http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm) is designed to satisfy JUST this 
sort of need.  You would set up a "preference file" to handle each of the different 
input formats and your support people would then just open the input file, select the 
preference file for the particular input format (long file names can be a blessing 
here!) and then output the results - either to a new file or appending to an existing 
one.

Since it can take command line parameters 
(e.g. PARSERAT   <INPUTFILENAME> <PREFERENCEFILENAME> <OUTPUTFILENAME>)
I have my most frequent conversions set up in batch files.

It won't cost anything to try it, you get a 30 day free trial.
```

```
until it looked like the new one was working. Certainly the usage logs showed lots of 
hits on the new location. Possibly the DNS entry hadn't got to your server yet when you 
tried.  I hope it's OK now.

The link should be http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm (which works from the ISPs I 
tested it from).
```

```
Hi:

I have a program which allows you to specify the leftmost column of
up to 12 fields in an ASCII file. It writes a record in a common
format. This would do what you want but would require a teeny bit of
fiddling to get your format in etc. Written in MF COBOL.

You are welcome to a copy. If you want one or have questions, just
email me or call 800-971-3663

Thanks

TOny Dilworth


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: ASCII Translation Tool

- **From:** Scott Mattes <smattes@erols.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3969CAD4.5FB3DD87@erols.com>`
- **References:** `<20000707175634.19907.00000595@ng-bg1.aol.com>`

```
Wouldn't the ultimate tool be one that lets the user see if the data fits the
specified input pattern, and if if doesn't, to visually change the input pattern to
suit the data?

Seeing what the output will be is a must.

Input patterns should be from flat files, new patterns could be written to flat
files. These patterns should easily be called up if the current one is not
suitable.

I love writing these type of programs.
Bob7536 wrote:

> Good Morning,
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bob Hennessey
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ASCII Translation Tool

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3969FCC6.7AC5CBB0@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<20000707175634.19907.00000595@ng-bg1.aol.com> <3969CAD4.5FB3DD87@erols.com> <3969F141.56CC@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`

```
Scott Mattes wrote:
>
> Wouldn't the ultimate tool be one that lets the user see if the data fits the
> specified input pattern, and if if doesn't, to visually change the input pattern to
> suit the data?

One would want it to recognize various patterns from libraries,
including patterns for spreadsheets, PC databases, mainframe databases
(data dictionary), metadata, ASCII & EBCDIC data, binary, packed,
floating point, trailing sign, variable length, embedded keys, pointers,
etc.  It could be very useful - occasionally.
```

<a id="t-a5a65bc59a"></a>
## Thread: A rather odd question — 7 msgs

### A rather odd question

- **From:** "Tom Wright" <twright@larimore.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nIq95.17$g62.245183@news1.i1.net>`

```
I would like to write a cobol program that will open any of our databases
and get info from them.  Since each database is different in size I don't
want to write FD and Select statments for all 300 databases.  This cobol
program will be called by a VB program.  Can I enter file names and select
statments in on the fly in MF cobol 4.032?

Does anyone have any other suggestions short of going with a third party
type middleware.....I'm trying to avoid the middleware thing.

Thanks for any info

Tom Wright
twright@larimore.net
```

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0S22e2AoLlZ5Iw6s@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<nIq95.17$g62.245183@news1.i1.net>`

```

<twright@larimore.net> writes
>program will be called by a VB program.  Can I enter file names and
>select
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi Tom,

I think you need to look into the 'EXTFH' routines in MF-COBOL. No doubt 
someone with more experience of that product line will be able to offer 
more detailed assistance.....   :^)

Cheers

Nigel
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A rather odd question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k5kfs$qfr$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<nIq95.17$g62.245183@news1.i1.net> <0S22e2AoLlZ5Iw6s@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`

```
You might also want to look at the FCD-- compiler directives.  I believe that
these will give you "access" to the file control blocks - that you could
"modify" or build on the fly.  I don't really recommend this, but it might
give you what you want.
```

#### ↳ Re: A rather odd question

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5nE95.72$HD.16281@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<nIq95.17$g62.245183@news1.i1.net>`

```
Tom Wright <twright@larimore.net> wrote in message
news:nIq95.17$g62.245183@news1.i1.net...
> I would like to write a cobol program that will open any of our databases
> and get info from them.  Since each database is different in size I don't
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Why not open the files from the VB program? If there are ODBC drivers for
the databases, the SQL is portable; and, you can use ODBC calls to get the
available table names, descriptions, etc.

If a third-party DLL is not total anathema to you, there is a pretty good
product for simplified ODBC access available from Perfect Sync at
www.perfectsync.com to eliminate much of the 'low-level' ODBC calls.


MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: A rather odd question

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kcbgl$pec$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<nIq95.17$g62.245183@news1.i1.net>`

```

Tom Wright wrote in message ...
>I would like to write a cobol program that will open any of our databases
>and get info from them.  Since each database is different in size I don't
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks for any info

Hi Tom,

It is possible to use the same SELECT statement to open multiple files,
we have an application which does this. One can access the 'file control
description' and save that away, then reuse the same SELECT statement.
The FCDREG directive sets up a special data item FH--FCD for each
file, and one can use this to access the FCD.

However this only works if you want to open lots of files which have the
same style SELECT statement. So if all your index files have the same
structure, then this is okay. However if they are all different, then
this won't work. Well you might be able to get it to work, but you
will have to store key definition blocks away somewhere as well.

Here is a skeleton program I hope you can make use of:

      $set FCDREG
123456 select db-file assign to db-file-name
           file status is file-status.
       data division.
       file section.
       fd db-file.
       01 db-rec pic x(80).
       working-storage section.

       01 file-status.
          03 db-stat-1  pic 9(2) comp-x.
          03 db-stat-2  pic 9(2) comp-x.

       01 ws-fcd occurs 300.
           *> should be in %COBDIR%\sources
           copy "xfhfcd.cpy".

       01 file-number pic x comp-x.
       procedure division.

           move "filename-1" to db-file-name
           move 1 to file-number
           perform open-file

           move "filename-2" to db-file-name
           move 2 to file-number
           perform open-file

           *> rest of files here

           move 2 to file-number
           perform access-file
           *> file i-o here
           perform save-file

           *> next file

           *> close all files
           perform close-all-files
           stop run
           .

123456 open-file section.
           open i-o db-file
           move fh--fcd of db-file to ws-fcd(file-number)
          *> fast close , posn 8 = fcd-open-mode
          *> needed so can use open statement again
           move x"80" to fh--fcd of db-file(8:1)
           .

       save-file section.
           move fh--fcd of db-file to ws-fcd(file-number)
           .

       access-file.
           move ws-fcd(file-number) to fh--fcd of db-file
           .

       close-all-files section.
           move 0 to file-number
           perform 300 times
               add 1 to file-number
               move ws-fcd(file-number) to fh--fcd of db-file
               close db-file
           end-perform
           .



Regards,
Steven Lalewicz.

>
>Tom Wright
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A rather odd question

- **From:** Scott Williamson <syw@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rXR2OCAWBia5EwIm@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<nIq95.17$g62.245183@news1.i1.net> <8kcbgl$pec$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
>Tom Wright wrote in message ...
>>I would like to write a cobol program that will open any of our databases
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Some years ago I wrote a standard file access routine that used either a
data dictionary or copybook files to access COBOL datafiles.  The basic
idea was that the setup program generated and compiled a skeleton file
access program for a specific file, and this was then called by the main
(generic) program.  I used it for ad-hoc reporting and problem-solving.

I've still got the code somewhere. if anyone's interested.  It was
written in RM-COBOL, but should be portable to most other compilers.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<smboh1g1nu7@corp.supernews.com>`

```

FILE-STATUS 46 error,  Fujitsu's runtime message pops up.

I currently have @NoMessage=YES in the runtime environment file.

Thanks in advance.

Joe Hunter
pcs@usaor.net
```

```
>How do I suppress the runtime messages in Fujitsu 5.0.  If I received a
>FILE-STATUS 46 error,  Fujitsu's runtime message pops up.
>
>I currently have @NoMessage=YES in the runtime environment file.
>

@NoMessage=YES works for me.  (Reminds me of the error message: A file
which does not exist was found.).

What I HAVE found is that this suppression does NOT exist if you are
debugging.  I'll have to check further to make sure it really is
working for me, but I'm 99.9% sure it is.
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

## Thread: Programmer / Analyst with ETI expertise wanted immediately — 1 msgs

### Programmer / Analyst with ETI expertise wanted immediately

- **From:** "OT & T Information Systems, Inc." <cindy@ottinf.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bc.unix,ca.jobs,can.jobs,comp.lang.cobol,dc.jobs
- **Message-ID:** `<zhma5.26528$qS3.56183@tor-nn1.netcom.ca>`

```
Job Title: ..................... Programmer / Analyst
Term: ......................... 8 months +
Location: ................... AB, Canada
Salary: ....................... Up to $80/hr
Reply to: .................... cindy@ottinf.com

Responsibilities:
- Design, code and implement software solutions for new processes and data
conversion of a data warehouse project, the technical specifications of
these solutions will be developed by the IS and business project teams
- Deliver quality applications in a dynamic and fast paced environment
- Prepare systems documentations
- Construct and unit test systems deliverables
- Execute integrated test plan, support user testing, implementation and
post-implementation activities
- Maintain version control of software developed
- Consult with respect to the best implementation of software modules and
application.

Requirements:
- College diploma or University degree in Computer Science or equivalent;
- Minimum of 2 years of database application development experience;
- Most important: Experience with ETI*EXTRACT Data Extract/Transform/Load
tool on a data warehouse environment
- Data conversion experience;
- Experience with Relational Database System, in particular DB2 UDB on a
RS6000 AIX platform would be an asset;
- Application development experience on a mainframe MVS and/or UNIX
environment using SQL;
- Knowledge of and/or experience in COBOL, Java, JDBC, Visual Basic, or HTML
would be an asset;
- Working knowledge of Windows NT/2000 and Microsoft Office Suite;
- An understanding of data warehousing concepts would be a definite asset;
- Excellent communication and interpersonal skills;
- Ability to work effectively independently as well as within a team;
- Ability to work within project timelines.

OT & T Information Systems, Inc.
```

#### ↳ cobol opportunity

- **From:** tsquare21@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k8i1h$187$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<NEBBJMLEOKPHICMJKDDPAEINCAAA.zavdan@tin.it>`

```


	I give up so I am turning to the COBOL community to help me in
this endeavor IO am working on.  Need I mentioned that there will be a
monetary reward for the best coders.

	 Here is the problem that I have been working with to no avail.
A simple alphanumeric to numeric conversion.  Before you say not again
I have tried all of previous suggestions.  From using reference to
redefines.  They all produced the same results, non numeric moves to a
numeric data.  when I use a reference to fill each byte of my numeric
feild with a alpha number (after moving zeroes to field) the numeric
field changes to a non numeric field.

	So here is the problem that I will pay to have solved.  It is
in two parts each is worth $100 dollars.  Would like to see them in one
program but that is not necessary.

	Program will include.
	1) working module with source and PC .exe
	2) sample files if used
	3) works on my data (will e-mail you sample on request).
	4) Be available for additional coding
	5) using Micro focus personale cobol 2.0.02 (some compatibility
required)
	6) all source and EXE submitted becomes my property

	The first part of the program will read in a variable length
record into a working storage area. The records are delimited by return
new line character (will have to processed probably).  this is a sample
of the record, Because of the new line characters this sample may not
work with the real data, so request sample data for your testing.

AADC,000530,1.500,1.500,1.437,1.437,2430000,0
aAMC,000530,0,0.875,0.562,0.750,0,0
AAMCL,000530,0,0.156,0.156,0.156,0,0
AAMCZ,000530,0,0.031,0.031,0.031,0,0
AANP,000530,0,0.281,0.187,0.210,0,0
AAPCE,000530,0.531,0.531,0.531,0.531,50000,0
AARO,000530,1.000,1.000,0.875,0.990,740000,0
AASR,000530,1.625,1.625,1.625,1.625,20000,0
AASRW,000530,0.040,0.040,0.040,0.040,40000,0
ABACQ,000530,0.018,0.018,0.015,0.018,2250000,0
ABCC,000530,0.190,0.190,0.190,0.190,600000,0
ABCP,000530,0.730,0.750,0.720,0.730,3030000,0
ABCU,000530,0,1.625,1.406,1.406,0,0
ABFC,000530,0,1.250,1.250,1.250,0,0
ABHH,000530,1.812,2.000,1.812,2.000,2460000,0
ABID,000530,0.450,0.450,0.380,0.387,19000000,0

	You will notice that all price have a 3 decimal precision so
that may make your job easier.  for your naming convince the relative
fields are

Name,date,open,hi,low,close,volumn,tics

Using these names in some form is required. Another plus is some
comments in a few areas (no big deal can read code just fine).

	Another coding form I must insist is the use of a numeric
procedure naming, for example

	perform 1000-start
	perform 9000-open-files
	perform 1-etc.
	stop run.

	1000-start
	9000-open-files.
	2000-process-main
	8000-close-files

etc. Your own number method should be fine.



	The second part is to transfer the ASCII data file into a cobol
recognized file. The numeric fields will be opened for computations
purposes in another module  The date field may be used to process days
between to dates.

	That is it, any question or for sample data  e-mail me at.
T. Thomas
tlthomas@nni.com

Thanks for any help.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: cobol opportunity

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Charles Hammond" <eggbtr@ezl.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kbrt2$401$1@news.ezl.com>`
- **References:** `<NEBBJMLEOKPHICMJKDDPAEINCAAA.zavdan@tin.it> <8k8i1h$187$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8k8rqh$1uadr$1@ID-39920.news.cis.dfn.de> <gE4a5.2623$B92.6725@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Todd sometimes some coders use Low-values to fill a field which will not be
displayed, especially in CICS I had this problem with dates which are
normally described as alpha numeric.  I would describe the field as alpha
numeric and have a redefine as numeric.
Use the redefines to test for a numeric condition
If <var> <nemeric one> is numeric move to <numeric field> else move zero to
numeric field.

As a warning you may be encountering incompatable data or data that is
corrupt somehow.  You could actually move the key and the values in question
to a table and print them out as alphanumeric and skip over those records.
Then you could evaluate what exactly is in those fields.  Possibly somehow
someone is entering data that does not make sense. There is a lot to be said
to inspect what exactly is in the field.

Also sometimes data may appear to be alphanumeric in a key or other field
which is actually packed data described as numeric.  Programmers sometimes
use this technique to use the smallest possible amount of space.  For
instance I have seen a SSN that is packed described as an alphanumeric.  It
would occupy 5 positions in an alphanumeric field but actually be a 9 digit
numeric SSN.
To convert you have to redefine as pic 9(09) comp-3, move the comp-3 to a
pic 9(09) normal numeric field.  SSN is normally displayed as alphanumeric
so we would redefine as alphanumeric.  Sometimes in the past we even turned
the ssn around so it would sort better as an alphanumeric field then packed
it and called in alphanumeric.

All of this is done to make the key as small as possible so you can search
through the key-fields as fast as possible.  I don't know if anyone else
does this anymore or not.  Storage costs may have gone down enough so that
it is not necessary.  I would set a trap in the program to either stop the
program and display the bad data, or to make a table of the bad data (with a
table size limit) to print out the data.  Often use counters to count the
number of bad data items so the programmer knows how many times bad data is
actually encountered and skip over it.  It all depends how many there are;
if less than say 100, you could just print out on the job control what is
actually in the field, with the record key, so you know where to go and
correct the data.

Charles--Good Luck.
```
