Hi T. Thomas,

This is a nicely specified Cobol task and an attractive price, but
remote projects involve first resolving a few simple trust issues.

An issue for remote programmers is how can they be sure the remote
project sponsor will pay them?  Remote programmers would like to be
paid up front to remove the risk.

An issue for remote project sponsors is how can they be sure the
remote programmer can do the work?  Remote project sponsors would like
to pay after the work is done to remove the risk.

A solution for both parties is to work through an escrow service like
HotDispatch.  An escrow service ensures that the sponsor can pay
before the project starts, and the programmer is paid when the sponsor
is satisfied with the completed project.

HotDispatch is a marketplace for programmer expertise where questions
often receive responses in less than an hour, and small programming
projects are often completed in less than 24 hours.   Prices can be
fixed, negotiable, or by bid.  As on auction services such as eBay,
participants who fail to meet their obligations risk their reputations
and their eligibility to participate.

HotDispatch includes an escrow service that takes care of the
accounting, so that you can pay for speedy solutions and tip for
heroic answers, without the overhead of getting physical addresses,
writing out a check, addressing an envelope, and finding a mailbox ---
all the things no one has time for during a time crunch.

  HotDispatch: the Market for Technical Expertise
  http://www.hotdispatch.com/home?aff=32819422
  http://www.hotdispatch.com/search-archives?CATEGORY=42&aff=32819422
  http://www.hotdispatch.com/IBM/home?aff=32819422

Cobol tasks can be categorized under Programming Languages.

(...to solve problems quickly, offer quick rewards in return...:)

-- jvwright

Well, that is too bad. I checked my system (W2K) and another computer here
(Win98), both had this file. The latter does not have Acucobol installed. It
is a system file, and the letters vc indicates that it is a part of the
runtime system for executables built with MS VC.

I tried to search the Microsoft web for the filename but nothing appeared. I
suggest you send Acucorp an email and ask them what to do.

Cheesle

## Thread: STRING help please — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### STRING help please

- **From:** "M S" <maz@websurf.fsnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8k5qr6$9bt$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi
I am using SCREEN SECTION to accept input to make the customer-address field
which is PIC X(60). It has got 4 lines:
  ie line one   maximum input pic x(20)
     line two   maximum input pic x(20)
     line three maximum input pic x(20)
     line four  maximum input pic x(20)
     ** Note total characters should not exceed 57 **(because of 3 DELIMITERS '#')

 The answer required is:- the ADDRESS FIELD should look like:

     line one#line two#line three#line four
     123456789012345678901234567890123456789012345678901234567890>>pic x(60)

 I tried very hard to get this answer but instead I get the following:

     line one            #line two           #line three         #line four
     123456789012345678901234567890123456789012345678901234567890>>pic x(60)

     ....line four truncated...delimiters # fixed on 21 st, 41st positions.

     I tried the following:

SCREEN SECTION...

      ...04  LINE 02 COLUMN 10     VALUE 'CUSTOMER ADDRESS'.
         04          COLUMN 30     PIC X(20) USING W-ADDRESS-A.
         04  LINE 03 COLUMN 30     PIC X(20) USING W-ADDRESS-B.
         04  LINE 04 COLUMN 30     PIC X(20) USING W-ADDRESS-C.
         04  LINE 05 COLUMN 30     PIC X(20) USING W-ADDRESS-D.

PROCEDURE DIVISION...
      ...   STRING W-ADDRESS-A  '#'
                   W-ADDRESS-B  '#'
                   W-ADDRESS-C  '#'
                   W-ADDRESS-D  DELIMITED BY SPACE (also tried DELIMITED BY SIZE)
            INTO CUSTOMER-ADDRESS 

which gives CUSTOMER-ADDRESS FIELD as:
           >>line one            #line two           #line three         <<

      
        I have got some idea, it is using pic x(20) for each field inputted
eventhough it is less than pic x(20),it uses the remaining spaces or tab.

        Please help me what am I doing wrong here? 
        compiler is fujitsu v3.

Regards
M S
```

#### ↳ Re: STRING help please

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39669D21.39D1738F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8k5qr6$9bt$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```


> M S wrote:
> 
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
> M S

I think :-

(A) the first question has to be why do you want it all in one Address
Field ? Assuming you are storing this in a file, then it would make
sense to store each line separately so that they can INDEPENDENTLY be
edited.

(B) Second, I'm no Einstein - but 4 x 20 doth not make 60. <G> You would
need to include ON OVERFLOW for eventuality the four address lines do
exceed your targeted 60 characters !

(C) If what you are trying to achieve is an 'address block' to CURRENTLY
print from the Screen data, then the STRING verb would be valid.

>          PIC X(20) USING W-ADDRESS-A.
>          PIC X(20) USING W-ADDRESS-B.
>          PIC X(20) USING W-ADDRESS-C.
>          PIC X(20) USING W-ADDRESS-D.

You really don't need those '#' markers. NOTE INITIALIZE FIRST for
safety :-

	initialize Customer-Address
	String  w-address-a 	delimited by "  " *> two spaces
		w-address-b	delimited by "  "	..
		w-address-c	delimited by "  "	..
		w-address-c	delimited by "  "	.. 
		into Customer-Address
	End-String

If you wanted the above as four lines for displaying/printing :-

	initialize Customer-Address
	String  w-address-a 	delimited by "  " *> two spaces
		x"odoa"		delimited by size
		w-address-b	delimited by "  "	..
		x"odoa"		delimited by size
		w-address-c	delimited by "  "	..
		x"odoa"		delimited by size
		w-address-c	delimited by "  "	.. 
		x"odoa"		delimited by size
		into Customer-Address
	End-String

And if you were into GUI displays, take the last example and before
'into Customer-Address' add :-

		x"00"		delimited by size

PS: Check on-line help for STRING syntax and use of hex values "od",
"oa" and "00".

Jimmy, Calgary, AB
```

<a id="t-5640dfef88"></a>
## Thread: Input pipe error in MF COBOL (HP-UX) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Input pipe error in MF COBOL (HP-UX)

- **From:** "Michael C. Kasten" <mck9@swbell.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39671A65.6FBED367@swbell.net>`

```
MF COBOL provides a way to read, not directly from a file, but from
another process via a Unix pipe.  This feature lets you slip in any
arbitrary preprocessing or massaging without writing an intermediate
file.  Within the COBOL runtime the pipe is opened via the standard
Unix function popen().

Since many of our files are on the large side (tens or hundreds of
megabytes), we've been using input pipes to reduce the IO overhead.
However, we've encountered a problem.

When the process in the pipe encounters an error condition, it stops
and returns a non-zero return code.  However, the COBOL program
appears to be blissfully unaware of the error.  It just sees 
end-of-file.  

In our case there's no reliable way for the COBOL program to detect the 
problem by checking the contents of the input stream, e.g. by noticing 
the absence of a trailer record.

(Before you suggest that we start using trailer records or some
similar trick, let me say that such an approach would be far more 
trouble than it would be worth, due to peculiarities of the application.
We'd resort to a separate step first, with an intermediate file.)

My question is: can the COBOL program examine the return code of the
input pipe, or otherwise detect an error in the pipe?  I know that the 
pipe's return code is available to the COBOL runtime via the pclose()
function, but I don't know if it's available to the COBOL program
itself.  If it's available at all, it's probably available only 
after the CLOSE.

The same question (and probably the same answer) applies to output 
pipes as well, but so far those haven't been an issue for us.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

---

<a id="t-8e0f168747"></a>
## Thread: Warning! -- SONY SUBSTANDARD SERVICE — 1 msgs

### Warning! -- SONY SUBSTANDARD SERVICE

- **From:** An_American_Consumer@anflamvg.net
- **Date:** 2000-07-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<00E4B552D.qhakdwu@orrj.com>`

```
Anyone considering the purchase of a Sony peripheral for their computer
might want to give it some further thought. There clearly is not a
reciprocal relationship between what they sell and what they service. 
-
I purchased a CD-RW drive back in April that just recently went bad
(won't read). When I called Sony service, I was told that I could not
get a replacement and that I had to ship it across the country to be
"repaired" (and we all know what that means...) - with a three (3) week
turnaround. Assuming that they keep their 3-week commitment, with
shipping both ways, I'd be without the use of the unit for at least
five weeks!!! 
-
I hardly think that's reasonable or fair.
-
Compare this to HP, who under the same circumstances would simply ship
you out a replacement unit and issue a call-tag for the old one. 
-
Sony is a behemoth in the marketplace and as such, they have an
obligation to scale their service facilities to meet demand. To not do
so is an indication of their lack of commitment to customer satisfaction
and an unwillingness to stand behind their products.
-
Buyer be ware!
-
-
-
-
-
-
-
-
-

#### ↳ Re: Liant Relativity for Fujitsu Power Cobol for Windows

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-07-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39667f33.79904807@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<NEBBJMLEOKPHICMJKDDPEEINCAAA.zavdan@tin.it>`

```
From my own "usually reliable source" inside Fujitsu.

(Reworded by me)

Indeed the Fujitsu file system was licensed from Liant.  However,
since that time Liant has made changes that are not reflected in the
Fujitsu version, and Fujitsu has made their own changes as well.  For
SOME things they may be compatible but this is by no means guarenteed.


Transoft corp has built an ODBC driver for Fujitsu COBOL format files
that may be available or may still be in testing.  It is expensive.

Fujitsu suggests licensing Btrieve and using that file system if ODBC
access is required.

- **References:** `<394200e6.14715565@news.adelaide.on.net> <20000709141553.26361.00000008@ng-fk1.aol.com> <8lib5l$9rq$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <397CBF4A.D6AF273B@home.com>`

```


CobolScript is a scalable, cross-platform interpreted programming
language and development environment. CobolScript makes
internet and intranet development accessible and practical for
development teams that are either unfamiliar or dissatisfied with
other internet development alternatives.  When your CobolScript
environment includes a database and web server, CobolScript also
functions as middleware to connect your internet clients and web server
to your database.

Compatibility and Functionality information can be found at
http://www.cobolscript.com/csfaq3.htm.  Sample source code can be found
at http://www.cobolscript.com/samples.htm.  Since you probably don't do
web, wml, or hdml server side development, it is probably not for you.
Our customers that are running Red Hat 6.2 Linux are primarily focused
on web development using Apache.  If you are porting an old system, you
should contact a sales associate from Liant.  I'm sure they will be
able to help you out.

Enjoy your programming,

Matt Dean
dean@deskware.com

<a id="t-de1b48c71f"></a>
## Thread: Help in Finding Information — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help in Finding Information

- **From:** akent@seanet.com (Arthur M. Kent)
- **Date:** 2000-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net>`

```
I need some assistance with locating a "string" within a record.  Let me
first explain the "particulars".

I am using COBOL in an OS 390 environment.  I will need to read a
sequential file, each record of fixed length 340 bytes.  The "string" I am
looking for is located within bytes 206 through 250 inclusive, but the
beginning of the "string" is not always in the same location within the
bytes 206 through 250 inclusive.

The "string" I am attempting to find is 11 bytes long, beginning with the
letter T (capitalized, as shown).  Once I have located this first
occurrence of the T, I need to be able to "easily" verify that the 11th
byte of this string is also the letter T.  Finally, once I have this 11
byte "string" with the two Ts, I need to determine if the 7th byte, within
the 11 bytes, is a dash "-".  The 2nd through 10th bytes will either
always be numeric, or will be of the format 99999-999, where each 9, of
course, is a number.  Here are two examples of such "strings":

   T120956688T

   T13466-122T

I don't believe the INSPECT verb will help be locate the first occurrence
of the letter T.  Even if it did, how can I "easily" analyze the 11 bytes
for the conditions stated above?

I realize that I could move bytes 206 through 250 into a array of length
45 bytes, and then analyze each byte sequentially, but this seems rather
"ugly" and complex.  I am looking for a simpler solution.

Any advice/comments etc. would be appreciated.  If you wish to send your
comments via EMail, my EMail address is akent@seanet.com.

Thank You,
Arthur M. Kent
```

#### ↳ Re: Help in Finding Information

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2000-07-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bZ7a5.12474$HD6.350172@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net>`

```
In article <akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net>,
Arthur M. Kent <akent@seanet.com> wrote:
>I need some assistance with locating a "string" within a record.  Let me
>first explain the "particulars".
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>bytes 206 through 250 inclusive.

    SET STRING-NOT-FOUND TO TRUE.
    PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 206 BY 1
     UNTIL SUB1 > 250 OR STRING-FOUND
        IF WS-INREC(SUB1:1) = 'T'
            MOVE WS-INREC(SUB1:45) TO WS-PIC-X-45-FLD
            PERFORM DO-MORE-EDITS-N-TESTS THRU DMENT-EXIT
        END-IF
    END-PERFORM.

    IF STRING-FOUND
        (do some stuff)
    ELSE
        (do some other stuff)
    END-IF.

...
DO-MORE-EDITS-N-TESTS.

(I'll leave the rest of the coding to you... I set this up as a separate
paragraph rather than as a continuation of the inline PERFORM because over
time your edit requirements *will* change... easier to deal with in a
paragraph, I'd say)

    IF ALL-CONDITIONS-MET
        SET STRING-FOUND TO TRUE
    END-IF.

DMENT-EX.
    EXIT.    

[snippage]

>I realize that I could move bytes 206 through 250 into a array of length
>45 bytes, and then analyze each byte sequentially, but this seems rather
>"ugly" and complex.  I am looking for a simpler solution.

... to a complex problem?  What are you doing, bucking for Management?

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Help in Finding Information

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000710173511.01839.00002629@nso-cb.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net>`

```
In article <akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net>,
akent@seanet.com (Arthur M. Kent) writes:

>bytes 206 through 250 inclusive.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Have you tried something like :

77 const-offset-206 pic 9(04) comp value 206.
77 var-offset-206-firstt pic 9(04) comp.
77 var-offset-206-lastt pic 9(04) comp.
77 var-offset-206-dash pic 9(04) comp.

move zero to tally
inspect <your-record>(const-offset-206:45) tallying all characters before
initial 'T'
if tally < 45  *>we have a hit!!
  compute var-offset-206-firstt = const-offset-206 + tally + 1 *> should be ON
'T'
  compute var-offset-206-lastt = var-offset-206-firstt + 10       *> should be
ON 'T'
  compute var-offset-206-dash = var-offset-206-firstt + 6       *> should be ON
'-'
  if var-offst-206-lastt <= 250   *> we have a good field to work with
    if (<your-record>(var-offset-206-firstt:1) = 'T') and
       (<your-record>(var-offset-206-lastt:1) = 'T')  *> we have your validated
string
       if <your-record>(var-offset-206-dash:1) = '-'  
           *** do what you need for dash formatted number ***
       else
           *** do what you need for NO dash formatted number ***
         end-if
      end-if
   end-if

Hope this helps
```

#### ↳ Re: Help in Finding Information

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YG8a5.2749$HD.449159@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net>`

```
Arthur M. Kent <akent@seanet.com> wrote in message
news:akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net...
> I need some assistance with locating a "string" within a record...
> I don't believe the INSPECT verb will help be locate the first occurrence
> of the letter T.....

Well, with your other edit requirements, I think the indexed table approach
is probably the right one, but you can use INSPECT to find the first
occurence of a literal in a string...AND I think you'll want to refresh your
memory on reference modification, too......


* working storage for later use with reference modification
01 CHECK-AREA.
    05 FILLER            PIC X(01).
    05 CHAR-2-7        PIC X(06).
    05 CHAR8            PIC X(01)
    05 FILLER            PIC X(02).
    05  LAST-CHAR    PIC X(01).


 MOVE ZERO TO WHERE-IT-IS
 INSPECT THE-DATA-NAME TALLYING WHERE-IT-IS FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE INITIAL
'T'
 IF WHERE-IT-IS NOT EQUAL FUNCTION LENGTH(THE-DATA-NAME)
   ADD 1 TO WHERE-IT-IS
   DISPLAY 'FOUND T AT ' WHERE-IT IS
   MOVE THE-DATA-NAME (Where-it-is:11) to CHECK-AREA
   IF CHAR-2-7 IS NUMERIC AND CHAR8 = '-' AND LAST-CHAR EQUAL 'T' THEN
      DISPLAY 'WHOOPEE ITS A GOOD RECORD'
.....

MCM
```

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)

>
>The "string" I am attempting to find is 11 bytes long, beginning with the
>letter T (capitalized, as shown).  [...]

INSPECT record-area TALLYING counter FOR CHARACTERS BEFORE INITIAL "T"

...and then use the counter either as a subscript into a table or as a
pointer in an UNSTRING statement.
```

Although the program is proprietary and I can't send it to you, we have a
"file split" program here (which I helped write in assembler) that can
accomplish your task with the proper PARM.  I would simply use a parm of
((206/250#TNNNNNNNNNT).OR.(206/250#TNNNNN-NNNNT))

Rather a powerful utility program, isn't it?  Sorry I can't send it to you.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help in Finding Information

- **From:** jack <jacksleightNOjaSPAM@hotmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1a5d65fc.f556b529@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net> <8kkodm$iar@netnews1.apci.com>`

```
Arthur,

I like WDS's solution of an inspect/tallying followed by an
unstring/pointer/not on overflow.

Add 1 to the tally fld and use it in the unstring delimited
by 'T'. On not oflow you perform the paragraph that does your
numeric checks etc.

The advantages:

    >  If no 'T's or not the right len then numchk not performed
    >  You don't clutter up your pgm w/logic
    >  If the number between the Ts is too short it will be flag-
       ged as non-numeric in numchk

The disadvantages:
    >  I ca't wait to find out


-----------------------------------------------------------

Got questions?  Get answers over the phone at Keen.com.
Up to 100 minutes free!
http://www.keen.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help in Finding Information

- **From:** jack <jacksleightNOjaSPAM@hotmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<21552940.06c423a4@usw-ex0105-038.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net> <8kkodm$iar@netnews1.apci.com>`

```
Hi Arthur,

I like WDSs suggestion to use inspect/tallying followed by an
unstring/pointer/not on overflow. After the inspect add 1 to the
tally fld and use it as a start position to unstring into a work
fld using 'T' as a delimiter. Note that the wrk fld won't
contain the Ts as delimiters, but that's ok.

In the "not on oflow" phrase preform the paragraph that does the
numeric chk on the wrk fld.

The advantages of this approach:

    >  non T recs, flds > 11 pos are bypassed via oflow
    >  flds < 11 pos are rejected in numchk paragraph because
       unfilled positions are not numeric
    >  program is not cluttered up w/unnecessary logic

The disadvantages:

    >  Probably inefficient. If you have "biwions and biwions"
       of records this might be a problem.

Here's my cut at the numchk paragraph.

      evaluate true also true also true
          when pos-1-5-are-numeric
               also pos-6-is-numeric
               also po-7-9-are-numeric
               do numeric stuff
          when pos-1-5-are-numeric
               also pos-6-is-a-dash
               also po-7-9-are-numeric
               do dash stuff
          when other
               do ignore stuff
      end-evaluate


01  ws-wrk-fld.
    05  ws-1-5              pic 9(005).
        88  pos-1-5-are-numeric        value 0 thru 99999.

    05  ws-6                pic 9.
        88  pos-6-is-numeric           value 0 thru 9.
    05  redefines ws-6      pic x.
        88  pos6-is-a-dash             value '-'.

    05  ws-7-9              pic 9(003).
        88  pos-7-9-are-numeric        value 0 thru 999.

 Have fun, Jack.

Poster's note: None of this stuff was tested.



-----------------------------------------------------------

Got questions?  Get answers over the phone at Keen.com.
Up to 100 minutes free!
http://www.keen.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Help in Finding Information

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XQQb5.221$l73.68481@nnrp3.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net>`

```
Might work--

PERFORM VARYING J FROM 206 BY 1 UNTIL J > 249
    COMPUTE K = J + 11
    COMPUTE L = J + 1
    COMPUTE M = J + 6
    COMPUTE N = J + 7
    IF REC(J:1) = 'T'
       AND REC(K:1) = 'T'
       IF REC(L:9) NUMERIC
       OR
       (REC(L:5) NUMERIC AND REC(M:1) = '-' AND REC(N:3) NUMERIC)
       do something important
       END-IF
    END-IF
END-PERFORM.

- **Date:** 2000-07-15T00:00:00

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Gmtc5.1021$oV.81756@nnrp3.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net> <XQQb5.221$l73.68481@nnrp3.sbc.net>`

```
Better (without all the J,K,L,M stuff) (I forgot - or didn't know -
the starting position in a reference modifier can be an arithmetic
expression)

 PERFORM VARYING J FROM 206 BY 1 UNTIL J > 249
     IF REC(J:1) = 'T'
        AND REC(J + 11:1) = 'T'
        IF REC(J + 1:9) NUMERIC
        OR
        (REC(J + 1:5) NUMERIC AND REC(J + 6:1) = '-' AND REC(J + 7:3)
NUMERIC)
        do something important
        END-IF
     END-IF
 END-PERFORM.

#### ↳ Re: Help in Finding Information

- **From:** Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.13da9aa5880adf509896ce@news>`
- **References:** `<akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net>`

```
Arthur,

	My solution to this uses a small called module and pointers...  it 
also is amode 31 rmode any and data 31 with trunc(bin) compiler option to 
allow for handling of binary data as true binary without any truncation. 
this is important as the program redefines pointers and increments them.

source code as follows....   

       CBL TRUNC(BIN) LIST DATA(31)
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.    UTL0050.
      *AUTHOR.        CHUCK HAATVEDT.
      *INSTALLATION.  PUBLIC DOMAIN.
      *DATE WRITTEN.  JULY 2000.
      *DATE COMPILED.
      *REMARKS.
      *    PURPOSE AND OBJECTIVES.
      *    THIS PROGRAM WILL SCAN A 45 BYTE AREA FOR AN 11 BYTE STRING
      *    OF THE FORMAT T999999999T
      *               OR T99999-999T
      *
      *    WHERE THE 9'S REPRESENT ANY VALID NUMERIC CHARACTER.
      *
      *    1    07/00 INITIAL PROGRAMMING                 C. HAATVEDT
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       77  ABEND-CODE                      PIC S9(8)   COMP VALUE 501.

       01  PTR-POINTER-AREA.
           05  PTR-SCAN                    USAGE POINTER.
           05  PTR-SCAN-X  REDEFINES  PTR-SCAN
                                           PIC X(4).
           05  PTR-SCAN-BCNTR  REDEFINES  PTR-SCAN
                                           PIC 9(9)   COMP.
           05  PTR-END                    USAGE POINTER.
           05  PTR-END-X  REDEFINES  PTR-END
                                           PIC X(4).
           05  PTR-END-BCNTR  REDEFINES  PTR-END
                                           PIC 9(9)   COMP.
           05  PTR-MAX-ADDRESS             PIC X(4) VALUE X'7FFFFFD3'.

       01  BCNTR-BINARY-COUNTERS.
           05  BCNTR-OFFSET                PIC S9(9)   COMP.
           05  BCNTR-OFFSET-X  REDEFINES  BCNTR-OFFSET
                                           PIC X(4).
       LINKAGE SECTION.

       01  LP-LINKAGE-PARMS.
           05  LP-STRING-FOUND             PIC X.
               88  LP-STRING-FOUND-IS-Y                VALUE 'Y'.
               88  LP-STRING-FOUND-IS-N                VALUE 'N'.
           05  LP-STRING-OFFSET-INDEX      USAGE INDEX.
           05  LP-STRING-OFFSET-X  REDEFINES  LP-STRING-OFFSET-INDEX
                                           PIC X(4).
           05  LP-STRING-OFFSET-BCNTR
                           REDEFINES  LP-STRING-OFFSET-INDEX
                                           PIC S9(9)   COMP.

       01  LP-DATA-AREA                    PIC X(45).

       01  SA-SCAN-AREA.
           05  SA-BYTE-01                  PIC X.
               88  SA-BYTE-01-IS-T                     VALUE 'T'.
           05  SA-BYTE-02                  PIC X.
               88  SA-BYTE-02-IS-NUMERIC       VALUES ARE '0' THRU '9'.
           05  SA-BYTE-03                  PIC X.
               88  SA-BYTE-03-IS-NUMERIC       VALUES ARE '0' THRU '9'.
           05  SA-BYTE-04                  PIC X.
               88  SA-BYTE-04-IS-NUMERIC       VALUES ARE '0' THRU '9'.
           05  SA-BYTE-05                  PIC X.
               88  SA-BYTE-05-IS-NUMERIC       VALUES ARE '0' THRU '9'.
           05  SA-BYTE-06                  PIC X.
               88  SA-BYTE-06-IS-NUMERIC       VALUES ARE '0' THRU '9'.
           05  SA-BYTE-07                  PIC X.
               88  SA-BYTE-07-IS-NUMERIC       VALUES ARE '0' THRU '9'.
               88  SA-BYTE-07-IS-DASH          VALUE '-'.
           05  SA-BYTE-08                  PIC X.
               88  SA-BYTE-08-IS-NUMERIC       VALUES ARE '0' THRU '9'.
           05  SA-BYTE-09                  PIC X.
               88  SA-BYTE-09-IS-NUMERIC       VALUES ARE '0' THRU '9'.
           05  SA-BYTE-10                  PIC X.
               88  SA-BYTE-10-IS-NUMERIC       VALUES ARE '0' THRU '9'.
           05  SA-BYTE-11                  PIC X.
               88  SA-BYTE-11-IS-T                     VALUE 'T'.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING LP-LINKAGE-PARMS
                                LP-DATA-AREA.

           SET PTR-SCAN         TO ADDRESS OF LP-DATA-AREA.
           MOVE PTR-SCAN-X             TO PTR-END-X.
           ADD +34                     TO PTR-END-BCNTR.

           IF PTR-SCAN-X GREATER THAN PTR-MAX-ADDRESS
               DISPLAY ' UTL0050 ===> SCAN FIELD ADDRESS MAXIMUM '
                   'AMODE 31 ADDRESS'
               CALL 'ILBOABN0'  USING ABEND-CODE
           END-IF.

           SET ADDRESS OF SA-SCAN-AREA TO PTR-SCAN.
           MOVE ZERO                   TO BCNTR-OFFSET.

           PERFORM WITH TEST BEFORE
               UNTIL PTR-SCAN-X GREATER THAN PTR-END-X
                  OR (    SA-BYTE-01-IS-T
                      AND SA-BYTE-11-IS-T
                      AND SA-BYTE-02-IS-NUMERIC
                      AND SA-BYTE-03-IS-NUMERIC
                      AND SA-BYTE-04-IS-NUMERIC
                      AND SA-BYTE-05-IS-NUMERIC
                      AND SA-BYTE-06-IS-NUMERIC
                      AND SA-BYTE-08-IS-NUMERIC
                      AND SA-BYTE-09-IS-NUMERIC
                      AND SA-BYTE-10-IS-NUMERIC
                      AND (    SA-BYTE-07-IS-NUMERIC
                            OR SA-BYTE-07-IS-DASH  ) )
               ADD +1                  TO PTR-SCAN-BCNTR
               ADD +1                  TO BCNTR-OFFSET
               SET ADDRESS OF SA-SCAN-AREA TO PTR-SCAN
           END-PERFORM.

           IF PTR-SCAN-X GREATER THAN PTR-END-X
               MOVE 'N'                TO LP-STRING-FOUND
               MOVE ZERO               TO LP-STRING-OFFSET-BCNTR
           ELSE
               MOVE 'Y'                TO LP-STRING-FOUND
               MOVE BCNTR-OFFSET-X     TO LP-STRING-OFFSET-X
           END-IF.

           MOVE ZERO                   TO RETURN-CODE.
           GOBACK.
       END PROGRAM UTL0050.


====>>>>  warning this code is NOT tested and is provided with NO 
warranty or guaranty of any kind...  as with any code it should be 
thoroughly tested and documented before being used in a production 
environment...

[This follow up was posted to comp.lang.cobol and a copy was sent to the 
cited author.]

11th

this 11

byte, within

either

9, of

> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

occurrence
> > of the letter T.  Even if it did, how can I "easily" analyze the
11 bytes
> > for the conditions stated above?
> >
> > I realize that I could move bytes 206 through 250 into a array of
length
> > 45 bytes, and then analyze each byte sequentially, but this seems
rather
> > "ugly" and complex.  I am looking for a simpler solution.
> >
> > Any advice/comments etc. would be appreciated.  If you wish to
send your

#### ↳ Re: Help in Finding Information

- **From:** "George E. Zielinski" <georgezielinski@retired.airforce.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<07020FD09B59C864.7F8F4C1B2BB33F37.EF59AD8D3F6ED29F@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net>`

```
You might want to do something on this order.  You may need to modify it to
fit your naming conventions, feel free.

        FIND-SUBSTRING.

             PERFORM VARYING SUB1 FROM 206 TO 239 BY 1 UNTIL

SUB1 > 239
                  IF SEARCH-FIELD (SUB1:1) = "T" AND
                      SEARCH-FIELD (SUB1 + 10:1) = "T"
                       EVALUATE TRUE
                           WHEN SEARCH-FIELD (SUB1 + 7:1) IS NUMERIC
                                  PERFORM DO-SOMETHING-NUMERIC-FIELD
                           WHEN SEARCH-FIELD (SUB1 + 7:1) = "-"
                                  PERFORM DO-SOMETHING-DASH-FIELD
                            WHEN OTHER
                                 CONTINUE
                      END-EVALUATE
                     ADD 50 TO SUB1
              END-PERFORM.

        FIND-SUBSTRING-EXIT.


"Arthur M. Kent" <akent@seanet.com> wrote in message
news:akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net...
> I need some assistance with locating a "string" within a record.  Let me
> first explain the "particulars".
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> Arthur M. Kent

#### ↳ Re: Help in Finding Information

- **From:** jack <jacksleightNOjaSPAM@hotmail.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-07-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2adc2a83.0b014b9b@usw-ex0106-046.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<akent-0907001427110001@dialup-209.245.162.150.seattle1.level3.net>`

```
Hello again Arthur,

Just a side comment on your problem. If I'm not mistaken, if t
your target field is explicitly defined as alphanumeric(or
implicitly defined as such, e.g. FLD-A(2:3) ) a field of the
form nnC or nnD is considered numeric in addition to all
occurances of the field as X'FnFnFn'. In addition, if the
NUMPROC and NUMCLS have the right (or wrong) compiler settings
any occurance of A thru E in the low order position of the field
will result in a positive result of the class test IF NUMERIC.

Regards, Jack


-----------------------------------------------------------

Got questions?  Get answers over the phone at Keen.com.
Up to 100 minutes free!
http://www.keen.com
```

## Thread: COBOL 85 - compiler error — 18 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### COBOL 85 - compiler error

- **From:** "Dejan Zoric" <dejan.zoric@bj.tel.hr>
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<8kbum7$bgi$1@as102.tel.hr>`

```
ERROR:    COMPILER ERROR  82180 (66987395): Op code or data_type not valid
in this context.

Does anybody know what's this error about, or have some clue where to find
some information about this error? I checked all Unisys documentation and
didn't find anything useful. It appears that error is in this syntax:

082180        FIND FIRST KDS-N AT
082220        KDS-TEXT-A NOT < C-TEXT-A(C-ST) AND
082260        KDS-TEXT-A NOT > C-TEXT-B(C-ST)
```

- **Message-ID:** `<8kcd8u$99t$1@news.ezl.com>`
- **References:** `<8kbum7$bgi$1@as102.tel.hr>`

```

First of all, I am NOT a COBOL programer, this was just a problem in our
department. But, yes we know that FIND is COBOL verb and this is the right
way to use it : FIND FIRST dataset_name AT criteria... Right?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 85 - compiler error

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<keta5.2245$304.178386@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8kbum7$bgi$1@as102.tel.hr> <8kcd8u$99t$1@news.ezl.com> <8kcd35$q9k$1@as102.tel.hr>`

```
Dejan Zoric <dejan.zoric@bj.tel.hr> wrote in message
news:8kcd35$q9k$1@as102.tel.hr...
> First of all, I am NOT a COBOL programer, this was just a problem in our
> department. But, yes we know that FIND is COBOL verb and this is the right
> way to use it : FIND FIRST dataset_name AT criteria... Right?
>

No. FIND is not a standard COBOL verb. This must be a Unisys 'extension'.

It looks familiar to me, as I did some Unisys work in the mid-80's (when it
was Burroughs), but I guarantee that FIND is not ANSI 85 COBOL.

If no one here knows Unisys COBOL, your best bet will be to contact Unisys
or find a Unisys-specific newsgroup.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 85 - compiler error

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Donald Gregory <donald@gregpub.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00

Dejan,

I'm surprised no one has replied to this yet.

FIND is part of the DMSII extensions to ALGOL, COBOL 74, and COBOL 85.
It is used to look up a record in a DMSII dataset on an A-Series
system.

A "compiler error" is something that shouldn't happen, so the problem
definitely has to be UCF-ed to Unisys. 

Regards,

Don Gregory

(both system and datasets, datasets use EBCDIC), but we changed system
CCSver to Polish, so we can use Latin2EBCDIC. We, also recompile database
(with ccsver=Polish), and everything finished OK, but when we started to
compile COBOL sources, this is what happend. Maybe this has to do something
with the problem. I don't think that this has something to the system
CCSver, because we use Polish for a while, error occurs only after we set
ccsver on database. Is it really bug or we just forget some step in changing
ccsver (maybe some compiler option...)

Dejan

- **From:** Charles Hammond <ceh1@cec.wustl.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys

- **References:** `<8kbum7$bgi$1@as102.tel.hr> <8kck5m$kbe$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8kcnil$3j9$1@as102.tel.hr> <8kcj6h$8gd$1@ih292.ea.unisys.com>`

```


Sometimes I have seen the "in this context" phrase in a compiler error
message when the op code is good and the data is good, but they were being
used in a way that the compiler was not designed to be implemented.

Most books on compilers do not explain things all the way.  Maybe the find
verb wasnt designed to be used in that way or requires an index or
something like that.  It may be sql specific or be required to be compiled
differently.  For example, a CICS program is compiled differently that a
standard COBOL batch program.


Charles Hammond, BSIM Undergraduate Program

On Mon, 10 Jul 2000, Damian Thomas wrote:

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 85 - compiler error

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,comp.sys.unisys
- **Message-ID:** `<8kfehg$8es$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8kbum7$bgi$1@as102.tel.hr> <8kck5m$kbe$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8kcnil$3j9$1@as102.tel.hr> <8kcj6h$8gd$1@ih292.ea.unisys.com> <Pine.SOL.3.96.1000711085119.2698A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu>`

```

Charles Hammond <ceh1@cec.wustl.edu> wrote in message
news:Pine.SOL.3.96.1000711085119.2698A-100000@ritz.cec.wustl.edu...
>
>
> Sometimes I have seen the "in this context" phrase in a compiler error
> message when the op code is good and the data is good, but they were being
> used in a way that the compiler was not designed to be implemented. ...

That may be the case for other vendors, but I think I can state without too
much fear of contradiction that that is *not* the semantic intent of a
"compiler error" in Unisys A Series COBOL compilers.  If a programmer
attempts something that was recognized as beyond the scope of the
implementation, the syntax error would indicate something along the lines of
"feature not implemented" or "unrecognized construct".

The intent of the "compiler error" message is to inform the user that there
is a *problem with the compiler* and that he should contact his support
organization.   The A Series COBOL74 version of this message, in fact, reads
"A compiler error has occurred.  Please contact your support organization."

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL 85 - compiler error

```

Straying from the topic a bit here but wanted to mention a Univac extension
to COBOL I ran across in the 70s:  The system date was stored in an 11 byte
field formatted as MM/DD/YYDDD.  Moving the reserved word CURRENT-DATE to an
8 byte destination moved the leftmost 8 bytes while moving it to a 5 byte
destination moved the rightmost 5.  Ain't that cute?!  Never bothered trying
anything but 8 or 5 but suspect a compiler error would've resulted.

#### ↳ Re: COBOL 85 - compiler error

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000710173509.01839.00002627@nso-cb.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8kbum7$bgi$1@as102.tel.hr>`

```
In article <8kbum7$bgi$1@as102.tel.hr>, "Dejan Zoric" <dejan.zoric@bj.tel.hr>
writes:

>
>ERROR:    COMPILER ERROR  82180 (66987395): Op code or data_type not valid
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My first thought is to add some parenthetical groupings to clarify the
NOT/AND stuff to the compiler.  Without the paranthesis, it is anyones
guess as to how the compiler is interpretting your condition.

It looks as though you are attempting to exercise a database read/find
of the first item within a range of low(C-TEXT-A...) to high(C-TEXT-B...).
I have been away from UNISYS for a long time and even longer for database 
stuff, but I would guess that you might have to break this into a 'find on not
<'
and 'process until not >'.

Hope this helps.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL 85 - compiler error

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000710215258.19250.00003725@nso-fm.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8kbum7$bgi$1@as102.tel.hr>`

```
In article <8kbum7$bgi$1@as102.tel.hr>, "Dejan Zoric" <dejan.zoric@bj.tel.hr>
writes:

>
>082180        FIND FIRST KDS-N AT
>082220        KDS-TEXT-A NOT < C-TEXT-A(C-ST) AND
>082260        KDS-TEXT-A NOT > C-TEXT-B(C-ST)
>
Your syntax is correct by the reference manual that I have handy (circa 1985).

My preferred variation of your request would be something along the lines of 

       FIND  FIRST KDS-N AT
             (( KDS-TEXT-A >= C-TEXT-A(C-ST)) AND
              ( KDS-TEXT-A <= C-TEXT-B(C-ST)))

I prefer to re-word queries to avoid any NOT logic.
Parenthetical grouping helps to clarify my understanding of what 
each component of a compound condition is operating upon.

Hope that this helps you get past the compiler error you are experiencing.
```

the cmos in an attempt to get a faster loading system.  The cmos currently
used on most widnows 95 pc's is antiquated and written for antiquated

accepted convention.

new operating system.  It has it's plus and minuses.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: For Nerds and Geeks: The ultimate challenge 3990

- **From:** Lord Apollyon <spammers-make-great-compost-July2000@paypc.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.sys.mac.programmer.tools,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<spfb5.4$f44.1107@nsw.nnrp.telstra.net>`
- **References:** `<8kcq08$fr2$381@news.brutele.be> <8kdoji$lb7$1@news.ezl.com> <396B1D59.9C7DE43A@iinet.net.au>`

```
In article <396B1D59.9C7DE43A@iinet.net.au>, Joseph J Katnic 
<josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:

> I would be surprised if Linux made use of the CMOS.

I think he/you mean BIOS.  CMOS is a semiconductor process.

> Most modern OSes, simply use it for the BOOT phase, thereafter, they
> provide their own considerably faster drivers.

Absolutely.  Windows NT, Solaris, *BSD, and Linux don't use a single 
scrap of BIOS code AT ALL post-boot (with the possible exception of 
Power Management in Linux's case).

In fact, the BIOS is only used to get the first stage loader into the 
system and for that loader to read enough sectors to load a kernel of 
some kind that uses its own routines from that point forward.

The time spent during that phase is less than a second.

=R=
```

## Thread: Microsoft.Net — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Microsoft.Net

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<396A45F6.3193D1B8@cusys.edu>`

```
I just read an announcement that Fujitsu will release a COBOL compiler
next year which will take advantage of the recently announced
Microsoft.Net framework's common runtime support for multiple
development languages, including COBOL, Visual Basic and C++.  

Fujitsu officials said "The compiler and its cross-language debugging
tools, in conjunction with the framework, will enable developers to more
easily build Web sites that incorporate existing COBOL applications".

I am not familiar with this framework, and am more interested in
integrating mainframe COBOL with web applications, but I am curious to
hear more about this.
```

- **From:** vcf@vintage.org

- **Message-ID:** `<144537.683.885598555207@vintage.org>`

```





37:45:14


                        Vintage Computer Festival 4.0
                    September 30 through October 1, 2000
                         San Jose Convention Center
                            San Jose, California


Mark you calendar!  The fourth annual Vintage Computer Festival is
scheduled for Saturday, September 30th though Sunday, October 1st at
the San Jose Convention Center in San Jose, California.

The Vintage Computer Festival is a celebration of computers and their
history.  The event features speakers, a vintage computer exhibition,
a vintage computer marketplace, and contests like The Nerd Trivia
Challenge.  We showcase all different types of computers for all
differents kinds of platforms in all different shapes and sizes.


REGISTER EARLY AND SAVE!

Since you're already a VCF fan, we'd like to extend an early-bird
registration offer to you.  Register by July 15th and pay only $15
per person (after July 15th the fee will jump to $20 per person).
Your registration includes complete event access, including speakers,
the exhibition and the marketplace.  And just like last year, the
first 32 registrants will receive a VCF t-shirt!  Act fast to secure
your early registration and a free VCF t-shirt.

We've made it easier than ever for you register online as we are
now accepting credit card payments.  Visit the VCF website for
complete details.


EXHIBIT YOUR VINTAGE COMPUTER

Vintage Computer Collectors: we want you!  Exhibit your favorite
computer in the Vintage Computer Exhibition.  Prizes will be
awarded in 12 different categories, as well as a Best of Show
prize which includes $50 cash!

For complete details on the VCF Exhibition, visit:

http://www.vintage.org/2000/exhibit.html

To see the exhibits from VCF 3.0 go to:

http://www.vintage.org/exhibit99.html


BUY/SELL/TRADE AT THE VINTAGE COMPUTER MARKETPLACE

Do you have some vintage computer items you'd like to sell?  Whether
you rent a booth or sell on consignment, the Vintage Computer
Marketplace is the premier venue for selling old computers and
related items.  If you would like to rent a booth or inquire about
consignment rates, please send e-mail to vendor@vintage.org for more
information or visit:

http://www.vintage.org/2000/vendor.html


CHECK OUT THE NEW SITE!

If you haven't visited the VCF website in a while, come on over and
have a look at the completely new design.  You'll find it easier to
navigate and easier to find the information you're looking for.
We've got plenty of good stuff to read and hundreds of new links to
explore vintage computing on the web.  We're constantly adding new
content.  Stop on by for a visit!


TELL A FRIEND! TELL A FRIEND! TELL A FRIEND!

We really hope to see you at VCF 4.0!  And remember, tell your
friends!!

                        Vintage Computer Festival 4.0
                   September 30th through October 1st, 2000
                          San Jose Convention Center
                             San Jose, California
                            http://www.vintage.org
```

Paddy Coleman

MERANT International.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Cobol Calling VB DLL

- **From:** Brad Stark <bradstark@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kd661$2re$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8kcla2$l7f$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8kcud8$rdp$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Thanks Paddy.  To the best of my knowledge, the Merant samples web site
only has examples of VB calling Micro Focus Cobol DLL's.  I was hoping
to find an example of Cobol Calling a VB DLL.  I've seen several
examples of Cobol Active Clients using OO, and I may go in that
direction.  Using Object Cobol is a lot more sophisticated (and
probably something I should learn) but is more difficult than using
DLL's.

I'm desperate.  I've been all over the web site, the demo's that come
with NetExpress, and the documentation.

I appreciate the reply!

Brad Stark


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Cobol Calling VB DLL

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kem1p$2pd$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8kcla2$l7f$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8kcud8$rdp$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <8kd661$2re$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Brad,

I am not a VB expert but I seem to remember one of my people
telling me that the DLLs VB creates are not "standard" DLL i.e.
they cannot be CALLed from COBOL/C and so on.

They are in fact OLE servers and have to be accessed via OLE
(or COM as I believe it is now called).  This will require you to
use OLE/COM domain support in Object COBOL in Net Express.

Paddy Coleman
Manger, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

Brad Stark <bradstark@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8kd661$2re$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Thanks Paddy.  To the best of my knowledge, the Merant samples web site
> only has examples of VB calling Micro Focus Cobol DLL's.  I was hoping
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: NetExpress Cobol Calling VB DLL

- **From:** "David Sands" <davs@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ken8h$2sm$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8kcla2$l7f$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8kcud8$rdp$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <8kd661$2re$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi Brad,

You need to create the VB DLL as a COM object as I don't think that VB can
create a "standard" DLL with entry points to call. You will need to use some
of the OO syntax in order to call this.

I have forwarded a sample of a Net Express COBOL module calling a VB COM
Object by email. I'll arrange for this to go on the Net Express samples site
but if anyone wants a copy in the short term then send me a note.

Regards
David.

news:8kd661$2re$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

> only has examples of VB calling Micro Focus Cobol DLL's.  I was hoping
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

                          MICHAEL TODD
          PO Box 2381, York, PA 17405
                         (717) 699-0660
                      mftodd@minspring.com
                                                                  
                                                                  
                                                                  
                
                                                                  
                                                                  
                                                                  
                
                                
        OBJECTIVE: COMPUTER PROGRAMMER/TECHNICAL SUPPORT
                            POSITION
                                
                  HIGHLIGHTS OF QUALIFICATIONS
       Strong knowledge of Micro Focus COBOL, DBASE III Plus, lotus
1,2,3 Release 5, Windows 3.1, WordPerfect 5.1 for DOS 6.1 for
Windows 3.1, UNIX System V, Informix 4GL.
       Skillful program writer in DOS 6.22, Micro Focus COBOL, UNIX
System V C and DBASE III Plus languages.
       Ability to troubleshot and problem solve.
       Flexible; seeking entry-level with upward mobility
abilities.
       Willing to relocate anywhere in the United States.
       Enjoy all facets of the computer field.

                 RELEVANT EXPERIENCE & TRAINING
Programming/Support Experience
     -Successful completion of an intensive computer programming
course with a strong emphasis
      on analysis and design areas.
     -Two completed years of college, working toward Computer
Programming degree

software package

graphing, and macro use.

and use of the entire word processing package.

software and using the network.

C Informix interfacing.
     -UNIX System V; concepts and commands, DOS, Dx 10 and Windows
95 operating systems.
     -UNIX System V Shells; uses of the command shell and C shell
programming.
     -Tested newly written programs for any bugs and documented
them.
     -Experience on Micro computers, mini-frames-TI 660 and NCR
3400.
Technical Support/Customer Service
     -Learned to administer hardware maintenance and repair on IBM
compatible PC's, moving and
                installation of IBM equipment.
     -Installed hardware, software and troubleshooting.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MERANT AnswerLab Down AGAIN!

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<396c8178.136139372@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<8kel4r$bb1$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <396B4425.DA7490FA@home.com> <8khb14$c9c$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
On Wed, 12 Jul 2000 08:26:44 +0100, "Paddy Coleman"
<paddy.coleman@merant.com> wrote:

>Folks,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>I am just interested...


Not a specific Merant reply .. but I hated to call into support.  We
always talked to George.  He's a nice enough guy and tries hard, but
the gauntlet you had to go through was just unreasonable.

Since I am no longer working for that company I don't call Merant any
longer, I think things have improved but there's a "problem" with
telephone support for anything.

It's TIME.  I don't have the TIME to make the telephone call.  Unless
I'm really really stuck and can't do anything else.  For my current
tools I use email support EXCLUSIVELY.  Here's why:

1.  I can attach examples.
2.  I can crank out an email between compiles, or write portions of it
in my "spare" time - I don't have to dedicate 100% of my attention to
it like I do a telephone call.
3.  When I get a reply, I can SAVE IT for later reference in an easy
electronic format.

That's why I prefer the electronic method over the voice method.
---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<396DB8ED.72F008A7@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8kel4r$bb1$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <396B4425.DA7490FA@home.com> <8khb14$c9c$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <396c8178.136139372@207.126.101.100>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 12 Jul 2000 08:26:44 +0100, "Paddy Coleman"
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's why I prefer the electronic method over the voice method.

I think that most people are computer phobic but once they embrace it,
like using news groups,  it actually becomes more comfortable and more
like a conversation.

The ability to work through your reply and send exact meaning actually
becomes important, then the preference is email.  Especially when you
want to save the reply for reference.  People get upset when
conversations are played to them.

Just as an example I had 6 'you' in the above sentence,  I got the
change to rip them out after I had written them, you don't get that in
a conversation.  It is out there and too late.

For support calls the ability to impart massive amounts of information
in a problem dump is far better than any phone call.  'No that hew
byte was Af not 8f'.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

and get stuck with "Press 1 if..., Press 2 if..." or some nice showbiz
music until they come on the line - it's my dime !

Don't give up - I think you are doing a great service getting involved
here.

Jimmy
```

<a id="t-112aedec86"></a>
## Thread: Windows 2000 and Terminal Server — 3 msgs

### Windows 2000 and Terminal Server

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kepir$354$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Folks,

Someone asked a question on the alt.cobol newsgroup about Windows 2000
and Terminal Server.  I am repeating the information here as I thought it
may
be of interest to other MERANT COBOL users.

MERANT has tested and supports Net Express 3.0 with Service Pack 1
on Windows 2000.  However, we do not support the use of Microsoft's
Terminal Server with this version of Net Express.

If you want to use Windows 2000 and/or Terminal Server then you should
upgrade to Net Express 3.1.

I hope the above is of help.

All the best.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.
```

#### ↳ Re: Windows 2000 and Terminal Server

- **From:** Fred Young <fred@perfectcircle.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<396E1C58.394E2639@perfectcircle.com>`
- **References:** `<8kepir$354$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```


Paddy Coleman wrote:

> MERANT has tested and supports Net Express 3.0 with Service Pack 1
> on Windows 2000.  However, we do not support the use of Microsoft's
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Thanks for the info Paddy.
We have been using Net Express 3.0 on Terminal Server using Citrix MetaFrame
in a production environment for about a year now.  The only problems we've
had were discrepancies between Merants 16 and 32 bit versions.

Yes, we still use the 16 bit compiler.  We have to, 32 bit code runs much
slower on Win98, as the 16 bit code runs much slower on NT.  I assume Win9x
"thunks" 32 bit code, while NT "wows" 16 bit code, causing the performance
discrepancy.  I thought Merant was a bit quick to sundown the 16 bit product
while we had a major operating system which is still uses 16 bit internals.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Windows 2000 and Terminal Server

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kmuom$14h$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8kepir$354$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <396E1C58.394E2639@perfectcircle.com>`

```
Fred,

I am glad to hear that you have been able to use NE 3.0 in your
TS environment without too much trouble.  As I said, we simply
have not tested with this version and therefore cannot guarntee it
will work without problem on TS/MF.

Your comments about 16/32 bit are spot on and tie in with
Microsoft's own recommendations on which operating system
a particular Customer should use.

Have a great weekend.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

Fred Young <fred@perfectcircle.com> wrote in message
news:396E1C58.394E2639@perfectcircle.com...

<a id="t-efb657a42d"></a>
## Thread: Programmer / Analyst with ETI expertise wanted immediately- up to $80/hr — 1 msgs

### Programmer / Analyst with ETI expertise wanted immediately- up to $80/hr

- **From:** "OT & T Information Systems, Inc." <cindy@ottinf.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** ab.jobs,bc.jobs,can.jobs,comp.lang.cobol,jobs
- **Message-ID:** `<mHOa5.27317$qS3.57729@tor-nn1.netcom.ca>`

```
Job Title: ..................... Programmer / Analyst
Term: ......................... 8 months +
Location: ................... AB, Canada
Salary: ....................... Open
Reply to: .................... cindy@ottinf.com

Responsibilities:
- Design, code and implement software solutions for new processes and data
conversion of a data warehouse project, the technical specifications of
these solutions will be developed by the IS and business project teams
- Deliver quality applications in a dynamic and fast paced environment
- Prepare systems documentations
- Construct and unit test systems deliverables
- Execute integrated test plan, support user testing, implementation and
post-implementation activities
- Maintain version control of software developed
- Consult with respect to the best implementation of software modules and
application.

Requirements:
- College diploma or University degree in Computer Science or equivalent;
- Minimum of 2 years of database application development experience;
- Most important: Experience with ETI*EXTRACT Data Extract/Transform/Load
tool on a data warehouse environment
- Data conversion experience;
- Experience with Relational Database System, in particular DB2 UDB on a
RS6000 AIX platform would be an asset;
- Application development experience on a mainframe MVS and/or UNIX
environment using SQL;
- Knowledge of and/or experience in COBOL, Java, JDBC, Visual Basic, or HTML
would be an asset;
- Working knowledge of Windows NT/2000 and Microsoft Office Suite;
- An understanding of data warehousing concepts would be a definite asset;
- Excellent communication and interpersonal skills;
- Ability to work effectively independently as well as within a team;
- Ability to work within project timelines.

OT & T Information Systems, Inc.
```

<a id="t-91dc8a3fc2"></a>
## Thread: Merant/Micro Focus boneheaded 'support?' — 51 this month / 83 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Merant/Micro Focus boneheaded 'support?'

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K11b5.1455$h67.547060@news2.mia>`

```
I just recently received and installed NetExpress 3.1 upgrade.  The
3.0 version had installed cleanly with no problems, so I (stupidly)
assumed 3.1 would do the same.  Duh!  I installed 3.1 over both my
desktop and notebook 3.0 installations before I tried to run it.
Wham!  It instantly crashes and burns identically on both machines.
The solid, hard crash on two totally different machines tells me
that there is a serious problem with NetExpress 3.1, or the CD-ROM
copy I was sent.  If any of my software ever trashes a client's
machine like this (it has never happened), I would drop everything
and make every effort to get them up *NOW*.  The response I received
from Merant was so stupidly unhelpful that I just had to post it
(with my irate response) here for everyone's amusement.  The email
messages are below, read from bottom up.  The subject line is:

   "Need Net Express response keys for my desktop and notebook"
====================================================================
It obviously escaped your notice, I assume because you didn't bother
to read my message, but I *DID NOT* send the message below to you
for technical assistance!  I NEED RESPONSE KEYS, SO YOUR COMPILER
THAT I HAVE PAID FOR WILL RUN AND I CAN MAKE A LIVING!!!  I only put
the technical stuff in there so you would know *WHY* I was asking
for two response keys!  I have already lost two days of work because
*YOUR PRODUCT* bombed my computer.  All I am asking of you is that
you let me get NetExpress 3.0 *BACK* running, until I can clear the
problem with tech support.  If this is too much to ask, then please
let me know, and I will let all my friends on comp.lang.cobol and
alt.cobol know what jerks you people are, and my hard earned money
will certainly go to another company, from now own.

Now, are you going to send me the response keys, that installing *YOUR*
buggy software trashed, or not?

P.S.- A copy of this message is being posted to the above newsgroups
      right now, as will your response, assuming you make one.
```

- **Date:** 2000-07-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ki8ht$u81$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<K11b5.1455$h67.547060@news2.mia>`

kenmullins

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-12T00:00:00

Ken,

I assume you are referring to the posting that I made the other day.  Alas
you have misrepresented its contents.  I will repeat what I wrote earlier
for the benefit of all.

Net Express 3.0 + SP1 is certified for use on Windows 2000.  However,
we do not support the use of Microsoft Terminal Server with this version.

Net Express 3.1 is certified for use on Windows 2000 and Terminal
Server.

I hope this is clear to everyone.  If you have any questions then please
drop me a note.

All the best.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

"Ken Mullins" <**Ken**Mullins**@**mindspring.com** remove **'s> wrote:
> what operating system do you use NE on?

Windows 98 Second Edition.  What blows my mind is that NE 3.0 loaded
and ran as slick as a whistle, no problems with installation at all.
NE 3.1 installed apparently okay, but when you run it, the splash
screen comes up, then it bites the dust.  I am very careful to only
install the minimum software I need on my production machines, so I
am very puzzled why it crashes so hard on two different machines. I
uninstalled 3.1 and installed it again with the same result.  I'm
thinking about installing 3.1 clean on a test machine that has never
had NE installed, to see if that makes a difference.

Has anybody else out there had similar problems with NE 3.1 upgrade
on Windows 98 Second Edition, or anything else?
```

I recently installed NE 3.1 on a win2000 machine...Seems ok...

Only problems so far are, it forced even my 3.0 installation to get another
security key...it changed the windows default for opening a .app file to NE
3.1, which is not what I want...

Could be you received a bad CD...If you go to MERANT websync, there are tons
of fixpacks for 3.1 already...You might try installing those before you
actually run it for the first time...

kenmullins

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merant/Micro Focus boneheaded 'support?'

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kk4ne$m7j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<K11b5.1455$h67.547060@news2.mia> <8ki8ht$u81$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <IX2b5.90$El.928@news2.mia> <8kift0$2v6$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Folks,

Now come on, we need to be fair with the postings here.  There are currently
five fixpacks on WebSync for Net Express 3.1.  The latest two were released
on the 12th July.

Now you can't have it both ways.  Some of you have complained that  MERANT
is not actively supporting its COBOL development tools anymore.  I can tell
you
that this is not true - we have just released Net Express 3.1, we are
nearing a
new release of Mainframe Express and new 64/32 bit versions of Server
Express
are also in development.

MERANT and Micro Focus before it has always had a policy of actively
supporting and fixing its products.  We carry out monthly WebSync runs and
build/release fixpacks when necessary.  Surely this is a positive thing?  It
shows
that MERANT is committed to the ongoing development of the tools you use?

It would probably be easier for MERANT to stop producing fixpacks and
save us money.  No doubt you would then complain that we are not fixing any
problems you find.

Which do you want?

All the best.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merant/Micro Focus boneheaded 'support?'

```
Paddy: FYI

Looking on websync this morning, I do see one problem...

There is an application server 'servicepack1' dated 6/14/00...This would be
the current distributable runtime for NE 3.1...Fixpacks for the compiler and
the runtime were posted on 7/12/00 (at least 1 of the 2 is a replacement for
a previous posted fixpack at the 3.1.00 level - see readme files)...

The application server 'servicepack1' has not been updated to reflect the
posted runtime fixpack dated 7/12/00. That means the runtime modules in the
current application server 'servicepack1' do not reflect the runtime 3.1.01
updates.

This should be brought to someone's attention...IMHO the application server
'servicepack' should be updated each time a runtime fixpack is posted...

kenmullins

Don't see anyone saying that the fixpacks are a bad thing...OBVIOUSLY, they
are not a bad thing...I would rather have a problem corrected before I
actually encounter it using the product...I just went to websync and see 10
different downloads (fixpacks/feature additions, etc) for NE 3.1. In fact
there were fixpacks there before I even received official notification that
3.1 was released...

My previous post was not negative towards fixpacks...Simply a suggestion to
try and help a fellow developer...Installing the downloads might replace any
module that was corrupt on his cd...

kenmullins

Yep.  I installed 3.1 on my 98 machine and lost the license.  We had a
programmer install it on his machine with NT 4.0 and lost the license.  He
uninstalled and reinstalled and the license came back.  Then after a couple
of weeks with lots of problems, he uninstalled 3.1 and reinstalled 3.0.  He
again lost his license.  Life is grand...

bill

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merant/Micro Focus boneheaded 'support?'

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Stephen Gennard <stephen.gennard@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39800BD6.728360A8@merant.com>`
- **References:** `<K11b5.1455$h67.547060@news2.mia> <8ki8ht$u81$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <IX2b5.90$El.928@news2.mia> <ytLd5.5855$Yy3.198697@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <KuYe5.2426$hR3.67788@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> I received an email from Answerline that the problem is known, they're
> working on a fix, and expect it for downloading in perhaps a week.
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."

Hi,

I have experienced something very similar to this can found it was due to having
a program running in memory that used it's own private version of msvcrt.dll.

In my case this was "Star Office", it started a program on startup (so51inst.exe
or something similar).  In this case I move "msvc*.dll" into a sub-dir of star office
and very thing still worked.

I found this by "killing off" any running program's one by one via windows
task manager and attempting to startup netexpress.  Once I found the offending
program, I then looked at what this program used... ie: does it have it's own
copy of msvcrt.dll/mfc42.dll etc...

Hope this helps...
```

"Stephen Gennard" <stephen.gennard@merant.com> wrote:
>
> I have experienced something very similar to this can found it was
> due to having a program running in memory that used it's own private
> version of msvcrt.dll.

In this case, there isn't anything else running (I tried this 'umpteen
times), and the only copies of msvcrt.dll there are out there are the
(very old) version for NetExpress, the versions loaded by VC++ 6, and
a couple used by PowerBasic, which was *definitely* not running.  I
tried loading NE 3.1 on a spare machine that did not have NE 3.0
installed, and it apparently worked fine.  However, that machine is
an old clunker I use for a server, and does not have all the stuff on
it that I need for development.
```

Actually, I was informed by AnswerLab folks that this DLL use to be in the
NE bin directory in error...When I installed 3.1 (not replacing 3.0) that is
one of the problems I had...Their setup deleted that DLL, even from the 3.0
bin directory...Thus all my setup processing for creating distribution CD's
failed because the DLL was no longer there...MF now says the DLL needs to be
in the windows directory...

kenmullins

to get 3.1 back running (probably as a result of your action), which
was what I was so worked up about.  I was dead in the water and losing
money by the minute.  Thanks again.  Trust me, I will never load a new
release on my production machine again, until I have checked it out.

I am very interested in a solution to the 3.1 problem of course, but
that is not as urgent.

For anyone who is interested, I am running Windows 98 Second Edition
on both the Dell PIII 550 desktop and WinBook PIII 650 notebook.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merant/Micro Focus boneheaded 'support?'

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<396F65C5.FF6D277A@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<K11b5.1455$h67.547060@news2.mia> <8kipf8$6u8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <6Ikb5.545$5l.1988@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> You're preaching to the choir now. :-)  I do make backups (I have a
> $1400 HP SureStore DAT), and back up my working files several times
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> for me to get a new key. :-(

Right off I'd say that your backup is corrupt, but you sound pretty sure
that it isn't. I rely on a backup storing an exact image of each file on
my hard disk, so the only reason a program won't run after a full backup,
reformat, and restore is because either the backup is corrupt, not all
files got backed up, or because of some security scheme in the program.
(Like recording specific file positions or disk IDs.)

I'd like to confirm this because we still use Workbench & I've been
lobbying for an upgrade to NE. If Merant's done something that prevents
NE from running after a full backup, reformat, and restore then I need to
change to lobbying for a different compiler. Totally unacceptable, if
that's what they've done.
```

that NE wouldn't run.  The message is "Your license has been corrupted
for one or more options and must be repaired."  Everything else worked
okay, with no other symptoms that I can detect.  I have only tried to

IBM ThinkPad 770e, a rather solid and reliable machine.

No I don't think it was a fluke. About 18 months ago, for whatever
reasons, and I suspect Windows made a blip, I got a corrupted Release
Key. Toronto tried to help me with a new key - no success.

It meant me laboriously removing NetExpress, particularly Registry
entries, which also contained a reference to Version 2. Then I had to
re-load V 3.0 from CD. Toronto were then able to step me through with a
new Release Key procedure.

all the latest fixpacks for V 3.0.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Merant/Micro Focus boneheaded 'support?'

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson2_@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<396D1CEB.3B4021A0@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<K11b5.1455$h67.547060@news2.mia> <8kipf8$6u8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Bill Hobbs wrote:
<SNIP>
> 
> Can you spell "backup"?  How about "restore"?
> 
<SNIP>

Yes I can.  However, Microsoft apparently can't.  You see, there
is a problem with backup & restore under Microsoft's Windows
systems where the REGISTRY is *NOT* restored in a case like this
(nor would you want it to be).  The problem is this, just because
I have crashed some software, does not mean that I wish the
entire registry to be restored so that I lose other information
that certain other programs store there (as a side effect of
their being run/used).

Therefore, restoring the registry will cause one to lose other
data.  This is a poor design/implementation for problems such as
this individual has just experienced.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merant/Micro Focus boneheaded 'support?'

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>

```


Steve Thompson wrote:
> 
> Bill Hobbs wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> this individual has just experienced.

I tend to do a full backup before I do installs.  This way I can restore
everything, including the registry.  If I forget, well then I might have
to go to a registry up to a week old.  

That's at home.  My workplace doesn't believe in making backups
available for the desktop.  In fact, the only workplace were I have
worked which HAD a tape backup for PCs did so because I said it was
necessary, and it mounted on the parallel port and was shared by
everybody - so it wasn't used very often.

If all those companies figure backing up is too expensive, and I am the
odd ball, maybe we are MEANT to suffer the consequences of bad software.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merant/Micro Focus boneheaded 'support?'

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2000-07-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<396F4846.3D072B91@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<K11b5.1455$h67.547060@news2.mia> <8kipf8$6u8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <396D1CEB.3B4021A0@neo.rr.com> <396DBD2E.1DBAA200@cusys.edu> <fMkb5.551$5l.2289@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> "Howard Brazee" wrote:
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> a full day shot, whether the install fails or not. :-)

I do all my installs 1st thing in the morning for 2 reasons. 1) it gives me
the rest of the day to retry/restore if things go wrong & 2) I do an
unattended backup/restore the night before. I use Veritas Backup Exec which
has the option of creating Emergency Restore disks-i.e. you boot from the
floppy & do a full restore so you don't need to reinstall Win98.

I also *try* to do all installs/upgrades first on a test machine. Doesn't
eliminate problems but it sure reduces them. Schedule your
upgrades/installations unless it's something that is needed right now. (I.e.
it's something you need for your current project.) Schedule them for periods
*between* projects. Never upgrade the rest of your machines until you've
confirmed that the first one you upgrade can be used for production again.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Merant/Micro Focus boneheaded 'support?'

- **From:** Bill Hobbs <hobbsb@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kkklo$g3u$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<K11b5.1455$h67.547060@news2.mia> <8kipf8$6u8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <396D1CEB.3B4021A0@neo.rr.com>`

```
Steve Thompson wrote:
> Bill Hobbs wrote:
> <SNIP>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> this individual has just experienced.

Do I understand this correctly for the original poster (Judson
McClendon):

	He may have tried Microsoft's backup solution (whatever that may
be) and found that it did not meet his needs.  He then terminated his
search for a backup solution.  Or he never considered backups as
necessary.  Whatever he did, he has no backups.

	His business depends on him being able to write, compile, and
link COBOL programs on either of two Wintel machines.  If he cannot do
this, his business stops.

	He appears to have been under the impression that Merant
software would never harm his livelihood.  (please note the use of the
past tense)

Is the above correct?  If not, please enlighten me.

My desktop Mac has three removable drives that I boot from.  I do
regular disk-to-disk low-level copies, then remove the original disks
and use the copies - I *know* the originals work, and shortly I'll know
if the copies are any good.  The internal drive is used for scratch
files and has a minimal OS for doing backups.

Awhile ago, I upgraded a program.  It required the OS to be upgraded.
That required other applications to be upgraded.  It took about ten days
to get everything working ... on that set of disks.  After making copies
of my last backup set, I could connect to the 'net to search for and ftp
the needed upgrades, then switch over to the "working" set to install
and test.

My livelihood does not depend on this machine.

My livelihood does depend on systems that are run by "old farts" who do
pretty much the same thing and can't imagine not making backups.  These
system managers also make tape backups for long term storage.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

"Bill Hobbs" <hobbsb@my-deja.com> wrote:
>
> Do I understand this correctly for the original poster (Judson
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> necessary.  Whatever he did, he has no backups.

Have multiple backups, however NE apparently is trashed when being
restored, and you still need a key.

> His business depends on him being able to write, compile, and
