<a id="t-dfa9edc4ff"></a>
## Thread: Rapid Application Development. — 1 msgs

### Rapid Application Development.

- **From:** Foodman <foodman123@aol.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kpiv7$2vl$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```



Hi:

Remember the Great Programming Contest?

To remind you, the GPC arose out of heated
arguments in COMP.LANG.COBOL about the pros
and cons of OO, structured style, other
languages, RAD and stuff like that.

The idea was for people to develop a simple
Time-Clock System (TCS) using their chosen
style/method, etc., so as to demonstrate to
the others that their way was superior. Un-
fortunately, nobody entered the contest,
except me. :(

You can read more of my minimalist views
concerning inexpensive and rapid development
of business applications at foodman123.com/tcs.htm
where you can download a working copy of my entry
in the GPC.

There are approximately 4,500 lines in the
compiled TCS. They break down as follows:

    New Code     1,213
    SP2 copies   2,279 (Generated by SP2)
    Other copies   963 (Standard copies)

TCS.EXE is 175k. The distributable
version is 1.15mb which includes SP2.DLL,
the WAV files, the panel file, and, the
various COBOL runtime things.

The TCS was designed from scratch, pro-
grammed, tested and packaged for dis-
tribution in about 16 hours. This represents
about 76 lines per hour of debugged code
(1213/16). (4500/16=281!).  Applications
like TCS can easily be created using
personnel with minimal training (no
expensive programmers required with
elaborate skill-sets. :)

If you can't improve on 16 hours, and
cannot produce a program which is better
in ANY way, using currently-popular
methods and languages, then who needs
those methods and languages?  The answer,
of course, is 'NOBODY'.


Thanks

Tony Dilworth



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Runtime Problem about RM/COBOL 85

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca (Lorne Sunley)
- **Date:** 2000-07-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hqdYs1NR7tmZ-pn2-iEmsLoqrqOGd@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<8kt3tq$1kaq$1@member.gcn.net.tw>`

```
On Sun, 16 Jul 2000 19:52:20, "True Yang" <dbc888@gcn.net.tw> wrote:

> Hi,
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

If this is a program compiled by the Microfocus
or the Netexpress COBOL compilers that error
is a problem with a temporary sort work file.

You may have a TMP or TEMP variable that
is pointing to a non-existent directory.
```

#### ↳ Re: Runtime Problem about RM/COBOL 85

- **From:** maxomtech@wiscasset.net
- **Date:** 2000-07-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8kvnt1$8ni$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8kt3tq$1kaq$1@member.gcn.net.tw>`

```


According to my manual, a 223 in error in RM/COBOL indicates:
          "...an error occured while loading a DLL file....This error
generally indicates that the DLL was found, but has an invalid format
for the operating system being used.  Some 'system out of memory'
conditions may cause an error 223, since Windows returns an ambiguous
error status in some low memory situations."

Based on that message, it sounds like either a memory problem or a
problem with a call to a DLL.  Many times these messages will return a
"Line number" that the 'call' failed on.

Hope it helps.

Chris Loy


In article <8kt3tq$1kaq$1@member.gcn.net.tw>,
  "True Yang" <dbc888@gcn.net.tw> wrote:
> Hi,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

```
Hi guys,

If there anyone/anywhere on the net that can help describe the full function
of the `screen section`?
I`ve seen it in more than enough code. I`m also wondering is it standard
COBOL?

Colonel.
psxzone@btinternet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Screen Section?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ktf3v$cef$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<S1rc5.3193$AS5.8501@east2.usenetserver.com>`

```
Concerning the question of whether or not it is "standard" - well that
depends <G>.

The existing ANSI/ISO Standards do NOT include it at all.

The existing X/Open specification *does* include it.

The draft of the next ANSI/ISO COBOL Standard includes it (similar - but not
identical to what X/Open specified).

AcuCOBOL (for example) has "enhanced" SIGNIFICANTLY the Screen Section beyond
what was in X/Open (and is in the draft Standard).

MERANT (Micro Focus) has an extension to work with CGI's.

   ***

Does this help - or confuse even more?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section?

- **From:** "Colonel" <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

(home pc).
I know it has `Screen Section`, but I`m interested in learning to get the
most possible from it - more attractive screen layouts for one reason, as
well as others.
I`ve seen in previous posts what seems like you can read data from the
screen which has been typed from keyboard? Or am I getting completely lost??
( like I say, I`m willing to learn as much as possible ).

specific as to level of implementation??

Thanks

Colonel
psxzone@btinternet.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39726A5F.A279C9AB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<S1rc5.3193$AS5.8501@east2.usenetserver.com> <8ktf3v$cef$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <XGrc5.3215$AS5.8416@east2.usenetserver.com>`

```


Colonel wrote:
> 
> Hmm. X/Open??? Sorry Bill, I`m a student learning COBOL.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> specific as to level of implementation??

I can understand your confusion as to Bill's reply - but as posed your
question seemed academic rather than a student "How to ?". At this time
there are different flavours - (BIG BITCH here from me - it means you
lose portability with source code, what works in Fujitsu wont
necessarily work in AcuCOBOL or Micro Focus (MERANT) COBOL).

So for the moment you CAN do what you want as described using any of the
three I've mentioned plus others with Screen Sections - but they vary
slightly. In you case concentrate on the Fujitsu - if you haven't
already got it, get Thane Hubbell's book "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24
Hours" which has Screen Section examples for Fujitsu.

In answer to your 'specific' - you  are on the right track - from a file
you can READ a record and then DISPLAY on a Screen, conversely you
ACCEPT on the Screen and you WRITE a new or changed record to file.

Thane will have to supply this, but there is a web address to look at
his book on-line.

PS:  While in UK I didn't think about miles/kms or inches/cms or
litres/Galls, OR - dollars/pounds - I just shut my eyes and paid - that
way it made the holiday more enjoyable <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-96b5334c2e"></a>
## Thread: Nested copies — 3 msgs

### Re: Nested copies

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-07-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3973A7F1.28D13729@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net> <8ird1d$dgp$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <39520E9D.87F43702@CUSYS.EDU>`

```
The COPY x REPLACING LEADING ... BY ... is needed but we need a better
means of qualification.  As I have posted previously I want to be able
to code MOVE file-name.record-name.group-name.field-name TO x instead of
having to code MOVE field-name OF group-name OF record-name OF
file-name.  I believe that it is easier to read, is more in keeping with
the way the English language works (we say the boy's dog rather than the
dog of the boy in most cases) and follows the form of many other
languages.  I know that many people in the shops where I have worked
have found the use of qualification made the program harder for them to
read.  I dislike the awkward syntax but put up with it because I don't
have to code the same record description twice thus eliminating the
problem of differences.  Unfortunately because qualification was viewed
as awkward we are stuck with needing a construct that allows a copy with
a change of prefix.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc. cfmtech@istar.ca  

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> another prefix), but what is the reason for this proscription?
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested copies

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8l5nom$5c$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8iqmbe$s6c$1@news.igs.net> <8ird1d$dgp$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <39520E9D.87F43702@CUSYS.EDU> <3973A7F1.28D13729@istar.ca>`

```
The "y.x" means "x of y" "enhancement" is on the list of candidates for the
next Standard (the one AFTER the one that is currently in draft format).  If
you believe in it strongly, you can send a letter supporting it (to the J4
chair) to be included when (if?) J4 gets around to prioritizing the next set
of enhancements.

P.S.  Personally, I do *not* like it, but that may because I am so used to
the "x of y" notation and find it incredibly easy to understand.
```

```
There is no fixed standard for OO COBOL and if I have any say at all,
the present standard will be revised to perhaps a large degree before
release -- at least in the OO Area.

The problems are not really syntactical, but there are problems with
the current definition.  This is so quickly changing that I would not
like to see the open source groups chasing the standard.  Let's get it
approved, CORRECTLY, and then let them tackle it.
```

## Thread: Signed pictures? — 8 msgs

### Signed pictures?

- **From:** "Colonel" <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D7Dc5.5489$AS5.12375@east2.usenetserver.com>`

```
I`m in need of a little help here guys. I am a student, although this has
nothing to do with my course work. It`s just a little experiment of my own
and I can`t seem to get it to work as planned.
Problem is -
If I write a line sequential file and one the fields is numeric (all
standard ASCII - no comp etc) and I intentionally store the sign of said
field ( PIC S9999 SIGN LEADING SEPARATE ) so that if you examine the ASCII
file afterwards you will see -9999 or +9999 in the file. I want to be able
to load the records back in and remove the sign character but still keep the
value of the field signed using PIC S9999 then write the record back out as
an ASCII 4 character.

( I hope someone understands that!!  ; )   )

I`ve tried all sorts of things messing with PIC clauses etc. but I`m now
thinking I might have to work out some code to change it manually.

Any ideas?
Thanks in advance

Colonel
psxzone@btinternet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Signed pictures?

- **From:** Warren Porter <wb999port@bellsouth12.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3973201D.60981279@bellsouth12.net>`
- **References:** `<D7Dc5.5489$AS5.12375@east2.usenetserver.com>`

```
Colonel wrote:

> Problem is -
> If I write a line sequential file and one the fields is numeric (all
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> an ASCII 4 character.

When you move an elementary numeric field to another elementary numeric field,
the conversion will be done for you automatically.  Without the "LEADING
SEPERATE" clause the (ususally) last digit will contain both the digit and the
sign of the field.

01  FIELD-1     PIC S9(5) SIGN LEADING SEPERATE.
01  FIELD-2     PIC S9(5).
01  FIELD-3     PIC ZZ,ZZ9-.

If Field-1 contains "+00024", after MOVE FIELD-1 TO FIELD-2, Field-2 would
contain
00002D on many systems.  If you move Field-1 to Field-3 (or move from Field-2
after the above move), Field-3 would contain "bbbb24b" ("b" represents a
space).  The trailing "-" in the picture field would only contain a "-" if the
source field was negative.  HTH
```

#### ↳ Re: Signed pictures?

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<eNIc5.383$5i3.1341@client>`
- **References:** `<D7Dc5.5489$AS5.12375@east2.usenetserver.com>`

```

Colonel <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:D7Dc5.5489$AS5.12375@east2.usenetserver.com...
[snip]
> If I write a line sequential file and one the fields is numeric (all
> standard ASCII - no comp etc) and I intentionally store the sign of said
> field ( PIC S9999 SIGN LEADING SEPARATE ) so that if you examine the ASCII
> file afterwards you will see -9999 or +9999 in the file. I want to be able
> to load the records back in and remove the sign character but still keep
the
> value of the field signed using PIC S9999 then write the record back out
as
> an ASCII 4 character.

Try this:

   03  SIGNED-FIELD      PIC S9999 SIGN LEADING SEPARATE.
   03  REDEFINES SIGNED-FIELD.
         05  SIGN-CHAR    PIC X.
         05  MAGNITUDE-FIELD    PIC 9999.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2000-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VSVc5.6854$IZ1.45173@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<D7Dc5.5489$AS5.12375@east2.usenetserver.com> <eNIc5.383$5i3.1341@client> <M7Sc5.2682$Wc3.5024@east2.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <M7Sc5.2682$Wc3.5024@east2.usenetserver.com>,
Colonel <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

[snippage]

>I`ve also tried Toms` answer but all this does is remove the
>sign. Say the sign was negative the value MAGNITUDE-FIELD ( in Toms`
>example ) becomes positive.

This is, if I recall, named the 'absolute value' by those of mathematical
inclinations.  Now consider a bit of condition-checking:

    05  SIGN-CHAR.
        88  MAGNITUDE-IS-POSITIVE VALUE '+'.
        88  MAGNITUDE-IS-NEGATIVE VALUE '-'.
...

EVALUATE TRUE
    WHEN MAGNITUDE-IS-POSITIVE
        MOVE MAGNITUDE-FIELD TO ANOTHER-SIGNED-FIELD
    WHEN MAGNITUDE-IS-NEGATIVE
        MULTIPLY MAGNITUDE-FIELD BY -1 GIVING ANOTHER-SIGNED-FIELD
    WHEN OTHER
        PERFORM TOUT-CEST-KERFLOOIE-GEGANGEN THRU TCKG-EX
END-EVALUATE.


DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Signed pictures?

- **From:** "Colonel" <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-18T00:00:00

```
Here some source ( MAJOR cut backs purely for demonstration purposes ).
Stored in "IN.DAT" is -123456789 (ASCII). After the run, exactly the same
thing is stored in "OUT.DAT", but with the 9 cut off the end!!

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
 PROGRAM-ID. DATA-CONV.
 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
 FILE-CONTROL.
     SELECT IN-FILE ASSIGN TO "IN.DAT"
        ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL.
     SELECT OUT-FILE ASSIGN TO "OUT.DAT"
        ORGANIZATION LINE SEQUENTIAL.
 DATA DIVISION.
 FILE SECTION.
 FD IN-FILE.
 01 IN-REC.
     03 IN-CODE PIC X(10).

 FD OUT-FILE.
 01 OUT-REC.
     03 PIC S9(7)V99.

 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
  01 TEMP-REC.
     03 TEMP-CODE PIC S9(7)V99 SIGN LEADING SEPARATE.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 MAIN.
     OPEN INPUT IN-FILE
          OUTPUT OUT-FILE
     READ CUST-IN-FILE
     MOVE IN-CODE TO TEMP-CODE
     WRITE OUT-REC FROM TEMP-REC

     CLOSE IN-FILE OUT-FILE
     STOP RUN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Signed pictures?

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2000-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8l19nb$d0j$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<D7Dc5.5489$AS5.12375@east2.usenetserver.com> <eNIc5.383$5i3.1341@client> <IkTc5.2950$Wc3.4136@east2.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <IkTc5.2950$Wc3.4136@east2.usenetserver.com>,
  "Colonel" <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:
> Here some source ( MAJOR cut backs purely for demonstration purposes
).
> Stored in "IN.DAT" is -123456789 (ASCII). After the run, exactly the
> same thing is stored in "OUT.DAT", but with the 9 cut off the end!!

<snip>

> FILE SECTION.
> FD IN-FILE.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

<snip>

>      MOVE IN-CODE TO TEMP-CODE
>      WRITE OUT-REC FROM TEMP-REC

WRITE ... FROM is an implicit MOVE, in your case from TEMP-REC to
OUT-REC, and then the WRITE to the file.

Both TEMP-REC and OUT-REC are aggregates rather than elemntary items. As
you should have learned, MOVEs from and to aggregates never edit fields,
but are treated as strings.
Cut off a lading separate sign and translate it into a 'standard' number
is a sort of edit, so you have to MOVE the elemntary items:

MOVE IN-CODE to TEMP-CODE
MOVE TEMP-CODE to OUT-CODE
WRITE OUT-REC

and yes, you have to define the OUT-REC as:

01 OUT-REC.
   03 OUT-CODE PIC S9(7)V99.

or even easier, you forget the TEMP-REC and MOVE IN-CODE TO OUT-CODE.

Cheers
```

---

```
Michael Satterwhite wrote:
> 
> We have applications written with Microfocus Cobol using their ISAM
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does anyone know of an ODBC and/or ADO driver for this format?

Siber Systems sells MfData2Cr -- this is a Crystal Reportrs Data Source,
that is a DLL that reads MF data files directly into Crystal Reports.

No ODBC or any other intermediate layer is needed,
just MF data files and Crystal Reports, and MfData2Cr DLL between them.

More details at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
Siber Systems
```

```
Is this written in COBOL or is it calling the MF External file
handler?

The reason I ask -- Fujitsu has similar calls for doing File I/O
documented in the windows users guide.  Fujitsu has no ODBC drivers
for their current files, so if you port this product for Fujitsu, you
might have some sales.
```

```
Well, in fact we did FscData2Cr for Fujistu and they seem 
to be pretty happy -- their users can now build big Cobol
applications with state of the art Crystal reporting
that do not use ODBC or any other heavy and expensive 
in-between driver stuff.

Doing ODBC driver is an obvious idea with non-obvious implementation, 
but I thought that Fujitsu already had some kinda 3rd party driver.

Anybody from FSC wants to shed more light on the subject?

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

```
Chovav,

What exactly do you mean by "profiling"?  If want to be able to
analyse your system/programs in detail and perform things like
impact analysis then you could look at MERANT's Revolve
product.  Details of Revolve can be found at:

http://www.merant.com/products/microfocus/revolve/

For information on prices etc. please contact your local MERANT
sales office.

If you are using Server Express then this has a new feature in it
called Profiler.  This will generate a report detailing the following:

%time
time (in milliseconds)
entries
ms/entry
paragraph

This feature is also in Net Express 3.1.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.
```

```
Alex,

I assume you would like to kick-off another EXE program from

CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT function.  Details of the call can be
found in the Net Express online help.
```

<a id="t-a6db814f65"></a>
## Thread: Announce: Tiny COBOL release (0.1.5) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Open-source COBOL (GnuCOBOL, OpenCOBOL, TinyCOBOL)`](topics.md#open-source)_

### Announce: Tiny COBOL release (0.1.5)

- **From:** David Essex <dessex@arvotek.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.compilers
- **Message-ID:** `<00-07-025@comp.compilers>`

```
A new version of the Tiny COBOL compiler is available at sourceforge (1)
and metalab (2). This release (0.1.5) contains some additions to the
COBOL 85 grammar and some bug fixes.


Project Description:

language:       COBOL (COmmon Business Oriented Language)
package:        Tiny COBOL
version:        0.1.5 (pre alpha)
license:        GPL, LGPL (runtime)
parts:          Compiler (produces GNU assembler code for the
                Linux i386 platform), grammar (yacc,lex),
                documentation, examples, test suite, run-time
                library, format conversion utility, source
                code included.
author:         Rildo Pragana <rpragana@acm.org>
location:       http://tiny-cobol.sourceforge.net/ (1)
                http://metalab.unc.edu/pub/Linux/devel/lang/cobol/ (2)
description:    COBOL compiler for the i386 Linux platform.
conformance:    Partial (as close as possible) ANSI COBOL 85. X/Open
                free format.
features:       Linux/UNIX extensions (command line, environmental
                variables, standard input/output/error).
requires:       GNU assembler and linker to produce an executable
                binary.
ports:          Linux on i386 platform.
status:         Development status is active.
discussion:     tiny-cobol-users <tiny-cobol-users@sourceforge.net>.
updated:        2000/07/11



David
```

---

```
I have a project for cobol.  I have to write a program that will get the
number of students and then ask for their student numbers.

The program must then sort the student numbers (selection sort), then it
must ask you to enter a number to search for.  The program must then do a
binary search for the number which the user entered.

here's the basic:

    accept total
    sort
    search until search number is "end"

my problem is that it doesnt work and I'm still at the beginning of my
course and don't know all that much.  It gets the total, maybe sorts, asks
for search number, but doesn't search properly.

If anyone has a program like this or would like to send me something like
it.  It is a short program.  I need something to work from.  My teacher is
totally useless.

Ryan
alee@netactive.co.za
```

#### ↳ Re: Sort Procedure!!

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2000-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000719212406.02132.00001904@nso-cr.aol.com>`

```
In article <8l56vc$17dm$1@nnrp01.ops.uunet.co.za>, "Andrew Lee"
<alee@netactive.co.za> writes:

>
>If anyone has a program like this or would like to send me something like
>it.  It is a short program.  I need something to work from.  My teacher is
>totally useless.
>

It might be helpful to show more of what you have tried.
Does your sort deal with an external file or an internal table?
If you are working with an internal table , as I would assume from
your mention of needing to do a binary search, you need to 
write your own sort routine to sort the table.  There has been 
mention of some vendors that have an extension to the language
that permits sorting of internal tables.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sort Procedure!!

- **From:** Charles Hammond <ceh1@cec.wustl.edu>
- **Date:** 2000-07-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pine.SOL.3.96.1000721125640.27532A-100000@hilton.cec.wustl.edu>`
- **References:** `<8l56vc$17dm$1@nnrp01.ops.uunet.co.za>`

```
Try reading the Book, what an idea, duh.

If all else fails write some code and then ask for help.  What a
remarkable Idea.

Put a display in the program so you know where it crashes.

Put the input in a table or a file?

Still pondering what a selection sort is? Never was too particular with
terms.  You just compare two items  and move the biggest one to the bottom
using a temporary field to hold the bottom one in while you move the top
one to the bottom, and then move the temp to the top.  You sort in a
loop.  Use a test field and turn it to true if you have to
move any fields, if it is false you exit the loop and everything is in
order, if it is try you keep doing the loop!

Perform table-sort-routine until sort-sw = 'f'.

Know what a bubble sort is?

I wrote a program in pascal that orders a table of 50 entries,
randomizes and then sorts them.  We used a bubble sort algorithm.
 There are faster sorting algorithms but they are more complex.

Charles Hammond

> number of students and then ask for their student numbers.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: "Memory violation" — 3 msgs

### "Memory violation"

- **From:** rezeerf@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8l4h5n$pos$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
When i execute a call statement with 3 parameters, i get a memory
violation for one of them in the called program, immediately after the
entry in the procedure-division.

>> call "myprogram" using param1, param2, param3.

param1 and param2 are given correctly, only when i'm looking to the
content of param3, it shows me the text: <memory violation>.

The size of all of the given params in sum is about 8000 Bytes.
There was enough unused memory for a correct execution so i don't know
why that happens.

Is there any limitation to the parameter size when i make a call ?

(using netexpress 3.0)

Thanx in adavance

alex


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: "Memory violation"

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BlrLDVBPmid5IwPK@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8l4h5n$pos$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi Alex,

Don't know too much about Net Express, but a memory violation typically 
means that you've tried to access a bit of memory that you don't 'own'. 
This is very often a result of a call by reference when you meant to 
call by value (or vice versa!), or (more likely) the size of the third 
parameter in the two programs differs. That would be the first thing I'd 
check.

Good luck!

Nigel

In article <8l4h5n$pos$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, rezeerf@my-deja.com writes
>When i execute a call statement with 3 parameters, i get a memory
>violation for one of them in the called program, immediately after the
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: "Memory violation"

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8l6oe6$phs$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<8l4h5n$pos$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
rezeerf@my-deja.com wrote in message <8l4h5n$pos$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>When i execute a call statement with 3 parameters, i get a memory
>violation for one of them in the called program, immediately after the
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Hi Alex,

I think there may be limits, can't remember of the top of my head.
Check system limits in the help. But I don't think you are having
that problem here.

Check the ENTRY statement has three parameters in the USING clause
and that the definitions of the items in LINKAGE SECTION match the
definitions of the items in the CALL statement in the calling program.

If the size of the third parameter is smaller in the calling program than in
the sub program (ENTRY statement). When you try and use the third
parameter, it wil try and access more memory than has been passed.
A possible reason for the memory violation.

If you are still stuck, it will be good to see the definitions of items
param1, param2 and param3 for the CALL statement and again
what your ENTRY statement looks like and the definitions for all
the items in the USING clause.

Regards,
Steve.

>(using netexpress 3.0)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

---

<a id="t-988bab582d"></a>
## Thread: job descriptions — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### job descriptions

- **From:** Bill Hobbs <hobbsb@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8l4klf$sq2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
What are the significant differences between ISO/ANSI COBOL and a
particular vendor's COBOL?

The reason I'm asking is that I've been turned down for several COBOL
programming jobs because the clients wanted *Micro Focus* COBOL
programmers.  I've developed programs for Burroughs, Honeywell, and DEC
COBOL compilers and the COBOL code differences seemed to me to be minor.
The really big differences were in the system calls, but that's a
different bucket of worms.

Is Micro Focus COBOL so different?

I find the same problem with databases: "the qualified candidate will be
proficient in embedded Oracle".  Excuse me, but doesn't Oracle use SQL?
Sure, there may be a couple of bells and whistles added (that make
portability a pain), but I'm pretty sure "embedded Oracle" code looks
exactly like SQL.

So if you have a couple of minutes, wander over to HR and ask to look at
the IT job descriptions used for recruiting.  Consider changing the
phrase "require Micro Focus COBOL and embedded Oracle experience" with
"require COBOL and SQL experience (Micro Focus and Oracle a plus)" or
some such.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: job descriptions

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3975eeac.21876647@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<8l4klf$sq2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Wed, 19 Jul 2000 16:23:36 GMT, Bill Hobbs <hobbsb@my-deja.com>
wrote:

>Is Micro Focus COBOL so different?
>

They don't want to take the time to train you to use the IDE.  See if
you can get a University Edition of NetExpress -- they you'll know how
to use the IDE and you will have a better shot at the job.

And... MicroFocus *IS* that different -- only because it has more
extensions than any other COBOL vendor's COBOL.  But not so much that
you could not pick it up in a hurry.  I think the IDE is the issue.

>I find the same problem with databases: "the qualified candidate will be
>proficient in embedded Oracle".  Excuse me, but doesn't Oracle use SQL?

Oracle also uses an internal proprietary language called PL SQL.  That
might be what they are looking for.


---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: job descriptions

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8l5nhh$ggg$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8l4klf$sq2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The COBOL compilers that you mention are all "mainframe" type compilers.
Micro Focus is PC/UNIX "oriented".  There are a "reasonable" number of
differences in "mind set" in programming for the mainframe as opposed to the
PC/Unix (Workstation).  That *might* be what is bothering them.

Specific "extensions" are common in all COBOL compilers.  IBM mainframes and
Micro Focus and Fujitsu all have their own "peculiarities" as far as the
language goes - and as someone mentioned the "programming
environment/interface" is QUITE different.  You might want to pick up one of
the free or low-cost workstation compilers/environments and "play with it" a
bit to see what it is like.
```

## Thread: FJ and Win Common Dialog — 10 msgs

### FJ and Win Common Dialog

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9iCd5.871$LG.153555@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```
We recently discovered the wonders of using the MS CommonDialog
control within a FJ PowerCOBOL program. It works pretty much as
advertised. For example,

INVOKE CD1 "ShowPrinter" (where "CD1" is the name of the Common Dialog
control)

brings up the normal Windows Select Printer dialog box.

But, doggone it, how can we tell if the user poked the "Cancel" button
on the dialog box?

Any hints would be appreciated.

Jerry Peacock
```

#### ↳ Re: FJ and Win Common Dialog

- **From:** "Tim O'Brien" <tdo658@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NMJd5.3101$U56.86018@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<9iCd5.871$LG.153555@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```
Jerry,

Good question Jerry - but I'm wondering why you would want to trap the
Cancel
click event from the Printer Dialog.  You might want to try the following:

- Select the MSCommonDialog control and bring up the properties window for
the control.
- Select the print tab and click the "CancelError" check box.
- Save and recompile.

Here is the behavior that you get.  When you click "Cancel", the printer
dialog goes away and a
Dialog box (with an exclamation icon) comes up that says, "Cancel was
selected."

Good luck,

Tim



"Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:9iCd5.871$LG.153555@nnrp1.sbc.net...
> We recently discovered the wonders of using the MS CommonDialog
> control within a FJ PowerCOBOL program. It works pretty much as
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
> selected."

Yeah, I got that far. The dialog box tells the user he poked the
"Cancel" button. But my question is: how does the dialog box tell the
calling program?

Other Win applications (e.g., Word) takes the "Cancel" button in the
printer dialog box to mean: 1) I don't want to select a (different)
printer, AND 2) I don't want to print anyway.

Suppose the user selects "Print." The program pops up the printer
dialog box. The user discovers there are NO printers available. He
selects "Cancel."

If the program can't discover the user attempted to abandon the
printing process.... oh well. I guess I could ask again: "Do you
really want to print now that you've selected a printer."
```

```
box showing printing in progression - but if I hit the CANCEL this
signals a Cancel event to PC_PRINT from which I can use a return code to
the invoking class. Surely F/J must give you a similar feature ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FJ and Win Common Dialog

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qLWd5.274$UI3.188510@nnrp2.sbc.net>`

```

James J.
>
> Not quite the same as I'm using PC_PRINT with N/E. It gives me a
Dialog
> box showing printing in progression - but if I hit the CANCEL this
> signals a Cancel event to PC_PRINT from which I can use a return
code to
> the invoking class. Surely F/J must give you a similar feature ?

Yeah, kinda.

I can put my own button on the screen to pause/cancel the printing and
test for the button having been pressed. And we do that.

But that's effective only after the printing gets started.

I want to be able to detect the 'Cancel' button in the "ShowPrinter"
method of the Common Dialog box. If I can't detect the 'Cancel'
button, then (without some other step) the program may well try to
open a non-existent printer before the user gets the chance to poke my
pause/cancel button.
```

#### ↳ Re: FJ and Win Common Dialog

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lf2nf$2lms$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<9iCd5.871$LG.153555@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```

Jerry,

We use the Printer Control under PC 5.0, and if we use the "SetPrinter"
Method, the ReturnValue can be interrogated for POW-TRUE which indicates the
OK button was pressed.  Otherwise, POW-FALSE indicates the cancel button was
pressed.  If we see Cancel, we skip the print routines entirely.  There
should be a sample program in the POWERCOBOL sub-directory in a folder named
PRINT that should illustrate this.

Hope this helps.

Denny
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FJ and Win Common Dialog

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pwWe5.452$pC5.148193@nnrp1.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<9iCd5.871$LG.153555@nnrp1.sbc.net> <8lf2nf$2lms$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Yes, we found that. There is, however, a problem:

We use PowerForm. PowerForm uses (always!) the system default printer
(a ghastly oversight). PowerForm has no way to set the system default
printer. The Print Control (that you suggest) has no way to set the
system default printer. The printer selected by the Print Control is
ignored by PowerForm. So it seems we have three alternatives: 1) Hope
the user set the default printer correctly before entering the
application, 2) Finding some way to get PowerForm to use the printer
we want; 3) Find some way to set the default printer for PowerForm's
use.

(There is another way (according to FJ) involving batch files and the
renaming/redirecting of DEFAULT.PRN information files from within the
application, a charitable description of which would be 'inelegant.')

After some experimentation, we opted for solution #3 using the
CommonDialog control (which is pretty nifty for other stuff too, like
"Save" and "SaveAs"). The CommonDialog box has two buttons: "OK" and
"Cancel." But we can't tell which button the user selected, which
brings us to the subject of this inquiry. And no, the content of
RETURN-CODE,  is not dependable .


"Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:8lf2nf$2lms$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com...
>

>

"SetPrinter"

indicates the
> OK button was pressed.  Otherwise, POW-FALSE indicates the cancel
button was
> pressed.  If we see Cancel, we skip the print routines entirely.
There
> should be a sample program in the POWERCOBOL sub-directory in a
folder named
> PRINT that should illustrate this.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

```

#### ↳ Re: FJ and Win Common Dialog

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<9iCd5.871$LG.153555@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```
Got an answer.

According to FJ support, the MS CommonDialog box posts an error
condition when "Cancel" is selected and this can be tested for only by
using DECLARATIVES.

Further, DECLARATIVES are not supported in PowerCOBOL Version 5 (but
will be in version SEVEN(??!!).


"Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:9iCd5.871$LG.153555@nnrp1.sbc.net...

button
> on the dialog box?
```
