<a id="t-7842d94676"></a>
## Thread: OT: What's Past is Prologue... — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: What's Past is Prologue...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.powerbasic,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hqne5.2407$AC2.225064@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

In 1968 I started college at the Milwaukee School of Engineering. At that
time, all students were required to own a slide rule; the college provided a
list of "acceptable" brand names/model numbers. All freshmen took an
eleven-week non-credit course in "using your slide rule." When tests were
graded, answers were accepted to the accuracy of a slide rule.

In 1975 my brother-in-law started college at the Milwaukee School of
Engineering. At that time, all students were required to own an electronic
calculator; the college provided a list of "acceptable" brand names/model
numbers. All freshmen took an eleven-week non-credit course in "using your
electronic calculator." When tests were graded, answers were accepted to the
accuracy of an electronic calculator.

This past week, in my electronic alumni newsletter, I read this:

"MSOE MAKES HISTORY WITH COMPAQ PC'S

"In Fall 1999 MSOE instituted an academic requirement that each full-time
freshman must have a Compaq notebook PC. MSOE purchased 650 Compaq Armada
notebooks (100 of which were designated for faculty and staff) and plans to
purchase a similar number this year for members of the incoming freshman
class. Students lease the PCs through MSOE and the computers are replaced
every two years."


(MSOE info: www.msoe.edu)
```

---

#### ↳ Re: printing from microfocus

- **From:** "Colonel" <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mB%e5.22729$T5.33914@east2.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<zUGe5.80943$lU5.564769@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com>`

```
If you mean print the report from inside the program read on -

One method -
You need to setup a SELECT clause and ASSIGN TO PRINTER. FD filename as say
PIC X(num) whereas `num` is the printer page width in characters - usually
80. Then OPEN the file as OUTPUT. You can  then WRITE the report one line at
a time.
There are other methods ; )

Any more help give me `mail.

Colonel.
psxzone@btinternet.com



James and Beth Kopec <jbethkopec@home.com> wrote in message
news:zUGe5.80943$lU5.564769@news1.rdc1.nj.home.com...

to

## Thread: Database exercises??? — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Database exercises???

- **From:** "Colonel" <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dX%e5.22749$T5.34797@east2.usenetserver.com>`

```
Hi guys.

Seems all employers only want to employ people with experience ( I can
understand that ). But what of us newcomers looking for our first break.
Obviously in the real world we will be dealing database structures such as
RDBMS, ACCESS, DB2, ORACLE and a whole load more, but our coursework doesn`t
deal with such subjects, and employers aren`t too interested in teaching
either.
I have an idea and before I spend hours of my time trying to implement this
idea, I was wondering if any of you chaps may know in advance whether I`d be
wasting my time.
Most students ( and I speak for myself ) will do at least some of their
coursework at home using a standard Windows PC. Now if they should install
the ODBC drivers that are available and set up various relational databases
in MSAccess, would it be possible to write COBOL programs that can access
such databases therefore mimic-ing a real life scenario?

If I have this completely wrong and screwed up, then please be gentle and
guide the way.

Colonel.
psxzone@btinternet.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Database exercises???

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<397ca7b7.63977188@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<dX%e5.22749$T5.34797@east2.usenetserver.com>`

```
You are not off base.  Oracle realizes this GAP exists between skilled
workers and their database requirements -- so they made Personal
Oracle available as a learning tool.

<colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>Hi guys.
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>

My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

their PRO*COBOL pre-compiler does work with the free version..

#### ↳ Re: Database exercises???

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2000-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-XhelddZCynfC@tcpserver>`

```
On Mon, 24 Jul 2000 18:30:11, "Colonel" 
<colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

> Hi guys.
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
 
This is a recognized problem. As another person posted in the thread
Oracle provides a free version of Oracle.

You can also download a free version of the IBM DB2 personal 
developer's
edition from the IBM web site. They break it into two pieces so that 
it is 
easier to do. Versions are available for OS/2 Warp 4, Linux and 
Windows 

Given the way the server market is moving, you might want to obtain
a copy of Linux and set up your machine to dual boot between Windows
and Linux and experiment with DB2 on both operating systems. You could
also pick up a copy of OS/2 Warp 4 and try that out as well. (Blatant 
plug
from a happy OS/2 user)

URL http://www-4.ibm.com/software/data/db2/

Follow the link for a free download, you have to register but
there is no cost to do so.

You can also download a free entry level version of IBMs
Visual Age for Java IDE if you are interested in branching
out to other languages (I have used COBOL for 25 years
myself but it is always a good idea to be multi-lingual)

URL http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/vajava/

Unfortunately they (IBM) want money for their COBOL compiler.
```

- **From:** Dwight Scott Miller <Merlin43PhD@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```


Colonel wrote:
> 
> Hi guys.
> 
> Seems all employers only want to employ people with experience ( I can
> understand that ). But what of us newcomers looking for our first break.

You are on track.  This is a problem not just for newbies, but for
anyone going through a "career change" as well.  There is a Clipper job
open, in fact it has been open since January, and the employer simply
won't consider a highly experienced programmer, even with dbase work; it
*has* to be Clipper.  So, scrounge up some books, find an old compiler
or whatever to try it, and create yourself some applications that you
can show to an employer.  You can ask Thane if the technique works!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Database exercises???

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<397D911D.D9ADA7CA@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<dX%e5.22749$T5.34797@east2.usenetserver.com> <397CFA1C.2630574F@netscape.net>`

```
Dwight Scott Miller wrote:
> 
> You are on track.  This is a problem not just for newbies, but for
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> can show to an employer.  You can ask Thane if the technique works!

I didn't know there were new Clipper programmers.  I have a friend who
is in big demand as a Clipper programmer.  He's at retirement age so he
likes this (not worried about keeping other skills current).  An
employer should Jump at the chance of getting an experienced dBase
programmer with a smidgen of Clipper though.

There is an old saw which says we should not be the first to jump into a
new idea, nor the last to leave the old idea.  That doesn't apply to
high tech nor programming.  If you are the first or the last to use a
technology, you will be in demand.  If you're in the middle, you have
competition.
```

"The Complete SQL Reference" from Osborne comes with a CD that contains
personal versions of 5 RDBMSs. Oracle, SQL Server, DB2, Sybase and Informix.
It's also a pretty complete reference on SQL and relational databases.

<a id="t-6e391467c2"></a>
## Thread: Another Press Release — 3 msgs

### Another Press Release

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8libu2$8v2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI (and of possible encouragement to those in Academia),

"Creative University Students Recognized for Outstanding Use of COBOL
Programming Language
July 24, 2000 10:01 AM Eastern Time
SANTA CLARA, Calif.--(BUSINESS WIRE)--July 24, 2000--

--MERANT announces seven winners of the GRACIE Competition 2000--

MERANT , a leading global e-business software solutions company, today
announced at the Syllabus 2000 Conference the winners of this year's Admiral
Grace Murray Hopper Student Programming Competition, or GRACIE Competition.
This competition recognizes students that creatively used COBOL (COmmon
Business Oriented Language) in real-world scenarios to solve application
development challenges. Winners were selected by an independent panel of
academic judges in computer science and included student and faculty advisors
from Auburn University Montgomery, Butler County Community College (Pa.) and
Texas A&M University-Kingsville. Student winners were each awarded a cash
prize of $1,000 and the sponsoring academic institutions receives a choice of
computer hardware.

This year's GRACIE's were divided into two categories, one for Net Express
University Edition, a Web-based COBOL development tool designed for the
academic community and Personal Cobol, a traditional COBOL development tool
for academia. Both of these MERANT products support e-business software
solutions for organizations leveraging mainframe-computing assets as they
integrate interactive, Web-based systems.

Net Express University winners included:

- From Auburn University Montgomery, Wesley M. Brand of Prattville, Ala. and
Michael A. Todd of Montgomery, Ala. Both students are seniors completing
their degrees in information systems and were advised by Dr. Ava Honan,
assistant professor of information systems. Their winning entry was an
application designed to permit an organization to have its potential
membership join via the Web. The application also allows tracking of
organization members varying schedules.

- From Butler County Community College, Joan D. Hutzley was recognized for
her program for a fictional bath and tea products company desiring to extend
its business to the Web. The software developed allows the end user to place
an order, enroll in seminars and subscribe to an electronic newsletter all
via the Web. Ms. Hutzley, of Butler, Pa. is working on her associate's degree
in computer information systems and was advised by Azad Ali, assistant
professor of business.

Personal COBOL winners all were from Texas A&M University-Kingsville and
included:

- Gary DeReese, from Alice, Texas and Ronnie Ries, from Kingsville, Texas
were recognized for their development of a database manager to track,
automate and organize the most common tasks of members of a social
fraternity, including member reports and records.

- James Desola, from Robestown, Texas and Melanie Girndt, from Kingsville,
Texas for their application designed for a radio station to document contest
winners, track prize inventory and create managerial reports. All four
winning students are junior computer information systems (CIS) majors. Dr.
Mohan Rao, associate professor of CIS advised both of these winning teams.

"MERANT is once again proud to sponsor this year's GRACIE Competition," said
Dr. Tony Hill, senior vice president and general manager of MERANT. "We are
committed to supporting information systems education. The innovative work of
these students is a credit to each one of them personally and their
respective institutions."

About the GRACIE Award

Rear Admiral Grace Murray Hopper was a professor of mathematics at Vassar
College when the U.S. Navy recruited her in 1943 to work on the first
computer in the United States, the Mark 1, at Harvard University. During the
course of her work, Admiral Hopper created and standardized many key
technical developments - most notably COBOL - the business programming
language used from the earliest computer to the newest microchip. This year
celebrates the 40th anniversary of the development of COBOL.

    ...
"
```

#### ↳ Re: ACU COBOL calling VB DLL's

Lee,

I cannot comment on ACU COBOL but you may find the following
of help.  I assume we are talking about 32 bit Visual BASIC?  If so,
then as far as I am aware the DLLs created by VB are actually OLE
servers and not a standard Windows DLL.

In Net Express we have to access these DLLs via our OLE support
in Object COBOL.

I suspect you may have to do the same in ACU COBOL.

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

- **From:** flexus@epix.net (Bob Wolfe)

- **Message-ID:** `<3981c8ad.1854058@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<964526334.6705.0.nnrp-01.c2dee4ed@news.demon.co.uk> <964538944.29776.0.nnrp-13.c2dee4ed@news.demon.co.uk> <8lmphk$dd7$1@news.cerf.net> <8lntcl$8te@newshost.fujitsu.com.au> <3980508d.303843255@207.126.101.100> <398074EC.8A2C5266@home.com>`

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy


Jimmy:

sp2 supports the ability to utilize any standard OCX control whether
or not is has anything to do with screen handling.

Because sp2 is a screen handling tool, invoking the control is done
through the normal method to display a GUI screen in sp2.

If the control has nothing to do with the user interface (such as a
communications control), then the panel is created using only the
embedded Active X Control and sp2 is called with a "hide window"
switch set to avoid displaying the screen.




The control is invoked from a COBOL
Bob Wolfe, flexus
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ GUI ScreenIO and controlling your controls

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Support@ScreenIO.com (Kevin J. Hansen)
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3981ebbd.17881752@news>`
- **References:** `<964526334.6705.0.nnrp-01.c2dee4ed@news.demon.co.uk> <964538944.29776.0.nnrp-13.c2dee4ed@news.demon.co.uk> <8lmphk$dd7$1@news.cerf.net> <8lntcl$8te@newshost.fujitsu.com.au> <3980508d.303843255@207.126.101.100> <398074EC.8A2C5266@home.com> <3981c8ad.1854058@news.epix.net>`

```
Jimmy,

I missed the first part of this (been incommunicado for the last few
weeks), so I hope this is what you're looking for:

GUI ScreenIO provides "attributes" for each control you define on your
panel.  These attributes can be used to set the color of a control,
protect(disable) a control, make it invisible, make a control active
when the panel is displayed, and so on.  GUI ScreenIO's attributes
have their origin waaaayyy back in our old CICS days; but are more
extensive than CICS supports.

GUI ScreenIO also allows you to define multiple controls in the same
screen location.  You might do this, for example, if you want to have
a field that appears to behave differently in different situations.
You just define fields with the behaviors you want, and at runtime set
the unwanted ones to invisible (which is implicitly
protected/disabled).  

A control which is invisible is, effectively, not there at all.

It's easy to use field attributes in GUI ScreenIO because the
generated copybook names your attributes using your field name plus a
suffix for the attribute's function.  

The possible values for field attributes are documented in comments in
the panel copybook so you don't even have to look 'em up in a manual.
(Nearly all of the values you'd use to control your GUI ScreenIO
application are defined in the panel copybook via 88-level items or,
in a couple of cases, comments).

We also use attributes to inform you which field was active when
control returned to your program, whether the data in fields was
modified by the user, and a bunch of other useful stuff.

Kevin



"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

>
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy

Support@ScreenIO.com
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
http://www.screenio.com
```

On Fri, 28 Jul 2000 22:00:04 GMT, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane
Hubbell) wrote:

>Part of this was about ActiveX controls.  How are ActiveX controls
>supported with GUI screenio?

Thane,

They're not - yet..  We have a few other basics we're hanging in GUI
ScreenIO, then we're going after some of the more magic stuff.

Most of our customers are more concerned with bread-and-butter data
processing apps; there  isn't much demand for ActiveX/OLE/COM in apps
you're porting to Windows/GUI from the mainframe or legacy ScreenIO.  

I expect, however, we'll be looking into it in a few months.

Kevin
Support@ScreenIO.com
Norcom - COBOL Programming Tools
http://www.screenio.com
```

<a id="t-6254d9ba36"></a>
## Thread: STRING, UNSTRING, and INSPECT under CICS under VSE — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### STRING, UNSTRING, and INSPECT under CICS under VSE

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lkq8b$n8k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On June 27th, the following question was asked,

"Dear all,
According to IBM APAR II06138, we should not use STRING, UNSTRING, INSPECT
because they will eat up our 24bit Getvis area. Any recommendation of
alternatives for these 3 words???? "

There were then responses showing that this didn't SEEM to apply to the
current COBOLs, but the original questioner replied,

"Thanks for your detail explanation. But IBM guy told me the issue applies to
COBOL/VSE even through it state in the COBOL manual that it only applies to
DOS/COBOL. Is that true??"

I have now received a reply from my "usually reliable sources" that states,

"I believe, from entries in the VSE-DIGEST newsgroup, that Patrick Lee was
referring to APAR II06138. That APAR certainly reported the problem with
STRING, etc. BUT it was raised in 1992, some 3 years before the advent of
COBOL/VSE. The APAR does say in the text that the COBOL involved is DOS/VS
COBOL. The verbs referred to are not restricted in COBOL/VSE applications for
CICS, and if there is a problem with those verbs it should be reported to
IBM."

> > > >

Sorry for the delay, but I hope that getting this "final" answer is useful to
anyone running across this thread or hearing the out-of-date information
elsewhere.
```

#### ↳ Re: STRING, UNSTRING, and INSPECT under CICS under VSE

- **From:** Fred Young <fred@perfectcircle.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39825968.4E998812@perfectcircle.com>`
- **References:** `<8lkq8b$n8k$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

professional developers require.  Thank you for your time and involvement.

 Fred Young

## Thread: shard memory — 7 msgs

### shard memory

- **From:** rezeerf@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ljp63$af2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi!

Can somebody please give me a short example how to pass a table via
shared memory to another program that i call.

Thanx in advance

alex


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: shard memory

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bEef5.289$fd7.27486@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8ljp63$af2$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Looks like homework, but....

To pass a table, define the table the same way in both the working storage
of the callING program and in the linkage section of the callED program.

If you define the table with a group item enveloping  the elements, as in...

     01  THE-TABLE.
           05 TABLE-ELEMENT     PIC X(01) OCCURS 100.

When you CALL:

CALL programname using THE-TABLE

Or, if the table is described without an enveloping group-item...

01  BIG-WS-AREA
     05 first-thing        pic whatever
     05 second-thing  pic whatever
     05 table-element  pic whatever  occurs 100

you can..

CALL programname USING table-element (1)


(This second method is not covered in textbooks very well. It works because
CALL USING passes the address of the parameter unless you go out of your way
to do otherwise. All the callED progam knows when it starts is that whatever
is that the parameter is an address where whatever is defined as the USING
parameter the PROCEDURE DIVISION header begins. Tables start at element
one).

MCM
```

web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

<a id="t-bda4746063"></a>
## Thread: Using a graphic with a report? — 2 msgs

### Using a graphic with a report?

- **From:** goble@kin.net.au (David. E. Goble)
- **Date:** 2000-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<397d3854.247491@news.adelaide.on.net>`

```
Hi all;

I would like to add a graphic (a bussines logo to a reporting program.
Can this be done?

I will be using Microfocus personal cobol. I have only writtern
reporting programs that write to a text file of filler pic x(80).

So if it is possible I will need a lot of help. Thanks

Also, whats the best way to do charts as reports (ie bar/line charts)?

--Regards :                David. E. Goble
                         goble[AT]kin.net.au
           http://www-mugc.cc.monash.edu.au/~degob1/goble
      Po Box 648 Kingscote Kangaroo Island South Australia 5223
```

#### ↳ Re: Using a graphic with a report?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<397DC996.1784DDC2@home.com>`
- **References:** `<397d3854.247491@news.adelaide.on.net>`

```


"David. E. Goble" wrote:
> 
> Hi all;
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
David,

I don't want to dampen your enthusiasm - but softly, softly, catchee
monkee. Personal COBOL is a learning tool, and you are an amateur
learning. Use the package to get a firm handle on the basics, screen
displays (I don't know whether it provides you any OO for Windows) and
flat file-handling, indexed, relative, sequential, line sequential :-

	- Display on screen as appropriate
	- Add new record			- WRITE new
	- Change existing record		- READ & REWRITE
	- Delete existing record		- READ & DELETE

And of course, produce reports from the data files.

You could then move on to NetExpress University Edition (about $US 100 )
which is same as professional NetExpress, giving you considerably more
features than 'Personal', but can't be used to sell applications.

If 'Personal' contains PC_PRINT routines, look at syntax for printing a
Bitmap, (your business logo).

Charts and Graphs - you could do Mickey Mouse ones in COBOL - BUT - this
is one area where you DEFINITELY don't reinvent the wheel. There are
chart packages, produced by those with mathematical expertise, to cover
this. (They are invariably written with C or C++ type languages in
mind).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<397FA1DD.F8709A27@home.com>`

```
I'm serious, things like the following I've figured out :-

- BTW (by the way)
- IMHO, (I actually believe very firmly in this, but just in case you 
pick me up on it....)

Be buggered if I can translate IIRC, LOL, (Lots of Laughter - maybe ?)
or ROFL - is there a lexicon for this stuff.

Reminds me when I came over to this side of the Pond in '75, I'd browse
through the TV magazine and see there was a program TBA, which was on
quite frequently - never did see the damned program !

PS: This doesn't warrant a 'canned' response from DD!

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
I know that someone will post a web site (that I have heard of before), but

IMHO - In My Humble Opinion
LOL - Laughing Out Loud
ROFL - Rolling on Floor Laughing
IIRC - If I Recall Correctly

(and NO, I will not be adding these to the COBOL FAQ)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help an Old Fogey

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<397FD6A1.C15C3186@home.com>`
- **References:** `<397FA1DD.F8709A27@home.com> <397FC511.236D7D5A@cliveden.com>`

```


"Guy K. Haas" wrote:
> 
> Here's a starting point.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> back in the day -- the era of the adoption of COBOL85.

Guy,

Thanks for site address  - pity Bill doesn't want to add to his FAQ - 
would've lightened it up <G>

As regards COBOL '85 I 'spose I could say again, "This is a fine mess
you've got us into Stanley" <G>

You an old fogey - merely a child. I remember '44 vividly - was 12
turning on 13 around D-Day 6th June. Lived at Portsmouth on south coast.
Military kept it incredibly secret from civilians even though there was
lots of activity in the city - big naval base.

First news on radio - Operation Overlord started at 06:00. They threw
open our sea-front beaches for the first time since '39. Couldn't see
the Isle of Wight some 6 - 8 miles south - view was blocked by all the
naval traffic, battleships, carriers, destroyers, troop transports
etc... And this was at 09:00 - so not the first wave. Overhead the sky
thundered with RAF and USAAF squadrons, like a huge horde of bees on
their way to Normandy....... 'Twas an exciting time - and the sacrifices
made, both sides, totally ignored by today's youngsters.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

James J. Gavan wrote:

>
> ....... 'Twas an exciting time - and the sacrifices
> made, both sides, totally ignored by today's youngsters.

That ist not true. The history of the Third Reich,
the war, D-Day and such is mandatory part of

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help an Old Fogey

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39806C79.BB009D86@home.com>`
- **References:** `<397FA1DD.F8709A27@home.com> <397FC511.236D7D5A@cliveden.com> <397FD6A1.C15C3186@home.com> <39802BCB.5D4B93BA@mch6.siemens.de>`

```


Herwig Huener wrote:
> 
> 2000-07-27 14:28:56 MESZ
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> history lessons in schools in this country.

Herwig,

I understand what you are saying - it is such a sensitive area for you
folks. Unfortunately that's not the case with the 'Western' allies. So
many Americans would just prefer that there was no reference to Korea,
Vietnam etc...

Recent trip to UK (J4 Newbury) I didn't visit my old city Portsmouth.
It's been a naval port since Roman times - Porchester Castle just NW of
the city. Home of a strong naval tradition, Nelson and Trafalgar, and
the first 'visitors' to Australia were shipped from Portsmoth. Kaiser
Wilhelm did a Fleet Review with his cousin Edward VII. Now I'm told its
glory has gone - it no longer has a dockyard for servicing naval ships.

(If history turns you on - I would recommend "Dreadnought" by Robert K.
Massie which deals with the German and British obsessions over naval
superiority - leading to the tragedy of WWI).

Jimmy
```

2000-07-28 00:00:00 MEST

"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> ...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> superiority - leading to the tragedy of WWI).

History does not turn me on, in spite of its
importancy - it too often reminds me of an
activity which we call in German "investigating
dirty underwear" - an activity mostly carried
out in divorce law suit. And a divorce law
suit is a valuabe exercise for every historican.

Whereas one has a clear vilain in WWII, WWI is
not so simple. Not so easily explainable like
a mathematical theory. The sequence of events
leading to WWI is too confusing. Much more
confusing than the CD 1.8 of OO-Cobol. - Oops:
We're back on Topic!

BTW: What is the exact meaning of "Fogey"?
You see, I am still trying to improve my
English.

Herwig
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help an Old Fogey

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Bill Hobbs <hobbsb@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lqdbi$9tr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<397FA1DD.F8709A27@home.com> <397FC511.236D7D5A@cliveden.com> <397FD6A1.C15C3186@home.com> <39802BCB.5D4B93BA@mch6.siemens.de> <39806C79.BB009D86@home.com> <3980B13B.6ABDDD58@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```
In article <3980B13B.6ABDDD58@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>, Herwig Huener
& Josella Simone Playton

> BTW: What is the exact meaning of "Fogey"?
> You see, I am still trying to improve my
> English.

Your English is a lot better than my Deutsch ever was.

<http://dictionary.cambridge.org/define.asp?key=fogey*1%2B0>

fogey, fogy   noun [C] INFORMAL
 a person who is old-fashioned and likes tradition
 The party is run by a bunch of old fogies who resist progress.
 When you're 16, anyone over 25 seems an old fogey.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help an Old Fogey

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lqdqc$cad$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<397FA1DD.F8709A27@home.com> <397FC511.236D7D5A@cliveden.com> <397FD6A1.C15C3186@home.com> <39802BCB.5D4B93BA@mch6.siemens.de> <39806C79.BB009D86@home.com> <3980B13B.6ABDDD58@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```

Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton wrote in message
<3980B13B.6ABDDD58@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>...

>BTW: What is the exact meaning of "Fogey"?
>You see, I am still trying to improve my
>English.
>

I'm not sure if this is a real word, or just slang, but essentially an
"old fogey" is a typical conservative, rather stuffy and set in their
ways person.  It is almost always used with "old", as an adjective.

In Canada, it would probably be applied to an old farmer who thinks
tractors are the work of the devil, whereas in Britain or Germany it
would probably apply to the stereotypical retired military man that
thinks everything should be done on five part forms, and filed in
four different buildings.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help an Old Fogey

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3980C0A1.236B30F5@home.com>`
- **References:** `<397FA1DD.F8709A27@home.com> <397FC511.236D7D5A@cliveden.com> <397FD6A1.C15C3186@home.com> <39802BCB.5D4B93BA@mch6.siemens.de> <39806C79.BB009D86@home.com> <3980B13B.6ABDDD58@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```


Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton wrote:
> 
> 2000-07-28 00:00:00 MEST
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> leading to WWI is too confusing.

Well it's too difficult to cover here briefly - we should trot off to a
History Newsgroup to chew this one over. But History groups - what a
waste of time, they send endless message to determine if Robin Hood of
Sherwood Forest fame was a gay - who gives a bugger (pun not intended) -
it was 1200 AD or thereabouts. And one of their current topics - 'Saving
Private Ryan' - that was the wrong unit of the Wermacht, it should be SS
so-and-so, wrong weapons, wrong tanks etc.. Again who gives a bugger ?
Excellent drama.

> Much more  confusing than the CD 1.8 of OO-Cobol. - Oops:
> We're back on Topic!

DEAD ON !

> BTW: What is the exact meaning of "Fogey"?

Oxford Concise says "Old-fashoned fellow, old man behind the times etc.
18th century, abbreviation of fogram, of unknown origin". Looks to me
like it might have a Latin, Scandanavian or Germanic root.

As regards "being behind the times....", there's a certain gentleman
called Pete Dashwood down there in NZ who would agree the handle fits me
exactly !

> You see, I am still trying to improve my English.

Your English is OK by me - much better than my German after two years
with NATO (RAF, Luftwaffe and R.Neth. A.F.) in Nord Westfalen where I
came back with a reasonable grasp of Dutch. My observation - you have to
hear a language to get a real handle on it.

Jimmy
```

James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Ed Guy wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jerusalem" - is that ye olde ancient pub gong back to 1100 or 1200s ?

That's the one.  It was there at the time of the crusades.  By the look of them some of 
the spiders on the ceiling date from then as well.  If one of them falls in your beer 
you get a free replacement (or did when I was a student - students would be running 
round with about half an inch of beer in the bottom trying to catch a spider).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help an Old Fogey

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39819CD5.B8712D04@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<397FA1DD.F8709A27@home.com> <397FC511.236D7D5A@cliveden.com> <397FD6A1.C15C3186@home.com> <39802BCB.5D4B93BA@mch6.siemens.de> <39806C79.BB009D86@home.com> <3980B13B.6ABDDD58@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton wrote:
> 
> History does not turn me on, in spite of its
> importancy


> Whereas one has a clear vilain in WWII, WWI is
> not so simple. 

The Japanese war machine?

One trouble with Hitler is that he has become a caricature.  What we
need to remember is that the real villains are those who didn't notice
what was happening, didn't fight hard enough, or who didn't know how bad
it would be.  Apparently Hitler was charismatic and told people they
weren't at fault for their problems - and he promised a great future. 
If we think we aren't vulnerable to similar promises then we are sadly
mistaken.
```

He fooled a lot of people for a long time - but that's politicians for you.

WDS wrote:
> 
> On Thu, 27 Jul 2000 12:52:10 GMT, docdwarf wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Maybe because the time of the announcement was TBD?

Ok you mirthful people - try this one on for size. During WWII on BBC
radio there was a program called ITMA (It's that Man Again) starring a
comedian, Tommy Handley. There was a character called Mrs. Mop.
(Visualize, some old bird, cigarette drooping from her mouth, head-band
WWII style, a pail and a mop - and a Cockney, of course).

"'Allo Mr. 'Andley",  she would start, make a few quips and then finish
off with a different acronym for every show - my favourite - NLYSDDTH !
(Never Let Your Sealegs Dangle Down The Hatch !).

Absolutely meaningless, but her quips and acronyms were enjoyed. This is
the same era as Gracie Allen and George Burns etc.....

Jimmy
```

## Thread: Active-x ?? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Active-x ??

- **From:** dw_french@NOSPAMyahoo.comNOSPAM (Dave French)
- **Date:** 2000-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<397ef26e.20879802@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Heres a question.

hase any one tried to use an active-x control with Cobol (Fujitsu in
particular) ??

If so, how do you do it.


Dave French
```

#### ↳ Re: Active-x ??

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<397F0D8C.F7E95600@home.com>`
- **References:** `<397ef26e.20879802@news.demon.co.uk>`

```


Dave French wrote:
> 
> Heres a question.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> If so, how do you do it.

As one of the 'Apostles' of ActiveX - contact the following who uses
Fujitsu and Activex. You wont see messages here because he has troubles
with his ISP in New Zealand :-

	Pete_Dashwood@compuserve.com

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Active-x ??

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2VIf5.272$Vh7.33582@nnrp2.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<397ef26e.20879802@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
Assuming you have the Active-X control registered properly with
Windows:

Right-click on the form-design toolbar.

After several seconds, a list will popup of available Active-X
controls (there may be dozens). Check the ones you want. The
newly-checked controls will now appear on the form-design toolbar.

From there you select the controls you want and spot them on your
form.

Hope this helps.


"Dave French" <dw_french@NOSPAMyahoo.comNOSPAM> wrote in message
news:397ef26e.20879802@news.demon.co.uk...
> Heres a question.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Active-x ??

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3980502d.303747638@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<397ef26e.20879802@news.demon.co.uk>`

```
There are three ways (maybe more!) that I have seen used.

1.  Use the control in conjunction with PowerCOBOL
2.  Use the control natively via INVOKE OLE statements within Standard
COBOL.
3.  Use a third party tool - COBOL Sp2 From Flexus.com that supports
ActiveX controls.

## Thread: Report Writer - Fujitsu v3 — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Report Writer - Fujitsu v3

- **From:** "M S" <maz@websurf.fsnet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2000-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ln7d4$lg5$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Hi
This is a student's question. I am trying to print report using
Report Writer in a program. The length of the maximum column to be printed
is 140.
The program gives me compilation error saying -
'REPORT WRITER LOGICAL RECORD IS TOO LONG. RECORD CONTAINS 140 ASSUMED'

I am not sure what it is ? whether the Report Writer allows *only 80
characters* to be
printed in one line - or if it is *my* compiler specific problem - or if I
need to set some options etc.
Any help will be much appreciated. Thankyou..
Regards
M S.

some of the coding is..
FD  PRINT-FILE
      REPORT IS INVALID-REPORT.
.
.
.
REPORT SECTION.
RD  INVALID-REPORT
      PAGE LIMITS ARE 40 LINES
      HEADING           01
      FIRST DETAIL    09
      LAST  DETAIL    35.
```

#### ↳ Re: Report Writer - Fujitsu v3

- **From:** petwir@saif.com (Pete Wirfs)
- **Date:** 2000-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<397f2c3d.61746146@news.transport.com>`
- **References:** `<8ln7d4$lg5$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Mainframe printing facilities at one time were limited to 132 columns
because that was all the printer could accept.  I believe the COBOL
REPORT WRITER compiler still contains this limit.

Solution:
You can not print 140.  You have to chop it into multiple print lines.

Pete




On Wed, 26 Jul 2000 18:25:35 +0100, "M S" <maz@websurf.fsnet.co.uk>
wrote:

>Hi
>This is a student's question. I am trying to print report using
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

 FD  PRINT-FILE
       REPORT IS INVALID-REPORT.
     .

to

 FD  PRINT-FILE
       Record contains 140 characters
       REPORT IS INVALID-REPORT.
     .

level is.  Is this a "serious" or only a "warning" message?  The "assumption"
that it is making seems like what you want anyway.

We've had & solved this problem with MicroFocus COBOL ReportWriter at work.
Unfortunately I'm home today so I can't look up the details but you're on the
right track. IIRC more important than the RECORD CONTAINS clause in the FD is the
LINE LENGTH clause in the RD.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Report Writer - Fujitsu v3

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lo09v$7l3$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8ln7d4$lg5$1@news7.svr.pol.co.uk> <8lnfuh$tup$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <397F73D3.4A22725A@alaska.com>`

```
I had to look this one up (again).

There is no "line length" option in the Standard RD statement.

In the draft of the next Standard, there is a
   PAGE LIMIT IS n COLUMNS

but that isn't part of the '85 Standard.

I really, REALLY, think that it should be the RECORDS CONTAIN (in the FD)
that deals with this.  (I have checked the latest Fujitsu LRM and they *seem*
to be ANSI conforming for this.)

Can anyone using Fujitsu V5.0 check and see if this makes a difference with
the current compiler and was just a "bug" in the V3.0 product?
```

## Thread: Converting a CSV file — 26 this month / 27 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Converting a CSV file

- **From:** brewman15205@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lpk59$lc8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I'm trying to figure out how to read in a record that was saved in CSV
format from Excel. It seems easy enough to "unstring...delimited by
comma" to break it apart into the various fields. The problem is, some
of the fields contain commas as the data. And there's the possibility
the data may also contain quotes. I can "inspect...replace" the double
quotes put in the record (to indicate a quote) to arrive at a single
quote. I'm trying to figure out how I can recognize the comma that's
part of the data and not what's separating the fields.

Thanks for your help.




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting a CSV file

- **From:** Craig <cyonkeNOcySPAM@sentry-direct.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<03334538.a56c3a4d@usw-ex0103-023.remarq.com>`
- **References:** `<8lpk59$lc8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Have them save it as a tab delimitted file and check for the hex
representation of the tab character.


-----------------------------------------------------------

Got questions?  Get answers over the phone at Keen.com.

http://www.keen.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting a CSV file

- **From:** WDS@WDS.WDS.5 (WDS)
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3980a802.22907510@news1.frb.gov>`
- **References:** `<8lpk59$lc8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Thu, 27 Jul 2000 15:23:28 GMT, brewman15205 wrote:

>I'm trying to figure out how to read in a record that was saved in CSV
>format from Excel. It seems easy enough to "unstring...delimited by
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>part of the data and not what's separating the fields.

I thing the UNSTRING is the place to start.  Use the comma as your
delimiter, and allow significantly more receiving fields than the
number of fields in the CSV record.  You might also want to add a
comma to the end of the record before UNSTRINGing it.

Then look at the first and last character in each of the receiving
fields for the quote character.  (If you have any fields for which the
data may start or end with a quote, then you would need to also
examine the first three and last three characters for three
consecutive quotes.)  If one field starts with a quote, and the next
ends with one, then STRING the two together as one alpha item.

Note, also, that numbers that are displayed as comma-formatted in
Excel will be saved into the CSV file with the comma, the decimal
point, and surrounded by quotes.

Another strange occurrence is that text fields, if all numeric, will
be written to the CSV file as numeric fields (without quotes).

In a sample Excel file, i entered several values into one row, and
saved them into a CSV file.  These are the results:

Field   Format            Value    Displays As   CSV Result
-----   ---------------   ------   -----------   ----------
  A     General           1        1             1
  B     Numeric w/Comma   1234     1,234.00      "1,234.00"
  C     Text              ab,cd    ab,cd         "ab,cd"
  D     Text              abcd     abcd          "abcd"
  E     Text              1234     1234          1234
  F     Text              "abcd"   "abcd"        """abcd"""

Resulting CSV file record:

1,"1,234.00","ab,cd",abcd,1234,"""abcd"""

I hope you might find some of this to be useful.  Good luck.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a CSV file

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a CSV file

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lqut7$lic$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8lpk59$lc8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3980a802.22907510@news1.frb.gov> <3980BB58.20A0F010@home.com>`

```
The right way is to open the spreadsheet as an OLE object, and
read the data out of the cells directly. Then there is no need to data
match.  The two objects speak a common language ...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>

It is fairly easy in Expel to reformat the file to have fixed width's and
left adjust or right adjust if numeric.

If you want I can give you instructions step by step to do this.

highlight the column
click on format
then column
then tell it how wide you need it, this wil then become fixed length
then click on format again
then click on cells
then tell it to left adjust if alpha
right adjust of numeric
and skip commas and dollar signs
it is pretty easy to do
but, this all depends on your having Expel from Microslop.......

talk later,


Bill

#### ↳ Re: Converting a CSV file

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-esevXC4KHZeo@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<8lpk59$lc8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Thu, 27 Jul 2000 15:23:28, brewman15205@my-deja.com wrote:

> I'm trying to figure out how to read in a record that was saved in CSV
> format from Excel. It seems easy enough to "unstring...delimited by
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Try something like this

There is a table of buffers to receive the decoded data

78  decode-buffer-number value 100.
01  buffer-table.
      02  decode-data occurs decode-buffer-number.
            03  decode-len  comp pic s9(9).
            03  decode-buf   pic x(200).

The input record is read into decode-record.

      * do delimited file conversion

           move 1 to i-ctr.

           perform varying idx from 1 by 1
               until idx > decode-buffer-number

               move space to wrk-buf1
               move space to decode-buf(idx)
               move zero to decode-len(idx)

               move 1 to ptr1
               move zero to ctr1
               if dosfile-record(i-ctr:1) = '"'
                   add 1 to i-ctr
                   unstring dosfile-record delimited by '",'
                       into wrk-buf1
                       count ctr1
                       pointer i-ctr
               else
                   unstring dosfile-record delimited by ","
                       into wrk-buf1
                       count ctr1
                       pointer i-ctr
               end-if
               move wrk-buf1(1:ctr1) to decode-buf(idx)
               move ctr1 to decode-len(idx)
           end-perform.
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting a CSV file

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UWrg5.333$hX1.64516@nnrp1.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<8lpk59$lc8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

<brewman15205@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8lpk59$lc8$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I'm trying to figure out how to read in a record that was saved in
CSV
> format from Excel.

Here's the way we do it:
1. Check the record byte-by-byte looking for both double-quotes and
commas. Convert the commas enclosed within quotes to something else
you can find later (LOW-VALUES in the example below). (Pay attention
to FLIP - it's a nifty way to turn a switch on and off)

MOVE 1 TO FLIP.
PERFORM VARYING INDX FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL INDX > RECORD-LENGTH
     IF MYREC(INDX:1) = ' " '
          COMPUTE FLIP = 3 - FLIP
     END-IF
     IF MYREC(INDX:1) = ','
          AND FLIP = 2
          MOVE LOW-VALUES TO MYREC(INDX:1)
     END-IF
     END-PERFORM

2. Having gotten rid of the interior commas, you can now unstring the
whole shebang into separate fields:

MOVE SPACE TO MYFIELDS.
MOVE 0 TO FIELD-INDX.
MOVE 1 TO MYREC-POINTER.
PERFORM UNTIL MYREC-POINTER > RECORD-LENGTH
     ADD 1 TO FIELD-INDX
     UNSTRING MYREC DELIMITED BY ',' INTO MYFIELD(FIELD-INDX)
          WITH POINTER MYREC-POINTER
     END-PERFORM.

3. Now reinsert the interior commas:
INSPECT MYFIELDS REPLACING ALL LOW-VALUES BY ','.

The trick is letting the double-quotes clue you as to the presence of
non-delimiting commas.

Some minor cleanup (like removing the double-quotes, justifying
fields, converting to numeric, etc.), and
you're done.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a CSV file

- **From:** Fred Young <fred@perfectcircle.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39825689.B0F8786D@perfectcircle.com>`
- **References:** `<8lpk59$lc8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <UWrg5.333$hX1.64516@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```
This is the method we use as well.  The real problem, is not how to
"unstring" the .CSV data, but what will you do with the data afterwords.
By changing the interior commas and quotes to something else (hex 00, 01,
02, 03, etc) Unstring can break out the fields nicely.  You can then
manipulate complete fields.  When the manipulation is finished, you can
recover the interior commas and quotes.

Fred Young

Firstly who was the nut that introduced the abbreviation "CSV" - I
nearly ignored the whole thread thinking it was something to do
specifically with a mainframe !

Jerry,

Rather than try out your code - would it handle the following crap which
I've encountered :-

123456,"H05EL","H-BAND ON 5 INCH ELBOW		",123.45,123.45,
123456,"H05EL","H-BAND ON 5' ELBOW		",123.45,123.45,
123456""H05EL","H-BAND ON 5" ELBOW		",123.45,123.45, 

This necessitated me checking for an odd number of quotes. Originally
did this in DOS, but I suppose with Windowing you could display the
'offending record' and get the user to edit the surplus guff - but what
a pain.

This whole business of ascii-delimiteds is a bloody mess if you are
dependent upon the data coming from either another application or
software package.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Converting a CSV file

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3981AEE0.FF00D160@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8lpk59$lc8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
brewman15205@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> I'm trying to figure out how to read in a record that was saved in CSV
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> part of the data and not what's separating the fields.

Comma delimited files are written wrong by more programs than not
Excel works and most others do not.

The standard is that the fields are either numeric (no quotes) or
string (quotes).  If you have quotes on a field then you read to the
next quote,  if this quote is doubled, quote quote, then it is a
single quote in data.  There is commas between fields.

I cannot think of how to do this except for scanning one character at
a time.

If you are parsing non-excel data then you must consider going
backwards and guess whether this comma is inside or outside a field. 
For some reason this works better but I cannot remember why.

If you do write a routine please consider giving it to the cobol
public domain.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

Ken Foskey wrote:

> The standard is that the fields are either numeric (no quotes) or
> string (quotes).  If you have quotes on a field then you read to the
> next quote,  if this quote is doubled, quote quote, then it is a
> single quote in data.  There is commas between fields.

Everybody seems to do it differently. ParseRat will handle a single quote inside a data 
field quite happily. It looks to see if the NEXT character is a comma or line-end before 
deciding that it's the end of the field.
```

Jerry P wrote:
> 
> Tried Parse Rat.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> and wouldn't be gainsaid.

A "stop" button is something I'll have to address.  The usual way to see if you've set 
up the parameters right is to use the "single step" process (the "next" button) and view 
the output preview display at the bottom of the screen. As you will have noticed, the 
effect of changing settings is instantly displayed there and of course you can "exit" 
any time you like before you tell it to convert the whole file.  I don't usually "let 
'er rip" until I've previewed a number of records.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a CSV file

- **From:** pgraham@dynasty.net
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:

|The standard is that the fields are either numeric (no quotes) or
|string (quotes).

Where did you see this standard?  My standard says that a string field
that does not contain commas does not need quotes. (quotes don't hurt,
of course, and sloppy code sometimes puts them around all string
fields)

Phil
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Converting a CSV file

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39850713.B6054A55@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<8lpk59$lc8$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3981AEE0.FF00D160@zip.com.au> <VUCg5.5568$Dw6.13870@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
pgraham@dynasty.net wrote:
> 
> Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> fields)

All right there is NO standard definition.  There are many,  this is
one of the originals from CPM-80, remember that long ago.

I do not think that enclosing every field is 'sloppy code'.   Do the
simplest thing that can work.  Working out whether you need them or
not adds an order of magnitude to the complexity of the code and is
typically just not required.  Your mileage may vary due to tight
transmission constraints and over spec CPUs though.

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

## Thread: OO-Cobol ">> define ..." understood? — 14 this month / 23 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### OO-Cobol ">> define ..." understood?

- **From:** Herwig Huener <Herwig.Huener@mch6.siemens.de>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3980114C.4D15C14@mch6.siemens.de>`

```
2000-07-27 12:26:16 MESZ

I now put my reputation as a programmer at
risk or I tell some company-secrets. The
way I understand the ">> define ..."
directive is as seen below. Does anybody
interpret the OO-Cobol Standard differently?

I always have my difficulties with verbal
descriptions of things which should be
described in a more formal way. To my mind,
the whole draft of OO-Cobol (1.8) is
too informal.

Here it goes:

 +----------------------------------------------------------
 |     The compiler-variable does already exist
 |     +----------------------------------------------------
…[64 more quoted lines elided]…
 |     |     |     |     |
 +-----+-----+-----+-----+----------------------------------

Regards
Herwig Huener

Herwig Huener wrote:
> 
> 2000-07-27 12:26:16 MESZ
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Herwig,

Point well made. However I can't really comment on your example because
I avoid messing around with directives like the plague - I go with the
defaults offered by the compiler vendor - that way, if it goes wrong, I
can say "So what is wrong, I haven't changed anything".

I agree the Standard MUST HAVE supporting examples, both as you
illustrate and then code for each VERB etc... As somebody once said to
me on Compuserve, when I gave him a quickie on Screen Section "One small
piece of COBOL source code is worth a million words".

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO-Cobol ">> define ..." understood?

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3980B109.AA801154@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`
- **References:** `<3980114C.4D15C14@mch6.siemens.de> <39806E61.10B4E83E@home.com>`

```
2000-07-27 23:50:00 MESZ

"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> ...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I avoid messing around with directives like the plague

I cannot avoid messing around with directives
now and then because I am implementing them!

> - I go with the
> defaults offered by the compiler vendor - that way, if it goes wrong, I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> piece of COBOL source code is worth a million words".

That's the thing which troubles me very much. A programming
language is a formal thing and it must be described in
formal ways in order to verify completeness, consistency
and such. In case of OO-Cobol, it would be nice if there
were a formal description as a starting point, that is,
a formal description Cobol 85. But I would not attribute
the word "formal" to the Cobol 85 describing document.
Too much to expect from a language which evolved around
the actual needs of programming life.

However, it was the same problem in the old days when I
was in an Ada-83 project. Endless disscussions about
the meaning of the evolving LRM. Many surprises when
implementing it - always new surprises lurking behind
innocent formulations in the manual. Ada-83 was an
entirely new language, and in spite of that, the language
description did not meet my preferences for precision.

In older yet days, I was in a government project where
I was investigating existing methodologies of
Requirement-Engineering. That's an important job
because there are indeed software-projects being launched
without anyone having a clue what the final product shall
accomplish. Then I was changing to compiler-writing, and
I thought: No problems anymore. The requirements for
a compiler is mostly a description of the language
to be compiled. There can't be any difficulties. That's
what I really thought.

Oops.

BTW: At the moment, I have a first look at XML. It occurs
to me that this could be used as a language-defining tool,
but I am not yet sure. Any ideas?

Herwig
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO-Cobol ">> define ..." understood?

- **From:** frlsfly@aol.com (FRLSFLY)
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20000728022843.11715.00000015@ng-fv1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3980B109.AA801154@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`

```

Snip-snip

>That's the thing which troubles me very much. A programming
>language is a formal thing and it must be described in
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>description did not meet my preferences for precision.

snip-snip

In my limited experience, if you cannot describe a function or concept in clear
language, it is because the function or concept is irrational.  Not necessarily
illogical, but definitely irrational.

The original concepts behind OO are, I think,  easy to understand and
relatively useful.  Nothing new,  but some interesting attempts to formalise
function bounding and calling protocols; and as Visual Basic 6 shows, the
notion of DataBase as Object can be useful in a wide variety of circumstances.

I suspect the grandiose snake-oil schemes we now dub "OOP"  are difficult to
comprehend because they are the products of wishful thinking .

Not that I know anything, but perhaps you cannot make sense of your
requirements because your requirements make no sense?
Nathan Meyer
Berkeley, CA
"Big Endian" is what I like.
```

2000-07-28 10:44:01 MEST

FRLSFLY wrote:
> 
> ...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> illogical, but definitely irrational.

That is true. But also true is that natural language does not allow
formal processing of a definition or such on completeness and
unambiguity and absence of contradiction. The larger a document
is, the more it will exceed the mind of any single person.

Natural language can describe everything. But people cannot.

BTW: Same thing applies to writing a fictuous story. I was
once counceling a writer, and we had a hard time to keep the
story free of contradictions. For example, one must keep a list
of people killed in order to prevent "resurrections" now and
then, one must keep a list of who knows what and since when
and so on.

> ...
> 
> I suspect the grandiose snake-oil schemes we now dub "OOP"  are difficult to
> comprehend because they are the products of wishful thinking .

Avoiding wishful thinking is always a good idea. But
it should be commonplace that not all jobs are done
best with OOP.

> Not that I know anything, but perhaps you cannot make sense of your
> requirements because your requirements make no sense?

Have to think about that. Understanding a technical document
on first time reading makes certainly sense - but of
course my limited capabilities may make that impossible.

> "Big Endian" is what I like.

What about One-Complement vs. Two-Complement representation of
integers? Just asking.

Herwig

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO-Cobol ">> define ..." understood?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3981BA6D.D8B1B3E8@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3980B109.AA801154@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <20000728022843.11715.00000015@ng-fv1.aol.com>`

```


FRLSFLY wrote:
> 
> Snip-snip
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> illogical, but definitely irrational.

>>>>>>>>>
I agree with what you are saying in your message but would question your
use of the word irrational in this context, (unreasonable, illogical,
absurd etc...) Would you accept "..... it is because the function or
concept is not written or explained in plain English..."  ???
>>>>>>>>
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> comprehend because they are the products of wishful thinking .

>>>>>>>
No, because they are not explained clearly and WITH EXAMPLES
illustrating what is intended.

As a result of a query by someone, I've had to go back to the Standards
CD and reference a definition of a method :-

"9.3.4. Methods

The procedural code in an object is placed in methods. Each method has
its own method-name and its own data division and procedure division.
When a method is invoked, the procedural code it contains is executed. A
method is invoked by specifying an identifier that references the object
and the name of the method. A method may specify parameters and a
returning item".

I am working solely in OO. If I read the above for the first time, it is
as clear as mud. Initially I was 'duped' by the last four words - saying
to myself, "Well what do you do when you want to return......".
It needs expansion, plus EXAMPLES. You can only return ONE LEVEL 01 item
but that may also be split down to Level 05s etc to return one or more
data items or object references. 

Before anybody asks - No. I'm not volunteering to do it !

While the Standards document makes a valiant attempt to cover all
points, note that Chapter 9, containing that method reference is listed
under :-

	9. I-O, objects and user-defined functions

No ! It should appear in a separate chapter(s) devoted solely to the OO
aspect of the Standards.

My own version of 9.3.4. would most certainly occupy at least half a
page, possibly two-thirds and with it I would emphasize the point by
including code examples. So now I've turned a two-volume book into 3 or
even 4 volumes. But in explaining the technical-ese, if I write in plain
English, the Standards document, (whilst it wont hit the best sellers
list), would be a document you could turn to for reference, rather than
have to call up each vendor's interpretation as provided in your on-line
help files.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO-Cobol ">> define ..." understood?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lsf6c$7i5$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3980B109.AA801154@Josella-Simone-Playton.de> <20000728022843.11715.00000015@ng-fv1.aol.com> <3981BA6D.D8B1B3E8@home.com>`

```
By design, the Standard is *not* intended to be a "reference" manual.  The
committees have repeatedly and consistently stated that it is NOT intended
for "users" - but for implementors.  Furthermore, it isn't even intended to
be "easy" for them to use - but is SUPPOSED to avoid "ambiguities" (although
see my other note about that one !!!)

If you (anyone) thinks the Standard *should* be targeted at users as well as
implementors (as the C Standard is - for example), then you should submit
this comment to your national Standards body.  (ANSI in the US).  This is NOT
something that (IMHO) sending a note directly to J4 will change in the
foreseeable future.
```

#### ↳ Re: OO-Cobol ">> define ..." understood?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3981AD0A.97778F9E@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<3980114C.4D15C14@mch6.siemens.de>`

```
Herwig Huener wrote:
> 
> 2000-07-27 12:26:16 MESZ
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Not a very helpful answer but I propose that the standards committee
deletes 'as parameter' and 'override'.  They add no value and they are
confusing as you have shown.

If you want to allow parameters into the compiler then do so with
parameters XYZ=ON.  If you want to have default settings in your code
then do so:

>> if not defined
	>> define XYZ=OFF
>> end-if

(Apologies if the syntax is wrong I don't use it so I don't know it)

If you want to override whether or not the command line is there then
simply put the define in.  It is impossible for it to be affected by
the command line.

I cannot for the life of me work out why you would add the extra
phrases as it just make the language more complex.

Comments before I forward it onto the cobol committee for comment.


Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OO-Cobol ">> define ..." understood?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lsc92$c49$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3980114C.4D15C14@mch6.siemens.de> <3981AD0A.97778F9E@zip.com.au>`

```
Well, ...
  IMHO *any* substantive (and controversial) change at this time will be
looked at QUITE skeptically by the Standards committees. (and your thoughts
are both) However, please feel free to send a note/paper if you think there
is a "real" problem (not just a "why bother" issue).

OVERRIDE is critical, in my opinion.  It allows you to CHANGE the value of a
compiler variable during a compilation.  "Normally" it would occur within the
scope of an >>IF or >>EVALUATE statements. (BTW, "override" functionality
does NOT work if it is the default, unless there is some other syntax
introduced that works the opposite way.)

As far as "parameters" goes, this is another "feature" that I used heavily
with the Micro Focus "design".  In that environment, you could define a
"constant" in three ways:

 - 78 level (in source code)
 - CONSTANT directive (command to compiler)
 - $CONSTANT (half way between the previous two)

I think that the draft would really be hampered if all 3 variations were not
"possible" (albeit via new syntax) to those wishing to use them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO-Cobol ">> define ..." understood?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39829F3E.2E9D875A@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`
- **References:** `<3980114C.4D15C14@mch6.siemens.de> <3981AD0A.97778F9E@zip.com.au> <8lsc92$c49$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <398238DC.1350C747@zip.com.au>`

```
2000-07-29 11:00:00 MEST

Ken Foskey wrote:
>
> ...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> extra features.   Any one for a OO cobol lite standard for GNU?

For the number of lines I may give a partial answer. Our
OO-Cobol is now implemented mostly in C. The partial
implementation of the directives is more than 6000 lines
(Pascal P4 Compiler is exact 4000 lines!).

However, other programers might have compressed my work
in less than 2000 lines. If one is compelled to use a
mainframe sourcetext editor - another twenty-four lines
every other two minutes - one would write more compact
code. Also, I do not use every sophisticated feature
of the C-language. I believe in defensive programming.
After all, they blame me if something goes wrong ...

"Keep it simple" is a great thing. That cannot anymore
be done with Cobol. But in order to keep it simple, should
it permissible to have a Gnubol (GNU-Cobol) language,
which is not exactly a subset of Cobol, in order to archive
the goal of utmost simplicity with definition and
implementation of the language?

As every neighbourhood drug dealer will tell you, once the
addiction is well established, cobol-addicts will accept
*any* complexity when moving to real-life Cobol!

Herwig
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO-Cobol ">> define ..." understood?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sob8mmjl9ft25@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Our IBM-compatible, non OO COBOL parser for the DMS Reengineering Toolkit
(http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMS/DMSToolkit.html)
has some 3500 grammar productions, filling some 7000 source lines.
Our parser also does name/type
resolution as an attributed grammer, which is some 30,000 lines,
including the bare grammar above.

We've briefly looked at OO COBOL, and expect the attributed
grammer to grow about 50%.
```

It is old knowledge that adding a few features to a
language does not add some lines to the compiler of
this language but multiplies the number of lines with
a factor somewhat greater than unity. Look at
"The Psychology of Computer Programming" by (???) -
an old and wise book, yet ignored by most
sodtware project managers.

Herwig

## Thread: Migrating VAX/VMS COBOL to UNIX COBOL(Solaris) — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Migrating VAX/VMS COBOL to UNIX COBOL(Solaris)

- **From:** john_alan_calma@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lop1u$22g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi!

We are studying the possibility of migrating our VAX/VMS COBOL system
to UNIX COBOL (Solaris).

Can anybody help me on what are the steps needed to convert the system?
What are the tools that we can use to convert our system?

Thank you very much in advance.

John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Migrating VAX/VMS COBOL to UNIX COBOL(Solaris)

- **From:** Bill Hobbs <hobbsb@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lpmue$njf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8lop1u$22g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <8lop1u$22g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, john_alan_calma@my-deja.com
wrote:
> We are studying the possibility of migrating our
> VAX/VMS COBOL system to UNIX COBOL (Solaris).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> What are the tools that we can use to convert our system?

As a VMS bigot, I'm sorry to see that you may be leaving VMS.

Another option to consider, if your heart is set on UNIX, is Tru64 UNIX.
I believe that the Tru64 COBOL compiler is shared with VMS.

Anyway, I suggest that you get all your source code files up to standard
on your current system - the definition of standard is left as an
exercise for the student.  :-)  Seriously, look at the COBOL compiler
options, especially /standard= and /warnings=.  Make the compiler as
cranky as possible.  Then go fix the code to eliminate the messages.
Some messages you may decide to live with but be prepared to explain
your reasons.  You may want to consider upgrading to the latest compiler
version for the bug fixes and standards compliance.

At a previous job, I wrote command procedures that searched for source
files (COBOL, C, forms, macro, link, ...), compiled them all, and linked
new executables.  We found programs that hadn't been compiled in years
(used compiler features that were no longer available), there were
missing source files (five year old backups can be very handy), lots of
unused programs (if no one links to it, get rid of it), etc..  We
started with around 1,500 source files, most of them COBOL, and it took
two of us about two months to get everything to compile and link ...
then the users had to test it to be sure this was pretty close to the
current production system, that took three months.  *Then* we started
making Y2K fixes.

So my recommendation is to use the tools you have and write code to
commonly accepted standards.



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Migrating VAX/VMS COBOL to UNIX COBOL(Solaris)

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-07-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39805b9b.306673753@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<8lop1u$22g$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I've answered a similar question here before, but here goes again.

VMS COBOL is very very standard.  If you don't use DEC forms the
conversion of the COBOL will be fairly simple.  However, the whole VMS
thing of Logicals and Symbols and how the file system creates
generations (Versions) of the data files, will be a bigger issue.
That and the fact that at least at the VMS installation were I am, we
do a LOT of stuff in the DCL.  Converting that DCL could be
significant.

<a id="t-9c88e48fb5"></a>
## Thread: Cobol as a noun — 22 this month / 53 total

### Cobol as a noun

- **From:** Fred Young <fred@perfectcircle.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3982536B.615784C7@perfectcircle.com>`

```
This time is interesting for those of us who deal with highly technical
writing, as well as normal business communication.  I have made up
words, made nouns into verbs, and changed the traditional rules of
punctuation, in order to improve the clarity of my writing.  I will give
you two examples I would like to see gain acceptance.

ï¿½COBOLï¿½, as most of us know, is an acronym for Common Business Oriented
Language.  ï¿½Cobolï¿½, in my opinion, is a noun.  I, and others, have been
using the word ï¿½Cobolï¿½ as a noun in speech for several decades.  We
don't spell out the letters C-O-B-O-L, as we would U-C-L-A, we say
ï¿½Cobolï¿½.  I assert that the word ï¿½Cobolï¿½ is now an acceptable word in
the English language.  In my dictionary, I have defined the word
ï¿½Cobolï¿½, with a usage of noun, and meaning of  ï¿½The computer language
COBOLï¿½.  I use the word ï¿½Cobolï¿½ in my business writing regularly.

My second issue is punctuation.  You will notice in my previous writing
that all commas and periods following a quotation mark are outside the
quotation.  This is contrary to traditional English punctuation.  I have
changed the rules of punctuation to require commas and periods to be
placed outside the quote, unless they are part of the quote.  I assert
the traditional English rules regarding quotation marks are not precise
enough for this century.  I believe these rules were created to improve
the appearance of writing, not for clarity.  If I was to end a sentence
with a quote, the placement of the period changes the meaning of the
quotation.  For example:

  At the command prompt, key in ï¿½CD ..ï¿½.    (My rules of punctuation)
  At the command prompt, key in ï¿½CD ...ï¿½    (Traditional rules of
punctuation)

Which method is preferable?  Some might argue that quotations should
only be used to quote a persons speech, or a chapter or section name, as
is defined in traditional grammar.  I would assert that a computers
speech is in the same category as a persons speech, and needs to be more
precise than traditional punctuation allows.

I urge ï¿½Cobolï¿½ to be accepted as a proper spelling of the noun.  I also
urge English texts to change the traditional rules of commas and periods
following quotations.

Fred Young
Perfect Circle Solutions, Inc.
Phn: (310) 395-5127
Eml: fred@perfectcircle.com
Web: www.perfectcircle.com
```

#### ↳ COBOL capitalization (was: Cobol as a noun

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ltoo7$5e4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3982536B.615784C7@perfectcircle.com>`

```
I disagree with your spelling of the programming language as "Cobol" 110%
<G>.

The reason doesn't have to do with it being an acronym (which it is) because
that would mean it SHOULD be spelled "CoBOL." (notice punctuation <G>).

HOWEVER, the reason that I absolutely, positively REQUIRE that anything that
I deal with uses "COBOL" is the "Acknowledgement" that appears in the
current, past, and draft Standards (as well in most/all COBOL language
references).  This states in part,

"Any organization using a short passage from this document, such as in a book
review, is requested to mention "COBOL" in acknowledgment of the source."

Therefore, unless you suddenly want to try and take the language definition
out of the "public domain," you, I, and everyone else MUST keep spelling it
as originally dictated. (Alternatively, you may request copyright permission
from the "owners" of the language to refer to it as "Cobol."  However, as
most of the original owners have gone out of business or been acquired by
someone else, you will have some difficulty doing this.  If your "text"
references any extensions from current products, then you would need that
vendor's prior permission.  Obviously, this is a LONG and slippery slope.)

P.S.  The capitalization has nothing to do with it being a noun or not.
"NASA" for example, is always in capitals - regardless of how it is used, as
is IBM and several other nouns. Whether such acronyms are spelled out or
pronounced as a single word, also is irrelevant as shown by NASA vs IBM.

As far as punctuation marks before vs after quotation marks, I use both
variations (depending upon my mood).  The Standard has a "style guide" which
requires that it be OUTSIDE the quotation mark.  However, most "academic"
papers follow the Chicago Style Guide (in the US) and other similar style
guides that still require the "inside" rule.
```
