When I used to do documentation, my pet peeve was trying to decide if it was
"an SQL statement" or "a SQL statement." It depended upon whether you
pronounced "SQL" as "Es-Q-el" or "Seequel".  There was no consensus that I
could ever find on this one.  (Even IBM's DB2 documentation uses both.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL capitalization (was: Cobol as a noun

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3985EA4D.6704BE5F@brazee.net>`
- **References:** `<3982536B.615784C7@perfectcircle.com> <8ltoo7$5e4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3985812A.3E8A8CA0@brazee.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-vZFMOkpdgg7h@tcpserver> <8m4l1q$a2b$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> When I used to do documentation, my pet peeve was trying to decide if it was
> "an SQL statement" or "a SQL statement." It depended upon whether you
> pronounced "SQL" as "Es-Q-el" or "Seequel".  There was no consensus that I
> could ever find on this one.  (Even IBM's DB2 documentation uses both.)

I figure if I see "an", I will pronounce it Es-Q-el when I read it.  I
am completely phonetic in my use of "an", which means that, since I
don't drop my aiches, I don't say "an historical moment".  But with
formal documentation, I attempt to avoid this issue altogether.

By the same token, I try to avoid the word "data" as a subject of a
sentence.  If I correctly say "data are", some people will get their
nose out of joint, if I incorrectly say "data is", others will frown.

Did you know that an acceptable plural of "schema" is "schemata"?

(of course while joking, one can be down to his last spagettus or
raviolus on his plate!)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL capitalization (was: Cobol as a noun

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m4rsc$6s3$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3982536B.615784C7@perfectcircle.com> <8ltoo7$5e4$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3985812A.3E8A8CA0@brazee.net> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-vZFMOkpdgg7h@tcpserver> <8m4l1q$a2b$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3985EA4D.6704BE5F@brazee.net>`

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:3985EA4D.6704BE5F@brazee.net...
>
> Did you know that an acceptable plural of "schema" is "schemata"?
>

"An" acceptable plural?  "*AN*" acceptable plural?    Anything else would
strike me as acceptable only insofar as "he surveilled the defense position"
is acceptable English!

<<(of course while joking, one can be down to his last spagettus or raviolus
on his plate!)>>

... And while *not* joking, one could be down to one's last spaggheto (dim.
of "spago", cord or string) or  raviolo (dim. of "rava", turnip), I'd
allege.  The "-us"  noun-marking endings, however, would only be appropriate
when joking, I'll agree, because they're Latin, not one of its descendants.

But then I was brought up that after-dinner beverages were "li-KERRs", not
"li-KYOORs" or "li-KOORs" to say nothing of the dreaded "LIKKers".  And
while one might lounge in the patio after dinner, the furniture on which one
was most likely to do so was not a "Shay's lounge" or a "Chay's lounge" but
a "shehz long" -- a long chair.   My parents were very particular about such
matters.

      -Chuck Stevens
```

Fred Young wrote:
> 
> This time is interesting for those of us who deal with highly technical
> writing, as well as normal business communication... <snip>

Fred,

Your may have found the Perfect (circle) Solutions - but I think you are
on a hiding to nothing on this one ...AND........I knew how William K.
would respond, even before I read him <G>. His point about
acknowledgement of "COBOL" is interesting. So far as I'm concerned like
Rhett Butler, "Frankly my dear. I don't give a damn".

From a generation that had to absorb all those funnies RAF, USAAF,
CINCPAC, SEAC, WHO, UNESCO, UN, CINCMEAF etc...., I would kinda feel
funny if they came out Raf, Usaaf, Cincpac, Seac, Who, Unesco, Un and
Cincmeaf. ('Case you're curious, last one is C-in-C Middle East Air
Force - when RAF was in Gibraltar, Malta, N.Africa, Arabia etc.) 

Valiant try, keep on plugging it. Just 40 years ago (correction 43 years
ago yesterday was anniversary), I had to submit the following bullshit
to get married while in the RAF, (legally it was actually a no brainer
to stop me), but that's the military for you :-

---------------------------------------------------------------------
				123456 Some Rank Bumblekins, J.
				RAF Northolt 
				Middx

Officer Commanding		Date....
Royal Air Force Northolt
Middx

Sir,
		Application to get Married

I have the honour to request that I may have your permission to get
married.

		I have the honour to be Sir,

		your obedient Servant,

		Josiah Bumblekins
		Some rank, RAF
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

And the same crap was being used back when Nelson was trying to blow the
French and Spanish out of the water at Trafalgar in 1805 !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

> This time is interesting for those of us who deal with highly technical
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

From Webster's:

COBOL (kobol) n. a computer language employing English words, used in
business applications Also written Cobol

Etymology [co(mmon) b(usiness) o(riented) l(anguage)]
(C)1995 Zane Publishing, Inc.   (C)1994, 1991, 1988 Simon & Schuster,
Inc.

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MFVg5.1042$Z51.198258@nnrp3.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<3982536B.615784C7@perfectcircle.com>`

```
COBOL as a noun?

As in "I have a COBOL."

or as an adjective, as in:

"This is a COBOL program." "Use the COBOL compiler."

Maybe a verb?

"I COBOLed it."


"Fred Young" <fred@perfectcircle.com> wrote in message
news:3982536B.615784C7@perfectcircle.com...
> This time is interesting for those of us who deal with highly
technical
> writing, as well as normal business communication.  I have made up
> words, made nouns into verbs, and changed the traditional rules of
> punctuation, in order to improve the clarity of my writing.  I will
give
> you two examples I would like to see gain acceptance.
>
> "COBOL", as most of us know, is an acronym for Common Business
Oriented
> Language.  "Cobol", in my opinion, is a noun.  I, and others, have
been
> using the word "Cobol" as a noun in speech for several decades.  We
> don't spell out the letters C-O-B-O-L, as we would U-C-L-A, we say
> "Cobol".  I assert that the word "Cobol" is now an acceptable word
in
> the English language.  In my dictionary, I have defined the word
> "Cobol", with a usage of noun, and meaning of  "The computer
language
> COBOL".  I use the word "Cobol" in my business writing regularly.
>
> My second issue is punctuation.  You will notice in my previous
writing
> that all commas and periods following a quotation mark are outside
the
> quotation.  This is contrary to traditional English punctuation.  I
have
> changed the rules of punctuation to require commas and periods to be
> placed outside the quote, unless they are part of the quote.  I
assert
> the traditional English rules regarding quotation marks are not
precise
> enough for this century.  I believe these rules were created to
improve
> the appearance of writing, not for clarity.  If I was to end a
sentence
> with a quote, the placement of the period changes the meaning of the
> quotation.  For example:
>
>   At the command prompt, key in "CD ..".    (My rules of
punctuation)
>   At the command prompt, key in "CD ..."    (Traditional rules of
> punctuation)
>
> Which method is preferable?  Some might argue that quotations should
> only be used to quote a persons speech, or a chapter or section
name, as
> is defined in traditional grammar.  I would assert that a computers
> speech is in the same category as a persons speech, and needs to be
more
> precise than traditional punctuation allows.
>
> I urge "Cobol" to be accepted as a proper spelling of the noun.  I
also
> urge English texts to change the traditional rules of commas and
periods
> following quotations.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-b4818c4185"></a>
## Thread: Standards, Precision, Formal definitions, and "all that jazz" — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Standards, Precision, Formal definitions, and "all that jazz"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ls7rd$cjf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Just as a follow up on a current (and past) threads.

The COBOL (draft and past) Standard is written as a "formal definition."  It
just isn't the same TYPE of definition that any language (computer, natural,
before, or since) has ever used. <G>

Furthermore, (and this has been a recent re-occurring theme), it is QUITE
INTENTIONAL that some of the "ambiguities" that have been there (and grown
over the years) are kept in.

It is the view of the Standards committees (and I think this is correct -
from my experience) that most (not all) production COBOL programmers and
sites want the SAME results over CORRECT results.

What this means to the Standards developers is that "upward compatibility"
(even of ambiguities and errors) OFTEN is of the highest priority. (Some
corrections are made and included in the "potentially impacting" appendix -
but the Standard writers TRY to keep these to a minimum - and only do them
AFTER most compilers are already working the same way.)

Now, I know that there are some individuals (and even shops) that like
getting "better" and/or more portable results.  But even when it comes to
portability, it is frequently in terms of "please make everyone ELSE's
environment work like mine". Furthermore, some people want the "new stuff" to
be more rigorous, even if the old parts have problems.  UNFORTUNATELY, as any
of us who have ever tried to write (even the smallest) proposal against the
'85 Standard know, it is SO full of problems, that if you "touch" one
paragraph, you are liable to fall into multiple existing problems.

There is, BTW, an ANSI/ISO "formal language definition" language which is
approved (and as far as I know used by almost no one).  I think (fear? hope?
expect?) that you are more likely to see ALL operating systems written in
COBOL before you see the COBOL Standard using any of the "known" formal
language definition languages.
```

#### ↳ Re: Standards, Precision, Formal definitions, and "all that jazz"

- **From:** Herwig Huener & Josella Simone Playton <webmaster@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39829BC9.78983BD8@Josella-Simone-Playton.de>`
- **References:** `<8ls7rd$cjf$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
2000-07-29 10:50:50 MESZ

"William M. Klein" wrote:
>
> ...

Hi,

I hope it is understood that I did not criticize the efforts
of the standard comittee as a whole. Nevertheless, thinking
aloud is allowed. And what I see is that defining effort
and implementation efforts of this language seems to grow and
grow and grow. At the same time, Cobol draws it's right of
existence from the gigantic base of productive Code of
non-OO-Cobol.

A programming language is a tool to save work. Like every
other machine, the costs of maintenace against the profits
must be assessed permanently. If it gets clear, that a machine
is just another creative way to burn up money, the machine
must go to the scrapyard.

So, with all the brilliant effort in improving a language,
the "Cui bono?" question remains.

And the first or second of many answers to follow comes
from the implementor who is asked to craft the concepts
in some form of reality.
```

Colonel

#### ↳ Re: slighly off topic

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rJrg5.297$hX1.62599@nnrp1.sbc.net>`
- **References:** `<P3kg5.6847$%W6.243882@news-east.usenetserver.com>`

```

"Colonel" <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:P3kg5.6847$%W6.243882@news-east.usenetserver.com...
> Is there a freely available version of MicroFocus COBOL?
> The reason I ask is I`m using Fujitsu 3 at the moment and my
coursework
> tutors are using MF. It seems that F3 must be doing some sort of
error
> correction when reading records. I am able to read a record field
with
> non-numeric data into a numeric data-name!! My tutors are picking it
up
> making me look more of a novice than need be ;-).

If you mean:

FD  MYFILE.
01  MYREC.
     02  MYNUM     PIC 9(5).
     02  MYEGG     PIC X(5).

And you read a record that looks like:

Manuscript

And you complain about MYNUM containing "Manus,"

sorry, MicroFocus won't help, nor will any other compliant COBOL
compiler. Compilers do not do nor generate error checking/correction
code; programmers do. The BEST a compiler can do is generate code to
abnormally terminate the program.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: slighly off topic

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8ltpk5$pto$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<P3kg5.6847$%W6.243882@news-east.usenetserver.com> <rJrg5.297$hX1.62599@nnrp1.sbc.net>`

```
I assumed that the original question was using "compiler" loosely and they
really mean that the Micro Focus (MEANT) "product" provides numeric
validation "checking."  This is determined (in INT) by the setting of the
+F/-F run-time switch.  With the "correct" setting numeric fields including
"incompatible data" get a run-time message (when referenced) - with the other
setting, they do not.

I have never used it, but I believe that using the Fujitsu

         CHECK(NUMERIC)

directive, MAY provide the same functionality.
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QDzg5.26$qd1.37643@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Colonel <colonel@psxzone.freeserve.co.uk> wrote in message
news:P3kg5.6847$%W6.243882@news-east.usenetserver.com...
>... It seems that F3 must be doing some sort of error
> correction when reading records. I am able to read a record field with
> non-numeric data into a numeric data-name!!

We had a discussion here on this maybe six months ago. IIRC, given:

FD THE-FILE
01  THE-RECORD.
      05 anything     pic 9(9).
..
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

01  WS-THE-RECORD.
      05 ws-anything     PIC 9(09).


It seems that any COBOL handles non-numeric data in anything if you use

 READ THE-FILE

.. but fails if you

READ THE-FILE INTO WS-THE-RECORD

and anything is non-numeric.


That, or check the compiler options - MF may have a switch which allows
instant verification.

Or, check the deja archives for the exact thread, as the memory is
fad..where was I? oh, yeah ...ing.

MCM
```

� Significant updates to �Books about COBOL�

� Changed several erroneous references to �Cobol� to the correct spelling
�COBOL�

� Added information on J & C Migrations conversion tools

� Updated information on the �Tiny COBOL� project to reflect their new goal
of providing an �85 Standard compiler

� Add information on the Semantic Designs and Progeni tools

� Added information on  Compaq COBOL (both for the formerly DEC COBOL and the
formerly TANDEM COBOL)

� Added a reference to http://www.geocities.com/timjosling/cobol.html for
information on the �COBOL to GCC� project and the status of the various �open
source� COBOL projects.

� Added an appendix with �miscellaneous� COBOL-related web pages

� Updated the Acucorp phone numbers

� Updated the information on who distributes ETK


   ***

As usual, any suggestions, corrections, updates, etc are appreciated (via
private email  - or via the NG).

## Thread: OT: Task Manager Software needed — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Task Manager Software needed

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.basic.powerbasic,microsoft.public.basic.dos,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RDzg5.27$qd1.37602@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

Wrong newsgroup, I know, but:

Does anyone know of some good 'task manager' software?

What I am looking for is something with the functionality of MS-DOS batch
files:

- Start and execute a DOS or Windows program, with option to run
synchronously or asynchronously (i.e., choice of "launch and return
immediately" or "launch and wait for the program to end before resuming").
- Execute a step conditionally based on the return or error code of previous
task
- User-written scripting
- Test for files' existence and/or file size

But I want a few more features...

- Show on the screen the current step being executed (user-friendly)
- Optional or automatic logging of session to disk file
- Allow user to terminate the script while running (after current invoked
task completes/ends)
- Ability to store multiple scripts and select which one to run
- (Optional) mechanism to prompt and accept user values which can then be
used as parameters to next step. Examples: "Run this step Y/N?" or "Enter
Filename to process"

I would prefer the software be Windows-based, but will look at MS-DOS.
Should cost no more than $250-$300 US (single user).

Scheduling (i.e, "run daily at 2:00 AM") is not necessary - I am looking for
something to assist a user to run a series of 'on-demand'  tasks in the
correct order.

If such an animal is not available commercially, I will consider writing it
myself, but I feel no great compelling reason to re-invent the wheel.

I am not interested in being the first user of a new product.

Product/company names, web sites, other news groups, search tips all
appreciated.

Thanks for all replies,
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Task Manager Software needed

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** microsoft.public.basic.dos,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.basic.powerbasic
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-PHvIkP6BI6WC@tcpserver>`
- **References:** `<RDzg5.27$qd1.37602@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Find a copy of the IBM REXX interpreter for the operating system
of your choice. There are versions that run on OS/2, Windows, MS-DOS
PC-DOS VSE, MVS and others.

The main IBM page for REXX is

URL http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/obj-rexx/ibmrexx.html

There is also another REXX interpreter called Regina that
is available from other sources.

A search at http://www.alltheweb.com on rexx and regina
should provide a number of leads

Lorne

Seeing as Michael raised the topic of Task Manager Software, prompted me
to thinking about batch-files. I threw my old MSDOS manuals away ages
ago. For the life of me can't remember the syntax, but I wanted to do
following :-

	mybatchfile parameter

Cannot remember how to check for existence of that parameter and precise
coding within the batch file if parameter supplied/not supplied. This is
batchfile I had, coding is not necessarily correct :-

rem --------------------- CSTONDT.BAT --------------------------------
echo off                                                              
rem                 cstondt.bat    2000 Feb 17                        
rem                                                                   
rem     Conversion of CS facility files to NDT format                 
rem                                                                   
rem This bat file requires CS Client/Facility as parameter :-         
rem             1% = cccccff                                          
rem                                                                   
rem  Copies files across from CS to NDT system, then runs CSNDT.COB   
rem  which reformats CS Items, Probes and Techs into 6 digit (ccyymm) 
rem  dates for NDT system.                                            
rem                                                                   
rem  **** The following would work if I could remember the syntax to  
rem       ensure that 'other' files don't get deleted because of missi
rem       parameter - Can you remember ????                           
rem                                                                   
rem     Make sure a parameter has been entered :-                     
rem                                                                   
REM echo on                                                           
REM if %1==                                                           
REM pause                                                             
REM goto :ERROR                                                       
rem                                                                   
rem     Delete files just to make sure we don't have any junk :-      
rem                                                                   
rem     del \ndt\data\%1*.*                                           
rem                                                                   
rem  copy data files, renaming from 'dat' to 'dta'                    
rem                                                                   
copy \usr\cs\data\%1C.dat   \ndt\data\%1C.dta                         
copy \usr\cs\data\%1R.dat   \ndt\data\%1R.dta                         
copy \usr\cs\data\%1S.dat   \ndt\data\%1S.dta                         
copy \usr\cs\data\%1V.dat   \ndt\data\%1V.dta                         
rem                                                                   
rem copy index files (both use standard RM suffix 'inx')              
rem                                                                   
copy \usr\cs\data\%1C.inx   \ndt\data\%1C.inx                         
copy \usr\cs\data\%1R.inx   \ndt\data\%1R.inx                         
copy \usr\cs\data\%1S.inx   \ndt\data\%1S.inx                         
copy \usr\cs\data\%1V.inx   \ndt\data\%1V.inx                         
rem                                                                   
rem   Now it runs CSTOND.COB and will prompt you again for Cleint/Faci
rem                                                                   
CD \NDT\PROGRAMS                                                      
RUNCOBOL CSTOND.COB                                                   
REM go to EXIT                                                        
rem                                                                   
REM :ERROR                                                            
REM echo Client/Facility parameter not supplied                       
REM EXIT:                                                             
cd c:\                                                                
rem                                                                   
*---------------------------------------------------------------------
```

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> What I am looking for is something with the functionality of MS-DOS batch
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> immediately" or "launch and wait for the program to end before resuming").

What about the win95 or NT START command  (they are unfortunately
different but)

START /WAIT will wait for completion.

> - Execute a step conditionally based on the return or error code of previous
> task

if ERRORLEVEL

> - User-written scripting

A Batch file

> - Test for files' existence and/or file size

if exists

> But I want a few more features...
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Filename to process"

Ok now I will get a little more realistic:   Look at source.cygnus.com
for the Unix tools.  It includes (along with all the unix commands you
could want) bash. With bash you have a full scripting language.

You can run the script with 'tee' which copies the file and dumps it
to console at the same time.

In KSH (another shell scripting language) you can use 'set -x' to turn
on debugging so that you can dump the commands as they execute.  (Some
unix guru confirm this).

> I would prefer the software be Windows-based, but will look at MS-DOS.
> Should cost no more than $250-$300 US (single user).

The price is right  ZERO.   I have run these for 12 months without any
problems.

> Scheduling (i.e, "run daily at 2:00 AM") is not necessary - I am looking for
> something to assist a user to run a series of 'on-demand'  tasks in the
> correct order.

Look up the 'at' command in NT.  It is there for you to use.  I have
looked this up but not used it in anger yet.   It appears that
Microsoft ripped off enough from SCO unix to make a decent Cron.  I
would assume that WIn2000 would have the same things.


Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/

For fast secure document delivery on the Net
http://www.themailxchange.com.au/
```

```
I am afraid that you won't get too much "sympathy" for not checking for
"incompatible data" from "real world" COBOL programmers.  This is something
that you need to learn about (and understand) EARLY in your programming
training.

I don't know the course that you are taking (although I have heard it
referenced several times in the NG).  However, I MUST believe that somewhere
before this project it talked about this "concept".

Again, I don't know how your text book/instruction is set up, but, if I were
you, I would look for references to "results are unpredictable" or even
"implementor defined" - and you will find things that MAY differ from
compiler to compiler.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Slightly off topic

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lvms5$5ot$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<kdEg5.1185$U7.50713@news-east.usenetserver.com> <8lvb7u$6no$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
There are two issues in your comment: "similar results" and "identical
compiler" results.

Any conforming compiler (and Fujitsu, IBM, MERANT, RM, Acucobol, all are)
provides a method of "flagging" when you use extensions to the Standard - at
compile time.  If you turn this on, you SHOULD be guaranteed that your code
will compile identically with different compilers.  (Well, there are some
"implementor defined" areas - such as Select/Assign statement - but these are
usually minimal.)

As far as "identical" run-time results go, this is less easy to check for and
guarantee.  HOWEVER, if you are going to become a "real world" programmer, it
is probably useful to learn about such differences early on.  Learning to
read a vendor's documentation - and how to adjust your program's behavior to
the  desired results is a useful skill and one that I would encourage you to
learn - while you are still learning how to program in COBOL.
```

## Thread: Error message when executing a MicroFocus program — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Error message when executing a MicroFocus program

- **From:** Jesper Frank Nemholt <jfn@dassic.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3982D0F0.453E23D7@dassic.com>`

```
Hi!

Can any of you give me a clue on solving this one :

Software/OS :

Digital Unix V4.0d
CICS V2.1B
DCE V3.0
Oracle V7.3.4
MicroFocus Cobol V4.1A


Error message :

Execution error: file ï¿½/tmp/test.gntï¿½
Error code: 114, pc= 0, call=6, seg=0
114 Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory (Signal 11)


Is it just a pointer error in the program, or are there other
possibilities ?



l8r/Jspr
```

#### ↳ Re: Error message when executing a MicroFocus program

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8lv938$la7$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3982D0F0.453E23D7@dassic.com>`

```
Jesper,

A 114 error indicates a memory violation.  A classic cause is a
mismatch of parameters on a CALL statement where the data
sizes differ.

I would suggest you check for this first.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

Jesper Frank Nemholt <jfn@dassic.com> wrote in message
news:3982D0F0.453E23D7@dassic.com...
> Hi!
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> l8r/Jspr
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<39831EAB.C69EEC12@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3982D0F0.453E23D7@dassic.com>`

```


Jesper Frank Nemholt wrote:
> 
> Hi!
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I'm using NetExpress OO - but most often times that Error 114 results
from following - non-matching set of SENDING and RECEIVING parameters
when doing an invoke; in your case when doing a CALL ???

Such as :-

	Call Something using Field1, Field2, Field3

	Linkage in 'Something'

	Linkage-section.
	01 lnk-field1		pic ???
	01 lnk-filed2		pic ???
	01 lnk-Field3		pic ???
	01 lnk-Field4		pic ???

OR ; Procedure Division using lnk-field1, lnk-field2, lnk-field3,
			      lnk-field4 ...AND....lnk-field5

All variations on a theme but your sending and receiving are probably
not matching.

Jimmy
```

- **From:** angelo <agrego@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2000-07-29T00:00:00

On Sat, 29 Jul 2000 14:41:20 +0200, Jesper Frank Nemholt
<jfn@dassic.com> wrote:

>Hi!
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>l8r/Jspr
Hi,

I got the same error code with Microfocus 4.0.16
It happens to me:

A - in DOS and Windows mode running  a program with CHECK_FILE_EXISTS
routine accessing a file with modification  date = 2000/xx/xx

B -  in DOS mode while creating a library file with GNT modules having
a modification  date = 2000/xx/xx

Another problem, but without any error message in Windows mode, is
that COBOL refuses to load a program with modification  date =
2000/xx/xx 

CONCLUSION:
 all seems related to CHECK_FILE_EXISTS routine used in Windows and
DOS mode  by COBOL.

All other  DOS and Windows functions work fine.

I change temporary the computer date from 2000 in 1999 for the
compilation and I replaced CHECK_FILE_EXISTS by CBL_READ_FILE with
flags=128 in order to obtain the same information  

Angelo
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Error message when executing a MicroFocus program

- **From:** "Paddy Coleman" <paddy.coleman@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m3dhn$4ur$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3982D0F0.453E23D7@dassic.com> <k386osoi8digbjr2lhmqrrjh3e5q06etqs@4ax.com>`

```
Angelo,

A ha!  This is a Y2K issue.  I believe it was caused by a problem in the
Microsoft C runtime (which was used to build Workbench 4.0).  We
corrected this in one of the later updates.

I would suggest you download 4.0.38 from:

ftp://ftp.merant.com/updates/cobol-workbench-v4.0/security-keyed/windows-nt-
95/

The above directory contains the updates for a Workbench that has a dongle
(security device).  If you are using unprotected Workbench then go to:

ftp://ftp.merant.com/updates/cobol-workbench-v4.0/non-security-keyed/windows
-nt-95/

Hope this helps.

Paddy Coleman
Manager, E-Business Support, EMEA
MERANT International.

angelo <agrego@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:k386osoi8digbjr2lhmqrrjh3e5q06etqs@4ax.com...

## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL 4.2 — 3 this month / 6 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL 4.2

- **From:** "barry_keith" <barry_keith@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uVwqZIk#$GA.401@cpmsnbbsa08>`

```
I am trying to re-install Fujitsu COBOL 4.2 to another drive but keep
getting a message that COBOL97 is already installed in C:\WINDOWS. I go to
that directory but cannot find COBOL97. What is the problem?
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL 4.2

- **From:** "Tim Hillock" <timhillock@home.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bq2h5.235175$7o1.6251076@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<uVwqZIk#$GA.401@cpmsnbbsa08>`

```
When you click the start button, and got to Programs, do you see Fujitsu
Cobol 4.2 listed?

If you do, you will have to remove it.  Click the start button, go to
settings and then control panel.   In the control panel click on Add/Remove
programs.  Go down the list of programs until you come to the Fujitsu
Cobols.  If 4.2 is the same as 4.0, there should be 4 or 5 entries that you
have to remove, one by one.   You have to click each name in order to delete
it.

You should do the above even if you don't find Fujitsu listed under
Programs.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL 4.2

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2000-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3985e389.103393971@207.126.101.100>`
- **References:** `<uVwqZIk#$GA.401@cpmsnbbsa08>`

```
As I recall, 4.2 had a problem uninstalling sometimes (or was that
4.0?)  Anyway, you might have to scan throught registry and make sure
all references are removed.

On Sun, 30 Jul 2000 09:18:26 -0700, "barry_keith"
<barry_keith@email.msn.com> wrote:

>I am trying to re-install Fujitsu COBOL 4.2 to another drive but keep
>getting a message that COBOL97 is already installed in C:\WINDOWS. I go to
>that directory but cannot find COBOL97. What is the problem?
>
>

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/

the printouts.

currently using a PC with Windows 98.

   ( from an out of touch programmer )
              Johnah@ntlworld.com

On Sun, 30 Jul 2000 17:04:51 +0100, j.holdham wrote:

[...]
>I would now like to continue programming but no longer have access to my 5
>inch floppy's with which my compiler and programs are on but still have all
>the printouts.

A couple of ideas may get you started.

1.  A 5�" floppy drive might cost less than a new compiler.  If the
    diskettes appear to be in good condition, then this might be the
    ticket.  Or, you might know someone with a PC having the ability
    to use both diskette sizes, so they could copy the compiler and
    your programs from your 5�" to some 3�" diskettes.

2.  Perhaps your existing compiler would qualify you for a reduced
    cost upgrade to a newer version from the same vendor.
```

## Thread: Display upon — 3 this month / 8 total

### Display upon

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3985F479.7E74FE4D@brazee.net>`

```
The history of the DISPLAY command makes it less useful than it might.  

What would be nice is if you could define a dozen different locations to
make displays.

DISPLAY UPON SYSOUX1, or DISPLAY UPON FILE.NAME or DISPLAY UPON
set-name.
```

#### ↳ Re: Display upon

- **From:** "Charles W. Cribbs II" <CHARLESCRIBBS@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2000-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m55kj$2veq$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3985F479.7E74FE4D@brazee.net>`

```

so why can't you do that?? if you define it as a screen section you can
display a screen at a time with hundreds of pieces of information.

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:3985F479.7E74FE4D@brazee.net...
> The history of the DISPLAY command makes it less useful than it might.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> set-name.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Display upon

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m56he$8bi$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3985F479.7E74FE4D@brazee.net> <8m55kj$2veq$1@newssvr05-en0.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I believe that Howard uses a COBOL compiler without the EXTENSION of a
"screen section".  That is not a part of ANSI/ISO COBOL (but will be in the
next revision).

FYI,
  X/Open also defined a way to use ACCEPT/DISPLAY to get and set "environment
variables".  This is NOT a part of the draft Standard - but is a relatively
common extension today (especially in UNIX COBOLs).
```

The history of the DISPLAY command makes it less useful than it might.  

What would be nice is if you could define a dozen different locations to
make displays.

dos-set-name.

## Thread: anyone using any variant of OOCOBOL? — 2 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### anyone using any variant of OOCOBOL?

- **From:** "Mike Sheehan" <msheehan@pks.ie>
- **Date:** 2000-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8m48db$drt$1@kermit.esat.net>`

```
Hi,
Are there any individuals or companies who have implemented OOCobol?

- What implementation\compiler are you using?
- How did you find the change in paradigm i.e. using OO features?
- How has it affected the way you code in COBOL?
- What are the advantages of using OOCOBOL rather than switching to
something else?

I'm writing a dissertation at present on OOCOBOL and transfering legacy
systems...
If you think you can answer any of these qs, I'll be grateful

Thx,
Mike Sheehan
```

#### ↳ Re: anyone using any variant of OOCOBOL?

- **From:** Steve Burns <stephen.d.burns2@boeing.com>
- **Date:** 2000-07-31T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3985F994.B3390E2F@boeing.com>`
- **References:** `<8m48db$drt$1@kermit.esat.net>`

```
To my knowledge no one at Boeing is using oocobol.  I would be very
surprised if anyone is using it without my knowledge, becaseu Boeing
works to internal company standards, and oocobol is definately NOT on
the standards lists.  Mostly thta is because no one seems to have a need
for it, but there is another issue as well.  I am quite sure that the
internal standards people at Boeing would simply resuse to declare an
internal standard for something where there is no international standard
in existance.  Maybe in December, 2002.....

More likely later, huh?
